冥报记 - Records of Recompense from the Unseen Realm

    Home

    冥报记 (唐 唐临)
    吏部尚书唐临撰

    　 冥报记卷上　
    　　夫含气有生。无不有识。有识而有行。随行善恶而受其报。如农夫之播植。随所植而收之。此盖物之常理。固无所可疑也。上智达其本源。知而无见。下愚闇其踪迹。迷而不返。皆绝言也。中品之人。未能自达。随缘动见。逐见生疑。疑见多端。各怀异执释典论其分别。凡有六十二见。邪倒于是乎生者也。临在中人之后。幸而寤其万一。比见众人不信因果者说见虽多。同谓善恶无报。无报之说。略有三种。一者自然。故无因果。唯当任欲待事而已。二者灭尽。言死而身灭。识无所住。身识都尽。谁受苦乐。以无受故。知无因果。三者无报。 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 冥報記 - Title meaning 'Records of Rebirth' or 'Records of Retribution,' referring to a collection of stories about karma and rebirth.
    • 含氣有生 - Philosophical expression meaning 'all beings that have breath and life.'
    • 有識而有行 - Means 'having consciousness and thus action,' emphasizing the link between awareness and behavior.
    • 隨行善惡而受其報 - Means 'receiving retribution according to one's good or evil actions,' a key concept in Buddhist and Taoist thought.
    • 如農夫之播植 - Simile comparing life to a farmer sowing seeds, suggesting that actions bear fruit in the future.
    • 此蓋物之常理 - Means 'this is the constant principle of all things,' a philosophical statement about universal law.
    • 上智達其本源 - Means 'the highest wisdom understands its fundamental source,' referring to enlightened beings.
    • 下愚闇其踪迹 - Means 'the lowest ignorance is unaware of its traces,' referring to those who fail to recognize the consequences of their actions.
    • 中品之人 - Refers to 'people of middle capacity,' those who are neither enlightened nor ignorant.
    • 釋典論其分別 - Means 'Buddhist scriptures discuss their distinctions,' referring to the classification of views in Buddhist philosophy.
    • 六十二見 - Refers to 'sixty-two views,' a Buddhist classification of erroneous beliefs.
    • 邪倒於是乎生者也 - Means 'heretical views arise from this,' a critique of misguided beliefs.
    • 臨在中人之後 - Means 'I, the author, am among those of middle capacity,' a self-referential statement.
    • 幸而寤其萬一 - Means 'fortunately, I have awakened to a small part of it,' expressing the author's limited enlightenment.
    • 比見眾人不信因果者 - Means 'I have seen many who do not believe in cause and effect,' referring to skeptics.
    • 自然 - Refers to 'naturalism,' the belief that the universe operates without divine intervention.
    • 滅盡 - Refers to 'annihilationism,' the belief that consciousness ceases to exist after death.
    • 無報 - Refers to 'the doctrine of no retribution,' the belief that actions have no consequences.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 冥報記 - A title that reflects the Buddhist and Taoist focus on karma and rebirth, which were influential in Tang Dynasty China.
    • 唐臨 - The author, Tang Lin, was a Tang Dynasty official and scholar known for his works on Buddhism and governance.
    • 吏部尚书 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for civil administration.
    • 因果 - A central concept in Buddhism, referring to the law of cause and effect, which governs rebirth and karma.
    • 六十二見 - A Buddhist classification of erroneous views, often discussed in early Buddhist texts.
    • 邪倒 - Refers to 'heretical' or 'perverted' views, which are rejected in Buddhist philosophy.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 含氣有生，無不有識 - This phrase is elliptical but means 'all beings that have breath and life, without exception, have consciousness.'
    • 隨行善惡而受其報 - The phrase is concise but clearly states the principle of retribution based on actions.
    • 如農夫之播植，隨所植而收之 - The simile is clear but requires careful rendering to maintain its metaphorical force.
    • 此蓋物之常理 - The phrase is a philosophical generalization that must be translated with care to preserve its universal scope.
    • 上智達其本源，知而無見 - This contrast between enlightenment and ignorance is central to the passage but requires clear differentiation in English.
    • 中品之人，未能自達 - The description of 'middle capacity' is key to understanding the author's self-positioning.
    • 釋典論其分別 - The reference to Buddhist scriptures requires contextual explanation to clarify its significance.
    • 無報之說，略有三種 - The classification of three views on the absence of retribution is central to the passage but requires careful translation to preserve its logical structure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冥報記 - The title of the work.
    • 唐臨 - The author's name.
    • 吏部尚书 - The author's official title.
    • 六十二見 - A Buddhist term referring to a classification of erroneous views.

    Records of Rebirth (唐 唐临)
    Compiled by Tang Lin, Minister of Personnel

    Volume 1 of Records of Rebirth
    All beings that have breath and life possess consciousness. With consciousness comes action, and one receives retribution according to the good or evil of their actions. This is like a farmer sowing seeds, for whatever is sown is reaped. This is the constant principle of all things, and there is nothing to doubt. The highest wisdom understands its fundamental source, knowing it without seeing it. The lowest ignorance is unaware of its traces, wandering blindly and never returning. These are matters beyond words. People of middle capacity cannot fully comprehend. They encounter phenomena by chance, and from these encounters, doubts arise. These doubts take many forms, each person holding their own differing views. The Buddhist scriptures discuss their distinctions, totaling sixty-two views, from which heretical beliefs arise. I, the author, am among those of middle capacity, and fortunately, I have awakened to a small part of it. I have seen many who do not believe in cause and effect, and though their views are numerous, they all assert that good and evil actions have no retribution. The doctrine of no retribution has three main forms:

    1. Naturalism, which holds that there is no cause and effect, and one should simply follow their desires and wait for events to unfold.
    2. Annihilationism, which claims that upon death, the body and consciousness cease to exist, leaving no one to experience suffering or joy. Since there is no experiencer, there is no cause and effect.
    3. The doctrine of no retribution, which denies any consequences for actions.
    [1]

    言见今人有修道德。贫贱则早死。或行凶恶。富贵灵长。以是事故。知无因果。临窃谓。儒书。论善恶之报甚多。近者报于当时。中者报于于累年之外。远者。报于子孙之后。当时报者。若楚子吞蛭。痼疾皆愈。宋公不祷。妖星多退。黍齿凶逆。旋踵伏诛。赵高惑乱。俄而灭族之类。是也。累年报者。如魏颗嫁妾。终以济师。孙叔埋蛇。竟享多福。汉幽鸩如意。苍茍成灾。齐杀彭生。立豕而崇之类是也。子孙报者。若弗父恭于三命。广宣尼之道。邓训岁活千人。遗和熹之庆。陈平阴计。自知无后。栾黡黍侈。盈被其殃之类。是也。若乃虞舜以孝行登位。周文以仁贤受命。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 言见今人有修道德 - Classical expression meaning 'observing that some people today cultivate morality.'
    • 贫贱则早死 - Concise phrase meaning 'if they are poor and lowly, they die early.'
    • 或行凶恶 - Means 'or they commit evil deeds.'
    • 富貴靈長 - Means 'if they are wealthy and noble, they live long.'
    • 以是事故 - Means 'because of this incident.'
    • 知無因果 - Means 'know that there is no cause and effect.'
    • 臨竊謂 - Means 'I secretly think.'
    • 儒書 - Refers to Confucian texts or teachings.
    • 論善惡之報甚多 - Means 'discuss the rewards and punishments for good and evil deeds, which are many.'
    • 近者報於當時 - Means 'those who are near in time receive their reward immediately.'
    • 中者報於累年之外 - Means 'those who are in the middle receive their reward after many years.'
    • 遠者，報於子孙之后 - Means 'those who are far away receive their reward in the descendants.'
    • 當時報者 - Means 'those who receive their reward immediately.'
    • 楚子吞蛭 - Refers to a story where a ruler swallowed a leech and was cured of a disease.
    • 痼疾皆愈 - Means 'all chronic diseases were cured.'
    • 宋公不祷 - Refers to a story where a ruler did not pray and an omen star retreated.
    • 妖星多退 - Means 'the omen stars retreated.'
    • 黍齿凶逆 - Refers to a story where a person with a bad tooth was punished immediately.
    • 旋踵伏诛 - Means 'punished immediately upon turning around.'
    • 赵高惑乱 - Refers to a story where Zhao Gao caused chaos.
    • 俄而灭族 - Means 'and soon his family was destroyed.'
    • 累年报者 - Means 'those who receive their reward after many years.'
    • 魏颗嫁妾 - Refers to a story where Wei Ke married his concubine and was rewarded by saving his army.
    • 终以济师 - Means 'and finally saved his army.'
    • 孙叔埋蛇 - Refers to a story where Sun Shu buried a snake and was rewarded with many blessings.
    • 竟享多福 - Means 'and eventually enjoyed many blessings.'
    • 汉幽鸩如意 - Refers to a story where Emperor Yuan of Han poisoned Prince Li and caused disaster.
    • 苍茍成灾 - Means 'and caused disaster.'
    • 齐杀彭生 - Refers to a story where Qi killed Peng Sheng and was punished by having a pig appear.
    • 立豕而崇 - Means 'and was punished by having a pig appear.'
    • 子孙报者 - Means 'those who receive their reward in the descendants.'
    • 弗父恭于三命 - Refers to a story where Fu Fu was respectful to three commands.
    • 广宣尼之道 - Means 'and spread Confucius's teachings.'
    • 邓训岁活千人 - Refers to a story where Deng Xun saved a thousand people each year.
    • 遗和熹之庆 - Means 'and left a legacy of peace and prosperity.'
    • 陈平阴计 - Refers to a story where Chen Ping had secret plans.
    • 自知无后 - Means 'and knew he would have no descendants.'
    • 栾黡黍侈 - Refers to a story where Luan Yan was extravagant.
    • 盈被其殃 - Means 'and was punished with disaster.'
    • 若乃虞舜以孝行登位 - Means 'as for Yu Shun, who ascended to the throne through filial piety.'
    • 周文以仁贤受命 - Means 'King Wen of Zhou received his mandate through benevolence and wisdom.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 修道德 - Refers to the cultivation of moral virtue.
    • 贫贱 - Refers to poverty and low social status.
    • 富貴 - Refers to wealth and high social status.
    • 儒书 - Refers to Confucian texts and teachings.
    • 善恶之报 - Refers to the rewards and punishments for good and evil deeds.
    • 楚子吞蛭 - A historical anecdote where a ruler swallowed a leech and was cured of a disease.
    • 宋公不祷 - A historical anecdote where a ruler did not pray and an omen star retreated.
    • 黍齿凶逆 - A historical anecdote where a person with a bad tooth was punished immediately.
    • 赵高惑乱 - A historical anecdote where Zhao Gao caused chaos.
    • 魏颗嫁妾 - A historical anecdote where Wei Ke married his concubine and was rewarded by saving his army.
    • 孙叔埋蛇 - A historical anecdote where Sun Shu buried a snake and was rewarded with many blessings.
    • 汉幽鸩如意 - A historical anecdote where Emperor Yuan of Han poisoned Prince Li and caused disaster.
    • 齐杀彭生 - A historical anecdote where Qi killed Peng Sheng and was punished by having a pig appear.
    • 弗父恭于三命 - A historical anecdote where Fu Fu was respectful to three commands.
    • 邓训岁活千人 - A historical anecdote where Deng Xun saved a thousand people each year.
    • 陈平阴计 - A historical anecdote where Chen Ping had secret plans.
    • 栾黡黍侈 - A historical anecdote where Luan Yan was extravagant.
    • 虞舜以孝行登位 - A historical anecdote where Yu Shun ascended to the throne through filial piety.
    • 周文以仁贤受命 - A historical anecdote where King Wen of Zhou received his mandate through benevolence and wisdom.

    Proper Nouns

    • 楚子 - A historical figure, likely referring to a ruler of the state of Chu.
    • 宋公 - A historical figure, likely referring to a ruler of the state of Song.
    • 魏颗 - A historical figure, likely referring to a nobleman from the state of Wei.
    • 孙叔 - A historical figure, likely referring to a nobleman from the state of Sun.
    • 汉幽 - A historical figure, likely referring to Emperor Yuan of Han.
    • 齐杀彭生 - A historical figure, likely referring to a ruler of the state of Qi.
    • 虞舜 - A historical figure, likely referring to Yu Shun, a legendary ruler.
    • 周文 - A historical figure, likely referring to King Wen of Zhou.

    Observing that some people today cultivate morality, if they are poor and lowly, they die early; or they commit evil deeds, if they are wealthy and noble, they live long. Because of this incident, one knows that there is no cause and effect. I secretly think that Confucian texts discuss the rewards and punishments for good and evil deeds, which are many. Those who are near in time receive their reward immediately; those who are in the middle receive their reward after many years; those who are far away receive their reward in the descendants. Those who receive their reward immediately, such as the ruler of Chu who swallowed a leech and all chronic diseases were cured, the ruler of Song who did not pray and the omen stars retreated, the person with a bad tooth who was punished immediately upon turning around, Zhao Gao who caused chaos and soon his family was destroyed, and so on. Those who receive their reward after many years, such as Wei Ke who married his concubine and finally saved his army, Sun Shu who buried a snake and eventually enjoyed many blessings, Emperor Yuan of Han who poisoned Prince Li and caused disaster, Qi who killed Peng Sheng and was punished by having a pig appear, and so on. Those who receive their reward in the descendants, such as Fu Fu who was respectful to three commands and spread Confucius's teachings, Deng Xun who saved a thousand people each year and left a legacy of peace and prosperity, Chen Ping who had secret plans and knew he would have no descendants, Luan Yan who was extravagant and was punished with disaster, and so on. As for Yu Shun, who ascended to the throne through filial piety, and King Wen of Zhou, who received his mandate through benevolence and wisdom. [2]

    桀纣以残忍亡国。幽厉以淫纵祸终。三代功德。下祚长久。秦皇骄暴。及子而灭。若斯之比。触类寔繁。虽复大小有殊。亦皆善恶之验。但事法王道。理关天命。常谈之际。非所宜言。今之所录。盖直取其微细验。冀以发起同类。贻告子孙。徵于人鬼之间。若斯而已也。释氏说教。无非因果。因即是作果。即是报。无一法而非因。无一因而不报。然其说报亦有三种。一者现报。于此身中。作善恶业。即于此身而受报者。皆名现报。二者生报。谓此身作业。不即受之。随业善恶。生于诸道。皆名生报。三者后报。谓过去身作善恶业。能得果报。应多身受。 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 桀纣以残忍亡国 - Historical figures Jie and Zhou, the last kings of the Xia and Shang dynasties, respectively, known for their cruel and tyrannical rule, leading to the downfall of their countries.
    • 幽厉以淫纵祸终 - Historical figures You and Li, the last kings of the Zhou dynasty, known for their debauchery and indulgence, leading to their ultimate downfall.
    • 三代功德 - Refers to the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, emphasizing their moral and cultural contributions.
    • 秦皇骄暴 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, known for his harsh and authoritarian rule.
    • 若斯之比 - Such cases as these, introducing examples to illustrate a theme.
    • 触类寔繁 - Such examples are numerous, indicating that there are many similar cases.
    • 虽复大小有殊 - Even though the scale may differ, suggesting that the examples vary in magnitude but share a common theme.
    • 亦皆善恶之验 - They are all verifications of good and evil, emphasizing that these examples demonstrate the consequences of actions.
    • 但事法王道 - But when it comes to the principles of kingship, introducing a shift in focus to the broader concept of governance.
    • 理关天命 - It is related to the will of heaven, suggesting that the outcomes are influenced by divine will.
    • 常谈之际 - In ordinary discourse, indicating that this topic is commonly discussed.
    • 非所宜言 - Not appropriate to say, suggesting that the topic is too complex or sensitive for casual conversation.
    • 今之所录 - What is recorded now, referring to the current discussion.
    • 盖直取其微细验 - It merely takes these subtle verifications, indicating that the focus is on minor details.
    • 冀以发起同类 - In order to inspire those of the same kind, suggesting that the examples are meant to encourage similar behavior.
    • 贻告子孙 - To warn and instruct descendants, emphasizing the importance of passing down lessons.
    • 徵于人鬼之间 - As evidence in the world of humans and spirits, suggesting that the examples are relevant in both human and spiritual contexts.
    • 释氏说教 - The teachings of the Buddha, referring to Buddhist doctrine.
    • 无非因果 - Nothing but cause and effect, emphasizing the Buddhist principle of karma.
    • 因即是作果 - Cause is the action, suggesting that actions create consequences.
    • 即是报 - Is the retribution, indicating that the consequences are the results of actions.
    • 无一法而非因 - Nothing is not a cause, suggesting that everything has a cause.
    • 无一因而不报 - Nothing has a cause that does not have a retribution, emphasizing that every cause has a corresponding effect.
    • 现报 - Immediate retribution, a concept in Buddhist and other religious traditions.
    • 生报 - Rebirth retribution, a concept in Buddhist and other religious traditions.
    • 后报 - Future retribution, a concept in Buddhist and other religious traditions.
    • 过去身作善恶业 - Good and evil deeds done in past lives, referring to actions in previous incarnations.
    • 应多身受 - Should be experienced in multiple lives, suggesting that the consequences may span multiple lifetimes.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 桀纣 - Jie and Zhou, the last kings of the Xia and Shang dynasties, respectively, known for their cruel and tyrannical rule.
    • 幽厉 - You and Li, the last kings of the Zhou dynasty, known for their debauchery and indulgence.
    • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, considered the first three dynasties of Chinese history.
    • 秦皇 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China, known for his harsh and authoritarian rule.
    • 释氏 - Refers to the Buddha and Buddhist teachings.
    • 因果 - The Buddhist principle of cause and effect, or karma.
    • 现报 - Immediate retribution, a concept in Buddhist and other religious traditions.
    • 生报 - Rebirth retribution, a concept in Buddhist and other religious traditions.
    • 后报 - Future retribution, a concept in Buddhist and other religious traditions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 桀纣 - Jie and Zhou, the last kings of the Xia and Shang dynasties.
    • 幽厉 - You and Li, the last kings of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
    • 秦皇 - Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of China.
    • 释氏 - The Buddha and Buddhist teachings.
    • 现报 - Immediate retribution.
    • 生报 - Rebirth retribution.
    • 后报 - Future retribution.

    Jie and Zhou perished their countries through cruelty. You and Li perished through debauchery and indulgence. The virtues and achievements of the Three Dynasties ensured their long-lasting rule. The tyrannical Qin emperor, though powerful, saw his dynasty fall within a generation. Such cases as these are numerous. Even though the scale may differ, they are all verifications of good and evil. But when it comes to the principles of kingship, it is related to the will of heaven. In ordinary discourse, it is not appropriate to say. What is recorded now merely takes these subtle verifications, in order to inspire those of the same kind and warn and instruct descendants, as evidence in the world of humans and spirits. The teachings of the Buddha are nothing but cause and effect. Cause is the action, and is the retribution. Nothing is not a cause, and nothing has a cause that does not have a retribution. However, the teachings of retribution also have three kinds: immediate retribution, where good and evil deeds done in this life are experienced in this life; rebirth retribution, where deeds done in this life are experienced in a future life, depending on the nature of the deeds; and future retribution, where good and evil deeds done in past lives are experienced in multiple lives. [3]

    是以现在作业。未便受报。或次后后生受。或五生十生。方始受之。是皆名后报。于此三报。摄一切法。无所不尽。是今诸见复然大寤。然今俗士尚有惑之。多习因而忘果。疑耳而信目。是以闻说后报。则若存若亡。见有效验。则惊嗟信服。昔晋高士谢敷。宋尚书令傅高。太子中书舍人报演。齐司徒事中郎陆果。或一时令望。或当代名家。并录观世音应验记。及齐竟陵王萧子良作宣验记。王琰作冥祥记。皆所以徵明善恶。劝戒将来。实使闻者深心感寤。临既慕其风旨。亦思以劝人。辄录所闻。集为此记。仍具陈所受。及闻见由缘。言不饰文。事专扬确。庶后人见者。能留意焉。 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 是以 - Classical connective meaning 'therefore' or 'thus,' used to introduce a conclusion.
    • 未便受报 - Means 'cannot immediately receive retribution,' indicating a delay in the consequences of actions.
    • 或次后后生受 - Means 'or the next generation may suffer,' suggesting a delayed effect on future generations.
    • 或五生十生 - Means 'or after five or ten lifetimes,' indicating a long-term consequence.
    • 是皆名后报 - Means 'all these are called delayed retribution,' summarizing the concept of delayed consequences.
    • 于此三报 - Means 'in these three kinds of retribution,' referring to immediate, intermediate, and delayed consequences.
    • 摄一切法 - Means 'encompass all phenomena,' indicating the comprehensive nature of the concept.
    • 无所不尽 - Means 'nothing is left out,' emphasizing completeness.
    • 然今俗士尚有惑之 - Means 'but today's common people are still confused by it,' indicating a lack of understanding.
    • 多习因而忘果 - Means 'they focus on causes and forget the results,' suggesting a lack of attention to outcomes.
    • 疑耳而信目 - Means 'they doubt what they hear but believe what they see,' indicating a preference for sensory evidence over hearsay.
    • 闻说后报 - Means 'when they hear about delayed retribution,' referring to the concept of delayed consequences.
    • 则若存若亡 - Means 'they either accept it or reject it,' indicating an inconsistent attitude.
    • 见有效验 - Means 'when they see evidence,' referring to observable proof.
    • 则惊嗟信服 - Means 'they are amazed, sigh, and believe,' indicating a strong reaction to evidence.
    • 言不饰文 - Means 'the words are not embellished,' indicating a straightforward style.
    • 事专扬确 - Means 'the facts are presented clearly and accurately,' indicating a focus on truth.
    • 庶后人见者 - Means 'may future readers,' indicating a hope for the audience.
    • 能留意焉 - Means 'pay attention to it,' indicating a desire for engagement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋高士谢敷 - Refers to Xie Fu, a renowned scholar from the Jin dynasty.
    • 宋尚书令傅高 - Refers to Fu Gao, a high-ranking official from the Song dynasty.
    • 太子中书舍人报演 - Refers to Bao Yan, a literary figure from the Southern Dynasties.
    • 齐司徒事中郎陆果 - Refers to Lu Guo, a scholar from the Southern Qi dynasty.
    • 观世音应验记 - Refers to a collection of miracles attributed to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 齐竟陵王萧子良作宣验记 - Refers to a work by King Jingling Xiao Ziliang of the Qi dynasty documenting miracles.
    • 王琰作冥祥记 - Refers to a work by Wang Yan documenting supernatural events.
    • 善恶 - Refers to the moral concepts of good and evil.
    • 劝戒 - Refers to the moral concepts of encouragement and warning.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋高士谢敷 - Xie Fu, a renowned scholar from the Jin dynasty.
    • 宋尚书令傅高 - Fu Gao, a high-ranking official from the Song dynasty.
    • 太子中书舍人报演 - Bao Yan, a literary figure from the Southern Dynasties.
    • 齐司徒事中郎陆果 - Lu Guo, a scholar from the Southern Qi dynasty.
    • 观世音应验记 - A collection of miracles attributed to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 齐竟陵王萧子良作宣验记 - A work by King Jingling Xiao Ziliang of the Qi dynasty documenting miracles.
    • 王琰作冥祥记 - A work by Wang Yan documenting supernatural events.

    Therefore, in the present actions, one cannot immediately receive retribution. Sometimes the next generation may suffer, or after five or ten lifetimes, the consequences may finally manifest. All these are called delayed retribution. In these three kinds of retribution—immediate, intermediate, and delayed—they encompass all phenomena, leaving nothing out. Thus, the various views are now greatly enlightened. However, today's common people are still confused by this concept. They focus on causes and forget the results, doubting what they hear but believing what they see. Therefore, when they hear about delayed retribution, they either accept it or reject it. But when they see evidence, they are amazed, sigh, and believe. In the past, there were notable figures such as the Jin dynasty scholar Xie Fu, the Song dynasty chancellor Fu Gao, the Southern Dynasties literary figure Bao Yan, and the Southern Qi scholar Lu Guo. Some were respected figures of their time, while others were famous scholars of the era. They all compiled collections of miracles, such as the Guanshiyin Yingyan Ji (Records of the Miracles of Avalokiteshvara), the Xuan Yan Ji (Records of Miracles) by King Jingling Xiao Ziliang of the Qi dynasty, and the Mingxiang Ji (Records of Supernatural Events) by Wang Yan. All these works are meant to demonstrate good and evil, to encourage and warn future generations, truly making those who hear deeply reflect. I have been inspired by their spirit and intent and also wish to encourage others. I have recorded what I have heard and compiled this record. I have also described what I have experienced and the reasons for what I have heard and seen. The words are not embellished, and the facts are presented clearly and accurately. May future readers pay attention to it. [4]

    随京师大德沙门。释信行。本相州法藏寺僧。初其母无子。久以为忧。有沙门过之。劝念观世音菩萨。母曰夜祈念。顷之有娠。生信行。幼而聪慧。博学经论。识达过人。以为佛所说经。务于济度。或随根性。指人示道。或逐时宜。因事判法。今去圣久远。根时久异。若以下人修行上法。法不当根。容能错倒。乃钞集经论。参验人法所当学者。为三十六卷。名曰人集录。开皇初。左仆射齐公。闻其盛名。奏文帝。徵诣京师。住公所造真寂寺。信行又据经律。录出三阶法四卷。其大旨。劝人普敬。认恶本。观佛性。当病授药。顿教一乘。自天下。勇猛精进之士。皆宗之。信行尝头陀乞食。六时礼拜。劳力定心。空形实智而已。每坐禅说法。常见青衣童子四人。持花立侍。尝与徒众。在堂中坐禅。众人忽闻奇香。光照堂内。相共怪异。咨问信行。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 随京师大德沙门 - Refers to a highly respected monk in the capital, '大德' indicating a high status or virtue.
    • 释信行 - The name of the monk, '释' is a common prefix for Buddhist monks.
    • 本相州法藏寺僧 - Indicates that the monk originally belonged to the Fa Zang Temple in Xiangzhou (present-day Hebei Province).
    • 初其母无子 - Means 'at first, his mother had no children.'
    • 久以为忧 - Means 'she was worried for a long time.'
    • 有沙门过之 - Means 'a monk passed by.'
    • 劝念观世音菩萨 - Means 'he advised her to recite the name of Guanyin Bodhisattva.'
    • 母曰夜祈念 - Means 'the mother said she would pray at night.'
    • 顷之有娠 - Means 'soon after, she became pregnant.'
    • 生信行 - Means 'she gave birth to Xinxing.'
    • 幼而聪慧 - Means 'he was intelligent from a young age.'
    • 博学经论 - Means 'he extensively studied Buddhist scriptures and treatises.'
    • 识达过人 - Means 'his understanding exceeded that of others.'
    • 以为佛所说经，务于济度 - Means 'he believed that the Buddha's teachings aimed at saving and guiding beings.'
    • 或随根性，指人示道 - Means 'sometimes according to the nature of the listener, he pointed out the path.'
    • 或逐时宜，因事判法 - Means 'sometimes according to the times and circumstances, he decided on the appropriate dharma.'
    • 今去圣久远，根时久异 - Means 'nowadays, it has been a long time since the Buddha's time, and the natures and times have changed greatly.'
    • 若以下人修行上法，法不当根，容能错倒 - Means 'if lower beings practice higher methods, the method may not suit their nature, and they might go astray.'
    • 乃钞集经论，参验人法所当学者 - Means 'he therefore compiled and collated scriptures and treatises to verify what should be learned by people.'
    • 为三十六卷，名人集录 - Means 'he compiled thirty-six volumes, titled 'Ren Ji Lu' (Human Collection Record).'
    • 开皇初 - Refers to the beginning of the Kaihuang era, the reign title of Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 左仆射齐公 - Refers to the Left Chancellor and Duke of Qi, a high-ranking official.
    • 闻其盛名，奏文帝，徵诣京师 - Means 'he heard of his great reputation, reported to Emperor Wen, and summoned him to the capital.'
    • 住公所造真寂寺 - Means 'he stayed at the True Silence Temple built by the official.'
    • 信行又据经律，录出三阶法四卷 - Means 'Xinxing then compiled four volumes of the Three Stages Method based on the Vinaya and Sutras.'
    • 其大旨，劝人普敬，认恶本，观佛性，当病授药，顿教一乘 - Means 'the main idea is to encourage universal reverence, recognize the root of evil, contemplate Buddha-nature, prescribe medicine according to the illness, and teach the immediate one vehicle.'
    • 自天下，勇猛精进之士，皆宗之 - Means 'from all over the country, those who are brave and diligent in practice all followed him.'
    • 信行尝头陀乞食，六时礼拜 - Means 'Xinxing often practiced asceticism, begging for food, and worshipped six times a day.'
    • 劳力定心，空形实智而已 - Means 'he worked hard to stabilize his mind, focusing on emptiness and true wisdom.'
    • 每坐禅说法，常见青衣童子四人，持花立侍 - Means 'whenever he sat in meditation and preached, he often saw four green-clad boys holding flowers standing by.'
    • 尝与徒众，在堂中坐禅，众人忽闻奇香，光照堂内，相共怪异，咨问信行 - Means 'once, while sitting in meditation with his disciples in the hall, everyone suddenly smelled a strange fragrance, and light filled the hall. They were all amazed and asked Xinxing about it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 观世音菩萨 - Guanyin Bodhisattva, a bodhisattva of compassion in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 三阶法 - The Three Stages Method, a Buddhist doctrine that emphasizes the importance of recognizing one's own nature and practicing accordingly.
    • 真寂寺 - The True Silence Temple, a temple built by a high-ranking official during the Sui dynasty.
    • 开皇 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Sui (581-604 AD).
    • 左仆射 - The Left Chancellor, a high-ranking official in the Sui dynasty.
    • 齐公 - Duke of Qi, a title given to a high-ranking official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 释信行 - The name of the monk.
    • 法藏寺 - The Fa Zang Temple, located in Xiangzhou.
    • 观世音菩萨 - Guanyin Bodhisattva.
    • 真寂寺 - The True Silence Temple.
    • 开皇 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 左仆射 - The Left Chancellor.
    • 齐公 - Duke of Qi.
    • 三阶法 - The Three Stages Method.

    In the capital, there was a highly respected monk named Xinxing. Originally, he was a monk at the Fa Zang Temple in Xiangzhou. At first, his mother had no children, and she was worried for a long time. A monk passed by and advised her to recite the name of Guanyin Bodhisattva. The mother said she would pray at night. Soon after, she became pregnant and gave birth to Xinxing. From a young age, he was intelligent and extensively studied Buddhist scriptures and treatises. His understanding exceeded that of others. He believed that the Buddha's teachings aimed at saving and guiding beings, sometimes according to the nature of the listener, he pointed out the path, and sometimes according to the times and circumstances, he decided on the appropriate dharma. Now, it has been a long time since the Buddha's time, and the natures and times have changed greatly. If lower beings practice higher methods, the method may not suit their nature, and they might go astray. Therefore, he compiled and collated scriptures and treatises to verify what should be learned by people, compiling thirty-six volumes titled 'Ren Ji Lu' (Human Collection Record).

    At the beginning of the Kaihuang era, the Left Chancellor and Duke of Qi heard of his great reputation, reported to Emperor Wen, and summoned him to the capital. He stayed at the True Silence Temple built by the official. Xinxing then compiled four volumes of the Three Stages Method based on the Vinaya and Sutras. The main idea is to encourage universal reverence, recognize the root of evil, contemplate Buddha-nature, prescribe medicine according to the illness, and teach the immediate one vehicle. From all over the country, those who are brave and diligent in practice all followed him. Xinxing often practiced asceticism, begging for food, and worshipped six times a day. He worked hard to stabilize his mind, focusing on emptiness and true wisdom. Whenever he sat in meditation and preached, he often saw four green-clad boys holding flowers standing by. Once, while sitting in meditation with his disciples in the hall, everyone suddenly smelled a strange fragrance, and light filled the hall. They were all amazed and asked Xinxing about it. [5]

    信行令问弟子僧邕惠如。邕曰。向见化佛从空中来。至禅师前。摩顶授记。如云亦摩邕顶授记。余状与邕说同。后邕典其徒众。隐太白山。一旦请众僧曰。当与师等还京。众敬邕皆从之。即下山。夜宿武功。未明便发。谓众曰。师等努力。今暝必须入城。曰没至漕上。闻鼓音叹曰。城门闭矣。遂宿于逆旅。至昏时。悲泣曰。无所及矣。众问其故不答。明早入城。至真寂寺。而信行昨夜昏时气绝。寺僧怪问邕来。答曰。在山遥见多人持香花幡盖。从西来入开远门。向真寂寺。邕疑禅师欲去。故来也。昨夜昏时。见禅师导从西去。顾与邕别。故知不及也。初京城诸师。有疑信行法者。至是相与议。据付法藏经。若人通身。过去闻正法故。于是。共观信行头骨。两耳正通。乃皆惭悔信服。初信行。徒众居京城五寺。后虽侵广。今犹号五禅师（老僧。及临舅说云尔）。 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 信行令问弟子僧邕惠如 - Formal command or request for a monk named Yong to inquire about something, possibly a spiritual matter.
    • 向见化佛从空中来 - Vision of a transformed or manifested Buddha, indicating a supernatural or spiritual event.
    • 摩顶授记 - Buddhist ritual where a teacher touches the top of a disciple's head and gives them a prophecy or blessing.
    • 隐太白山 - Action of retreating or hiding to Mount Taibai, a mountain in Shaanxi, China, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 典其徒众 - Management or oversight of a group of disciples or followers.
    • 没至漕上 - Arrival at a specific location, possibly a canal or waterway, with unclear significance.
    • 无所及矣 - Statement indicating it is too late or no longer possible, with unclear circumstances leading to this conclusion.
    • 共观信行头骨 - Examination of the skull of Xin Xing, with unclear context and purpose.
    • 两耳正通 - Description of the ears being perfectly aligned or connected, which could be a reference to a specific physical characteristic or a metaphor for spiritual clarity.
    • 五禅师 - Term used to refer to the five teachers or masters, a common term in Buddhist contexts.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 信行 - Historical figure, possibly a Buddhist monk or teacher, known for his teachings and influence.
    • 太白山 - Mountain in Shaanxi, China, known for its historical and cultural significance, often associated with religious retreats.
    • 真寂寺 - Buddhist temple, likely a significant site in the story.
    • 开远门 - Gate in the city, possibly a reference to a specific location in the city of Xi'an.
    • 付法藏经 - Buddhist text that records the lineage of transmission of the Dharma.

    Proper Nouns

    • 信行 - Historical figure, possibly a Buddhist monk or teacher.
    • 僧邕 - Monk named Yong, who plays a significant role in the story.
    • 太白山 - Mountain in Shaanxi, China.
    • 真寂寺 - Buddhist temple.
    • 开远门 - Gate in the city.
    • 五禅师 - Term used to refer to the five teachers or masters.

    Xin Xing commanded his disciple Monk Yong to inquire about something. Yong said, "I saw a transformed Buddha coming down from the sky, arriving before the Master, touching his head, and giving him a prophecy. It was as if the Buddha also touched my head and gave me a prophecy. The rest of the scene was the same as what I described." Later, Yong managed his group of disciples and retreated to Mount Taibai. One day, he called together the monks and said, "I will return to the capital with you." The monks, respecting Yong, all agreed. They then descended the mountain and stayed overnight in Wugong. Before dawn, they set out, and Yong told the group, "Strive, everyone. Tonight we must enter the city." They reached a place called Cao, and Yong said, "The city gates are closed." They then stayed at an inn. In the evening, Yong wept and said, "It is too late." When asked why, he did not respond. The next morning, they entered the city and arrived at Zhenji Temple. At dusk the previous night, Xin Xing had passed away. The monks at the temple were surprised to see Yong and asked why he had come. Yong replied, "I saw many people carrying incense, flowers, banners, and canopies coming from the west and entering Kaiyuan Gate, heading towards Zhenji Temple. I suspected the Master wanted to leave, so I came. Last night, I saw the Master leading his retinue westward and looking back to say goodbye to me. That's why I know it's too late." Initially, the monks in the capital who doubted Xin Xing's teachings discussed it and referred to the Fayuan Zangjing (Transmission of the Dharma Treasury), which states that if a person has heard the true Dharma in a past life, their ears will be perfectly aligned. They then examined Xin Xing's skull and found that his ears were indeed perfectly aligned. They all felt ashamed and regretful, finally believing and respecting him. Initially, Xin Xing's followers lived in five temples in the capital. Later, the number of temples increased, but they were still known as the Five Masters. (As told by Old Monk and Uncle Lin.) [6]

    京城真寂寺沙门慧如。少精勤苦行。师事信行。信行亡后。奉遵其法。隋大业中。因坐禅修定。遂七曰不动。众皆叹异之。以为入三昧也。既而慧如开目。涕泣交流。僧众怪问之。答曰。火烧脚痛。待视疮毕乃说。众逾怪问。慧如曰。被阎罗王请。行道七曰满。王问。须见先亡知识不。如答曰。欲见二人。王即遣唤一人。唯见龟来。舐慧如足。目中泪出而去。更一人者。云罪重不可唤。令就见之。使者引慧如至狱门。门闭甚固。使者唤守者。有人应声。使者语慧如。师急避道。莫当门立。如始避而门开。大火从门流出。如锻铁者一星。迸着如脚。如以被拂之。举目视门。门已闭讫。竟不得相见。王施绢三十匹。固辞不许云。已遣送后房。众僧争往。后房视之则绢在床矣。其脚烧疮。大如钱。百余曰乃愈。武德初卒。真寂寺。即今化度寺是也（此寺。临外祖齐公所立。常所游观。每闻舅氏说。云尔）。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Proper Nouns

    • 京城真寂寺沙门慧如 - 慧如 is the name of a monk, 沙门 refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 信行 - Name of a teacher.
    • 隋大业 - Era name of Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 阎罗王 - King Yama, a figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 武德 - Era name of Emperor Gaozu of Tang.
    • 真寂寺 - Name of the temple, means 'True Silence Temple'.
    • 化度寺 - Name of the temple, means 'Transformation and Measure Temple'.
    • 齐公 - Duke Qi, a historical figure.

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少精勤苦行 - 少 means 'young', 精勤 means 'diligent', 苦行 means 'ascetic practices'.
    • 师事信行 - 师事 means 'to follow as a teacher', 信行 is the name of a teacher.
    • 奉遵其法 - 奉遵 means 'to follow and adhere to', 其法 means 'his teachings'.
    • 因坐禅修定 - 坐禅 means 'meditation', 修定 means 'to cultivate concentration'.
    • 遂七曰不动 - 遂 means 'thus', 七曰 means 'seven days', 不动 means 'motionless'.
    • 众皆叹异之 - 众 means 'the crowd', 皆 means 'all', 叹异 means 'to marvel'.
    • 以为入三昧也 - 以为 means 'to think', 入三昧 means 'to enter a meditative state'.
    • 既而慧如开目 - 既而 means 'afterwards', 开目 means 'opened his eyes'.
    • 涕泣交流 - 涕泣 means 'tears', 交流 means 'flowing'.
    • 僧众怪问之 - 僧众 means 'monks', 怪问 means 'to ask in surprise'.
    • 答曰 - 答 means 'answer', 曰 means 'said'.
    • 火烧脚痛 - 火烧 means 'burned by fire', 脚痛 means 'feet pain'.
    • 待视疮毕乃说 - 待 means 'wait', 视疮 means 'to examine the wound', 毕 means 'finished', 乃说 means 'then speak'.
    • 被阎罗王请 - 被 means 'was', 阎罗王 means 'King Yama', 请 means 'invited'.
    • 行道七曰满 - 行道 means 'to walk the path', 七曰 means 'seven days', 满 means 'completed'.
    • 王问 - 王 means 'the king', 问 means 'asked'.
    • 须见先亡知识不 - 须 means 'need', 见 means 'to see', 先亡 means 'deceased', 知识 means 'friends', 不 means 'whether'.
    • 欲见二人 - 欲 means 'want', 见 means 'to see', 二人 means 'two people'.
    • 王即遣唤一人 - 王 means 'the king', 即 means 'immediately', 遣 means 'sent', 唤 means 'called', 一人 means 'one person'.
    • 唯见龟来 - 唯 means 'only', 见 means 'saw', 龟 means 'turtle', 来 means 'came'.
    • 舐慧如足 - 舐 means 'licked', 慧如 is the name of the monk, 足 means 'feet'.
    • 目中泪出而去 - 目中 means 'in the eyes', 泪出 means 'tears flowed', 而 means 'and', 去 means 'left'.
    • 更一人者 - 更 means 'another', 一人 means 'one person'.
    • 云罪重不可唤 - 云 means 'said', 罪重 means 'heavy sin', 不可唤 means 'could not be called'.
    • 令就见之 - 令 means 'ordered', 就 means 'to go', 见 means 'to see', 之 means 'it'.
    • 使者引慧如至狱门 - 使者 means 'messenger', 引 means 'led', 慧如 is the name of the monk, 至 means 'to', 狱门 means 'prison gate'.
    • 门闭甚固 - 门 means 'gate', 闭 means 'closed', 甚 means 'very', 固 means 'solid'.
    • 使者唤守者 - 使者 means 'messenger', 唤 means 'called', 守者 means 'guard'.
    • 有人应声 - 有 means 'there was', 人 means 'person', 应声 means 'answered'.
    • 使者语慧如 - 使者 means 'messenger', 语 means 'told', 慧如 is the name of the monk.
    • 师急避道 - 师 means 'teacher', 急 means 'quickly', 避 means 'avoid', 道 means 'path'.
    • 莫当门立 - 莫 means 'do not', 当 means 'block', 门 means 'gate', 立 means 'stand'.
    • 如始避而门开 - 如 means 'he', 始 means 'began', 避 means 'avoid', 而 means 'and', 门开 means 'the gate opened'.
    • 大火从门流出 - 大火 means 'great fire', 从 means 'from', 门 means 'gate', 流出 means 'flowed out'.
    • 如锻铁者一星 - 如 means 'like', 锻铁 means 'forge iron', 者 means 'one', 一星 means 'a star'.
    • 迸着如脚 - 迸 means 'burst', 着 means 'onto', 如 means 'his', 脚 means 'feet'.
    • 如以被拂之 - 如 means 'he', 以 means 'with', 被 means 'blanket', 拂 means 'brushed', 之 means 'it'.
    • 举目视门 - 举目 means 'raised his eyes', 视 means 'looked at', 门 means 'gate'.
    • 门已闭讫 - 门 means 'gate', 已 means 'already', 闭 means 'closed', 讫 means 'completed'.
    • 竟不得相见 - 竟 means 'finally', 不得 means 'did not', 相见 means 'meet'.
    • 王施绢三十匹 - 王 means 'the king', 施 means 'gave', 绢 means 'silk', 三十匹 means 'thirty bolts'.
    • 固辞不许云 - 固 means 'firmly', 辞 means 'refused', 不许 means 'would not allow', 云 means 'said'.
    • 已遣送后房 - 已 means 'already', 遣 means 'sent', 送 means 'to', 后房 means 'back room'.
    • 众僧争往 - 众僧 means 'monks', 争 means 'rushed', 往 means 'to go'.
    • 后房视之则绢在床矣 - 后房 means 'back room', 视 means 'looked', 之 means 'it', 则 means 'then', 绢 means 'silk', 在床 means 'on the bed', 矣 means 'already'.
    • 其脚烧疮 - 其 means 'his', 脚 means 'feet', 烧疮 means 'burned wounds'.
    • 大如钱 - 大 means 'large', 如 means 'like', 钱 means 'coin'.
    • 百余曰乃愈 - 百 means 'hundred', 余 means 'more', 曰 means 'days', 乃 means 'then', 愈 means 'healed'.
    • 武德初卒 - 武德 is the era name of Emperor Gaozu of Tang, 初 means 'beginning', 卒 means 'died'.
    • 真寂寺 - Name of the temple, means 'True Silence Temple'.
    • 即今化度寺是也 - 即 means 'is', 今 means 'now', 化度寺 means 'Transformation and Measure Temple', 是也 means 'it is'.
    • 此寺 - 此 means 'this', 寺 means 'temple'.
    • 临外祖齐公所立 - 临 means 'by', 外祖 means 'grandfather', 齐公 means 'Duke Qi', 所立 means 'established'.
    • 常所游观 - 常 means 'often', 所 means 'the', 游观 means 'visited'.

    The Monk Hui Ru of the True Silence Temple in the Capital City

    In his youth, Hui Ru was diligent in his ascetic practices and studied under the teacher Xin Xing. After Xin Xing's death, Hui Ru continued to follow his teachings. During the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui, he engaged in meditation and cultivation of concentration. For seven days, he remained motionless, which amazed the crowd who thought he had entered a meditative state. Later, Hui Ru opened his eyes and cried, with tears flowing freely. The monks were surprised and asked him why. He replied that his feet were burning and in pain, and he would speak after examining the wound. The monks asked further, and Hui Ru said that he had been invited by King Yama. He had walked the path for seven days, and King Yama asked if he wished to see any deceased friends. Hui Ru answered that he wanted to see two people. King Yama immediately sent for one, but only a turtle appeared, licking Hui Ru's feet before leaving with tears in its eyes. Another person was said to have committed heavy sins and could not be called. Hui Ru was ordered to go and see them. A messenger led Hui Ru to the prison gate, which was very solid. The messenger called the guard, and someone answered. The messenger told Hui Ru to quickly avoid the path and not block the gate. As soon as Hui Ru moved aside, the gate opened, and a great fire flowed out, like a star forging iron, striking Hui Ru's feet. He brushed the blanket over it, raised his eyes to look at the gate, which was already closed. He could not see anyone. King Yama gave him thirty bolts of silk, but Hui Ru refused, saying it had already been sent to the back room. The monks rushed there, and when they looked, the silk was on the bed. Hui Ru's burned feet were as large as coins, and it took more than a hundred days to heal. He died at the beginning of the reign of Emperor Gaozu of Tang. The True Silence Temple is now known as the Transformation and Measure Temple. (This temple was established by Duke Qi, my maternal grandfather, and I often visited it. I often heard my uncle speak of this.) [7]

    绛州大德沙门。释僧彻。少而精练。于孤山西阿。造立堂宇。多树林等。颇得山居形胜。僧彻尝出行山间。土穴中。见一癞病人。疮痍臭秽。从彻乞食。彻愍之。呼出与归。于精舍旁。为造土穴。给衣食。教令诵法华经。此人不识文字。性又顽鄙。彻句句授之。殊费功力。然终不懈倦。此人诵经向半。便梦。有人教之。自后稍聪寤。至得五六卷。渐觉疮愈。比诵一部毕。须眉平复。生肥体如常。而能为疗疾。临尝患肿。僧彻遣此人。禁咒有验。 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 绛州大德沙门 - A great monk from the state of Jiang; '大德' is a title for a highly respected monk, and '沙门' refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 少而精练 - Young and skilled; describes the monk's early proficiency.
    • 造立堂宇 - Built a hall; refers to the construction of a hall or temple.
    • 颇得山居形胜 - Gained a good location for mountain living; describes the favorable location of the temple.
    • 土穴中 - In a cave; refers to a natural or artificial cave.
    • 疮痍臭秽 - Ulcers and foul odor; describes the condition of the leper.
    • 愍之 - Pity him; expresses the monk's compassion.
    • 句句授之 - Taught him word by word; describes the method of teaching the leper to recite the *Saddharma Pundarika Sutra*.
    • 殊费功力 - Cost a lot of effort; emphasizes the difficulty of teaching the leper.
    • 终不懈倦 - Never tired; describes the monk's persistence.
    • 聪寤 - Came to understand; indicates the leper's gradual improvement.
    • 平复 - Healed; describes the leper's recovery.
    • 生肥体如常 - Regained normal weight and appearance; indicates the leper's full recovery.
    • 禁咒 - Recited incantations; refers to the practice of reciting mantras or spells for healing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 绛州 - The state of Jiang, a historical region in northern China.
    • 大德沙门 - A highly respected Buddhist monk.
    • 法华经 - The *Saddharma Pundarika Sutra* (Lotus Sutra), a central text in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 土穴 - A natural or artificial cave, often used as a shelter or dwelling.
    • 疮痍臭秽 - Describes the condition of leprosy, a disease that was stigmatized and feared in ancient times.
    • 禁咒 - A practice of reciting mantras or spells for healing, common in Buddhist and other religious traditions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 绛州 - The state of Jiang.
    • 释僧彻 - The name of the monk.
    • 法华经 - The *Saddharma Pundarika Sutra* (Lotus Sutra).
    • 孤山 - A mountain, likely a specific mountain in the region.

    A Great Monk from the State of Jiang. The monk named Seng Che was young and skilled. He built a hall on the western slope of Lonely Mountain, surrounded by many trees. The location was quite suitable for mountain living. Once, while Seng Che was walking through the mountains, he saw a leper in a cave. The leper had ulcers and a foul odor and begged Seng Che for food. Moved by compassion, Seng Che called him out and took him back to his temple. He built a cave next to the temple for the leper, provided him with clothing and food, and taught him to recite the Lotus Sutra. This person could not read or write and was also dull-witted. Seng Che taught him word by word, which required a great deal of effort. However, he never tired. After reciting about half of the sutra, the person began to have dreams where someone taught him. From then on, he gradually became more intelligent. By the time he had recited five or six chapters, his wounds began to heal. By the time he finished reciting the entire sutra, his beard and eyebrows had grown back, and he had regained his normal weight and appearance. He was also able to heal others. Once, when Seng Che himself was suffering from swelling, he sent this person to recite incantations, which proved effective. [8]

    自说云然。后房仁裕为秦州刺史。表僧彻所立精舍。为陷泉寺。初此地无水。僧彻常远汲山下。以自供。一朝忽有陷。陷处泉出。故因以名陷寺也。僧专以劝善为务。而自修禅业。远近崇敬如父焉。永徽二年正月。忽属累徒众。自言将死。既而端坐绳床。闭目不动。其天气晴朗。而花如雪。香而不消。方二里许。树叶上皆有白色。如轻粉者三曰。乃复常色。而僧彻已终。至今三岁。独坐如故。亦不臭怀。唯目泪下云（彻弟子实秦等。及州人并说云尔也）。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自说云然 - Literal translation: 'said so himself.' Indicates a self-reported statement or claim.
    • 表僧彻所立精舍 - Literal translation: 'submitted a memorial to establish a monastery.' Refers to the act of officially requesting the establishment of a religious site.
    • 陷泉寺 - Literal translation: 'Sunken Spring Temple.' Refers to the name of the monastery, reflecting the unusual water source.
    • 初此地无水 - Literal translation: 'originally lacked a water source.' Indicates that the area originally lacked a water source.
    • 远汲山下 - Literal translation: 'carried water from far away.' Describes the effort to obtain water.
    • 忽有陷 - Literal translation: 'suddenly there was a sinkhole.' Indicates an unexpected event.
    • 陷处泉出 - Literal translation: 'water emerged from the sinkhole.' Describes the sudden appearance of a water source.
    • 故因以名陷寺也 - Literal translation: 'thus named after the sinkhole.' Explains that the temple was named after the sinkhole, with '陷寺' meaning 'sinkhole temple.'
    • 劝善为务 - Literal translation: 'to promote goodness as a duty.' Indicates the primary focus of the monk's activities.
    • 自修禅业 - Literal translation: 'to practice Zen meditation personally.' Describes the monk's personal spiritual practice.
    • 远近崇敬如父焉 - Literal translation: 'was revered by people near and far like a father.' Indicates the high regard in which he was held.
    • 永徽二年正月 - Literal translation: 'the first month of the second year of the Yonghui era (651 AD).' Refers to the specific time of the monk's death.
    • 忽属累徒众 - Literal translation: 'suddenly gathered his disciples.' Indicates a final gathering before his death.
    • 自言将死 - Literal translation: 'said he would die.' Indicates a premonition of his impending death.
    • 既而端坐绳床 - Literal translation: 'then sat upright on a rope bed.' Describes his posture at the time of his death.
    • 闭目不动 - Literal translation: 'closed his eyes and remained motionless.' Indicates his state of death.
    • 天气晴朗 - Literal translation: 'the weather was clear and bright.' Describes the atmospheric conditions.
    • 花如雪 - Literal translation: 'flowers like snow.' Describes the appearance of the flowers.
    • 香而不消 - Literal translation: 'fragrant but did not dissipate.' Indicates the lasting fragrance.
    • 方二里许 - Literal translation: 'about two li in diameter.' Describes the extent of the phenomenon.
    • 树叶上皆有白色，如轻粉者三曰 - Literal translation: 'leaves had a white color, like light powder for three days.' Describes the temporary change in the leaves' appearance.
    • 乃复常色 - Literal translation: 'then returned to their normal color.' Indicates the return to normalcy.
    • 僧彻已终 - Literal translation: 'the monk had passed away.' Confirms his death.
    • 至今三岁 - Literal translation: 'until now, three years.' Indicates the duration since his death.
    • 独坐如故 - Literal translation: 'sat alone as before.' Describes his unchanged posture.
    • 亦不臭怀 - Literal translation: 'also did not emit a foul odor.' Indicates the absence of decay.
    • 唯目泪下云 - Literal translation: 'only tears flowed from his eyes.' Describes the final emotional expression.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永徽 - Refers to the Yonghui era, a period during the Tang Dynasty (650-655 AD).
    • 秦州 - Refers to the ancient Chinese province of Qin, located in present-day Gansu Province.
    • 僧彻 - The name of the monk, who is the central figure in the text.
    • 精舍 - Refers to a monastery or a place of study and meditation.
    • 陷泉寺 - The name of the temple, which reflects the unusual water source.
    • 禅业 - Refers to Zen meditation and practice.
    • 绳床 - Refers to a simple bed or chair, often used in monastic settings.
    • 轻粉 - Refers to a light, powdery substance, often used to describe a fine, white powder.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This phrase can be read as a direct quote or a self-reported statement.
    • The sentence structure is somewhat elliptical, but it clearly indicates the act of submitting a memorial to establish a monastery.
    • The sentence describes the sudden emergence of water from the sinkhole, which is a key event in the text.
    • This phrase emphasizes the monk's primary focus on promoting goodness.
    • This phrase describes the monk's personal spiritual practice.
    • This phrase indicates the high regard in which the monk was held by people near and far.
    • This phrase provides the specific time of the monk's death.
    • This phrase indicates a final gathering of the monk's disciples.
    • This phrase indicates a premonition of the monk's impending death.
    • This phrase describes the monk's posture at the time of his death.
    • This phrase describes the monk's state of death.
    • This phrase describes the atmospheric conditions at the time.
    • This phrase describes the appearance of the flowers.
    • This phrase describes the lasting fragrance.
    • This phrase describes the extent of the phenomenon.
    • This phrase describes the temporary change in the leaves' appearance.
    • This phrase indicates the return to normalcy.
    • This phrase confirms the monk's death.
    • This phrase indicates the duration since the monk's death.
    • This phrase describes the monk's unchanged posture.
    • This phrase indicates the absence of decay.
    • This phrase describes the final emotional expression.
    • This phrase indicates the source of the account.

    Proper Nouns

    • 永徽 - The Yonghui era.
    • 秦州 - The ancient Chinese province of Qin.
    • 僧彻 - The name of the monk.
    • 陷泉寺 - The name of the temple.
    • 绳床 - A simple bed or chair used in monastic settings.
    • 轻粉 - A light, powdery substance.

    The monk said so himself. Later, Fang Renyu became the governor of Qin Prefecture. He submitted a memorial to establish a monastery founded by Monk Chè, which was named Sunken Spring Temple. Initially, this place had no water. Monk Chè used to carry water from far away to supply his own needs. One day, suddenly there was a sinkhole, and water emerged from it. Hence, the temple was named after the sinkhole. Monk Chè devoted himself to promoting goodness and personally practiced Zen meditation. He was revered by people near and far, like a father. In the first month of the second year of the Yonghui era (651 AD), he suddenly gathered his disciples and said he would die. Then he sat upright on a rope bed, closed his eyes, and remained motionless. The weather was clear and bright, and flowers appeared like snow, fragrant but not dissipating, covering an area of about two li in diameter. The leaves on the trees had a white color, like light powder for three days, then returned to their normal color. Monk Chè had passed away. Until now, three years have passed, and he still sat alone as before, without emitting a foul odor. Only tears flowed from his eyes. (His disciples Shi Qin and others, as well as the people of the state, all said this.) [9]

    河东有练行尼。常诵法华经。访工书者一人。数倍酬直。特为净室。令写此经。一起一浴。燃香熏衣。仍于写经之室。凿壁通外。加一竹筒。令写经人。每欲出息。辄遣含竹筒。吐气壁外。写经一卷。八年乃毕。供养严重。尽其恭敬。龙门僧法端。常集大众。讲法华经。以此尼经本精定。遣人请之。尼固辞不与。法端责让之。尼不得已。乃自送付。法端等。开读。唯见黄纸。了无文字。更开余卷。皆悉如此。法端等惭惧。即送还尼。尼悲泣受。以香水洗函。沐浴顶戴。绕佛行道。于七曰七夜。不暂休息。既而开视。文字如故。贞观二年。法端自向临说（当具说尼名字。临忘之。唯记其事云尔）。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 數倍酬直 - Offered several times the usual payment.
    • 供養嚴重 - Offered the utmost respect and veneration.
    • 一起一浴 - Bathed once.
    • 燃香熏衣 - Burned incense to perfume her clothes.
    • 仍於寫經之室，鑿壁通外 - Had a wall in the writing room broken through to the outside.
    • 加一竹筒 - Added a bamboo tube.
    • 令寫經人，每欲出息，輒遣含竹筒，吐氣壁外 - Ordered the calligrapher to hold the bamboo tube and exhale outside whenever he wanted to breathe out.
    • 寫經一卷，八年乃畢 - Completed one scroll of the scripture in eight years.
    • 七曰七夜 - Seven days and nights.
    • 文字如故 - The text was as before.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 河東 - Refers to Hedong, a region in Shanxi Province.
    • 法華經 - The Lotus Sutra, a central Mahayana Buddhist scripture.
    • 龍門 - Longmen, a place in Henan Province, known for its Buddhist grottoes.
    • 貞觀 - The era name of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627-649 CE).

    Proper Nouns

    • 練行尼 - The name of the virtuous nun.
    • 工書者 - A skilled calligrapher.
    • 龍門僧法端 - The monk Fahuan from Longmen.
    • 貞觀二年 - The second year of the Zhenguan era.

    There was a virtuous nun from Hedong who often recited the Lotus Sutra. She sought out a skilled calligrapher and offered several times the usual payment. She specially prepared a clean room and asked him to write this scripture. She bathed once, burned incense to perfume her clothes, and also had a wall in the writing room broken through to the outside, adding a bamboo tube. She ordered the calligrapher to hold the bamboo tube and exhale outside whenever he wanted to breathe out. She completed one scroll of the scripture in eight years, offering the utmost respect and veneration.

    The monk Fahuan from Longmen often gathered a large audience to lecture on the Lotus Sutra. Because this nun's copy was meticulously accurate, he sent someone to request it. The nun firmly refused to give it, and Fahuan criticized and reproached her. The nun, unable to refuse, then personally delivered it. When Fahuan opened and read it, he saw only yellow paper with no writing. When he opened other scrolls, they were all the same. Fahuan and his followers felt ashamed and fearful, and immediately returned it to the nun.

    The nun wept and accepted it, washed the box with fragrant water, bathed, and placed it on her head. She walked around the Buddha, reciting sutras, for seven days and nights without stopping. Afterward, when she opened it, the text was as before.

    In the second year of the Zhenguan era, Fahuan himself came to tell the story. [10]

    蒲州仁寿寺僧。释道县。少聪慧。好学。为州里所崇敬。讲涅槃八十余遍。号为精熟。贞观二年。崔义直任虞卿县令。人请县讲经。初发题。悲泣。谓众人曰。去圣遥远。微言隐绝。庸愚所传。不足师范。但以信心归向。自当识悟。今之讲说。止于师子。时曰既没。愿各在心。既而讲至师子。一旦无疾而卒。道俗惊恸。义直身自徒跣。送之南山之阴。时十一月。土地冰冻。下尸于地。地即生花。如莲而小。头及手足。各有一花。义直奇之。令人夜守。守者疲睡。有人盗折其花。明旦视之。周身并有花出。总五百余茎。经七曰乃萎干（义直及道俗皆说云尔）。 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少聪慧，好学 - Describes someone who is young, intelligent, and fond of learning.
    • 为州里所崇敬 - Indicates that the monk was highly respected by the local community.
    • 讲涅槃八十余遍 - Refers to the monk having delivered the *Nirvana Sutra* more than eighty times.
    • 号为精熟 - Means that he was renowned for his mastery of the sutra.
    • 去圣遥远，微言隐绝 - Expresses the idea that the teachings of the Buddha are distant in time and the subtle meanings are difficult to grasp.
    • 庸愚所传，不足师范 - Suggests that what is commonly taught by ordinary people is not worthy of being a model or example.
    • 但以信心归向，自当识悟 - Implies that one should rely on faith and devotion to understand the teachings.
    • 今之讲说，止于师子 - Indicates that the current discourse will end at the point of the lion's roar.
    • 时曰既没，愿各在心 - Means that after the time has passed, everyone should reflect on it in their hearts.
    • 下尸于地，地即生花 - Describes a miraculous event where flowers sprang up from the ground when the body was laid down.
    • 如莲而小 - Compares the flowers to small lotuses.
    • 头及手足，各有一花 - Specifies that there was a flower on each of the head and limbs.
    • 令人夜守 - Means to have someone guard it at night.
    • 守者疲睡，有人盗折其花 - Describes how the guard fell asleep and someone stole the flowers.
    • 明旦视之，周身并有花出 - Means that in the morning, it was seen that flowers had grown all over the body.
    • 总五百余茎 - Indicates that there were more than five hundred stems of flowers.
    • 经七日乃萎干 - Means that the flowers withered and dried after seven days.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蒲州仁寿寺僧 - Refers to a monk from Ren Shou Temple in Pu Prefecture.
    • 释道县 - The name of the monk, likely a transliteration of a Chinese name.
    • 贞观二年 - Refers to the second year of the Zhen Guan era, which was 628 AD during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 虞卿县令 - Refers to the magistrate of Yu Qing County.
    • 涅槃 - The *Nirvana Sutra*, an important Buddhist scripture.
    • 师子 - Refers to the 'lion's roar,' a term used in Buddhism to denote the profound and powerful teaching of the Buddha.
    • 南山之阴 - Refers to the southern mountain range, indicating the location where the monk was buried.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蒲州 - The name of the prefecture.
    • 仁寿寺 - The name of the temple.
    • 释道县 - The name of the monk.
    • 贞观二年 - The year of the event.
    • 虞卿县令 - The title of the magistrate.
    • 涅槃 - The *Nirvana Sutra*.
    • 师子 - The lion's roar.
    • 南山之阴 - The southern mountain range.

    A monk from Ren Shou Temple in Pu Prefecture, named Shi Daoxian, was young, intelligent, and fond of learning. He was highly respected by the local community and had delivered the Nirvana Sutra more than eighty times, earning a reputation for his mastery. In the second year of the Zhen Guan era, Cui Yizhi served as the magistrate of Yu Qing County. The people invited him to give a lecture on the sutra. At the beginning of the lecture, the monk wept and told the audience, "The teachings of the Buddha are distant in time, and the subtle meanings are difficult to grasp. What is commonly taught by ordinary people is not worthy of being a model. One should rely on faith and devotion to understand the teachings. Today's discourse will end at the point of the lion's roar. After the time has passed, everyone should reflect on it in their hearts." As he reached the part about the lion's roar, he suddenly passed away without any illness. The monks and laypeople were shocked and mournful. Cui Yizhi himself walked barefoot to accompany the monk to his burial place on the southern slope of a mountain. It was November, and the ground was frozen. When the body was laid down, flowers sprang up from the ground, resembling small lotuses. There was a flower on each of the head and limbs. Cui Yizhi found this miraculous and had someone guard it at night. The guard fell asleep, and someone stole the flowers. In the morning, it was seen that flowers had grown all over the body, totaling more than five hundred stems. After seven days, the flowers withered and dried. (Both Cui Yizhi and the monks and laypeople all said so.) [11]

    河东沙门。释道英。少修禅行。以练心为本。不慎威仪。然而经律奥义。莫不一闻县解。远近僧尼。争就请决。英辄报谓曰。汝尚未疑。宜且思疑。凝成然后来问。问者还而思疑多。固思自解而去。有思而不寤。重来问者英为说其机要。皆喜寤而还。尝与众人。乘船黄河。中流船没。众人皆死。道俗望见英没。临河恸哭。是时冬末。河冰始泮。两岸犹坚。英乃水中出行至岸。穿冰而去。岸人敬喜。争欲解衣衣之。英曰。体中尚热。勿覆衣也。徐出而归。了无寒色。视其身体。如火炙处。其识者以为入定故也。或时为人牧牛驾车。食蒜啖饭。或着俗衣。发长数寸。尝至仁寿寺。道悬敬安处之。曰晚求食。悬谓曰。上德虽无食相。岂不为息讥嫌。英笑答曰。悬公心方驰惊。不暂休一息。而空饥饿。何自苦也。道悬叹服。贞观中卒（法端。及道俗皆说云尔）。 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少修禅行 - Means 'in his youth, he practiced Chan meditation,' indicating early dedication to spiritual practice.
    • 以练心为本 - Means 'taking the refinement of the mind as the foundation,' emphasizing the core of his practice.
    • 不慎威仪 - Means 'without regard for external decorum,' suggesting a disregard for formal rituals or appearance.
    • 经律奥义 - Refers to the deep meanings of Buddhist scriptures and precepts.
    • 一闻县解 - Means 'understood instantly upon hearing,' indicating exceptional intellectual ability.
    • 争就请决 - Means 'rival monks and nuns sought his judgment,' showing his reputation for wisdom.
    • 宜且思疑 - Means 'it is better to ponder your doubts,' advising the seeker to reflect before asking.
    • 凝成然后来问 - Means 'formulate your questions clearly before coming to ask,' emphasizing preparation.
    • 固思自解而去 - Means 'they thought it through and resolved it themselves,' indicating the effectiveness of reflection.
    • 有思而不寤 - Means 'some still could not understand,' acknowledging the limits of self-reflection.
    • 重来问者英为说其机要 - Means 'those who returned to ask, Dao Ying explained the key points to them,' showing his patient teaching.
    • 皆喜寤而还 - Means 'all returned delighted and enlightened,' indicating the success of his teachings.
    • 尝与众人乘船黄河 - Means 'once, he traveled on the Yellow River with others,' setting the scene for a miraculous event.
    • 中流船没 - Means 'the boat sank in midstream,' describing the dangerous situation.
    • 道俗望见英没 - Means 'monks and laypeople saw him disappear,' indicating the miraculous nature of the event.
    • 临河恸哭 - Means 'they cried by the river,' showing their grief and reverence.
    • 河冰始泮 - Means 'the river ice was just beginning to melt,' indicating the season and the natural setting.
    • 两岸犹坚 - Means 'the riverbanks were still frozen,' emphasizing the difficulty of the situation.
    • 英乃水中出行至岸 - Means 'Dao Ying walked out of the water onto the shore,' describing the miraculous escape.
    • 穿冰而去 - Means 'he walked through the ice,' emphasizing the supernatural aspect.
    • 体中尚热 - Means 'his body was still warm,' indicating the miraculous nature of the event.
    • 徐出而归 - Means 'he slowly emerged and returned,' showing the gradual nature of the miracle.
    • 了无寒色 - Means 'without any signs of cold,' emphasizing the miraculous warmth.
    • 视其身体，如火炙处 - Means 'his body appeared as if it had been roasted by fire,' describing the miraculous condition.
    • 识者以为入定故也 - Means 'those who understood recognized it as the result of deep meditation,' providing a possible explanation.
    • 或时为人牧牛驾车 - Means 'at times, he worked as a cowherd or driver,' showing his humility.
    • 食蒜啖饭 - Means 'he ate garlic and rice,' indicating his simple lifestyle.
    • 或着俗衣 - Means 'sometimes he wore secular clothes,' showing his flexibility.
    • 发长数寸 - Means 'his hair grew several inches long,' indicating a natural, untrimmed appearance.
    • 尝至仁寿寺 - Means 'once, he visited Ren Shou Temple,' setting the scene for another interaction.
    • 道悬敬安处之 - Means 'Dao Xuan respectfully arranged for him to stay,' showing hospitality.
    • 曰晚求食 - Means 'he said he would seek food later,' indicating his simplicity.
    • 上德虽无食相 - Means 'a person of great virtue may not show signs of hunger,' emphasizing spiritual detachment.
    • 岂不为息讥嫌 - Means 'how can one avoid criticism,' showing Dao Ying's concern for others' perceptions.
    • 英笑答曰 - Means 'Dao Ying smiled and replied,' indicating his calm and wise demeanor.
    • 体中尚热，勿覆衣也 - Means 'his body is still warm, do not cover him with clothes,' showing his miraculous condition.
    • 徐出而归 - Means 'he slowly emerged and returned,' indicating the gradual nature of the miracle.
    • 了无寒色 - Means 'without any signs of cold,' emphasizing the miraculous warmth.
    • 视其身体，如火炙处 - Means 'his body appeared as if it had been roasted by fire,' describing the miraculous condition.
    • 识者以为入定故也 - Means 'those who understood recognized it as the result of deep meditation,' providing a possible explanation.
    • 贞观中卒 - Means 'he died during the Zhenguan era,' indicating the time of his passing.
    • 法端及道俗皆说云尔 - Means 'Fazhuan and monks and laypeople all said this,' indicating the widespread knowledge of his story.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 河东 - Refers to Hedong, a region in Shanxi Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 释道英 - A monk whose name indicates his religious identity and lineage.
    • 少修禅行 - Indicates early dedication to Chan (Zen) Buddhism, a significant school in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 以练心为本 - Emphasizes the importance of mental cultivation in Chan practice.
    • 经律奥义 - Refers to the deep meanings of Buddhist scriptures and precepts, which are central to Buddhist study.
    • 一闻县解 - Indicates exceptional intellectual ability, a common theme in biographies of learned individuals.
    • 争就请决 - Shows the respect and admiration Dao Ying received from fellow monks and nuns.
    • 宜且思疑 - Advises the seeker to reflect before asking, a common practice in Buddhist teaching.
    • 凝成然后来问 - Emphasizes the importance of clear and focused questions.
    • 固思自解而去 - Indicates the effectiveness of reflection, a key aspect of Buddhist practice.
    • 有思而不寤 - Acknowledges the limits of self-reflection, showing the need for guidance.
    • 重来问者英为说其机要 - Shows Dao Ying's patient and effective teaching.
    • 皆喜寤而还 - Indicates the success of his teachings, showing the enlightenment of his followers.
    • 尝与众人乘船黄河 - Sets the scene for a miraculous event, emphasizing the dangers of the journey.
    • 中流船没 - Describes the dangerous situation, highlighting the miraculous nature of the event.
    • 道俗望见英没 - Shows the reverence and grief of the onlookers.
    • 临河恸哭 - Indicates the emotional impact of the event.
    • 河冰始泮 - Indicates the season and the natural setting, emphasizing the difficulty of the situation.
    • 两岸犹坚 - Emphasizes the difficulty of the situation, showing the miraculous nature of the event.
    • 英乃水中出行至岸 - Describes the miraculous escape, showing Dao Ying's supernatural abilities.
    • 穿冰而去 - Emphasizes the supernatural aspect, showing the miraculous nature of the event.
    • 体中尚热 - Indicates the miraculous condition, showing the warmth of his body.
    • 徐出而归 - Shows the gradual nature of the miracle, emphasizing the miraculous condition.
    • 了无寒色 - Emphasizes the miraculous warmth, showing the absence of cold.
    • 视其身体，如火炙处 - Describes the miraculous condition, showing the appearance of his body.
    • 识者以为入定故也 - Provides a possible explanation, showing the recognition of deep meditation.
    • 或时为人牧牛驾车 - Shows his humility, indicating his willingness to engage in simple labor.
    • 食蒜啖饭 - Indicates his simple lifestyle, showing his detachment from material comforts.
    • 或着俗衣 - Shows his flexibility, indicating his willingness to blend in with secular society.
    • 发长数寸 - Indicates a natural, untrimmed appearance, showing his simplicity.
    • 尝至仁寿寺 - Sets the scene for another interaction, showing his travels.
    • 道悬敬安处之 - Shows hospitality, indicating the respect he received.
    • 曰晚求食 - Indicates his simplicity, showing his detachment from material comforts.
    • 上德虽无食相 - Emphasizes spiritual detachment, showing his concern for others' perceptions.
    • 岂不为息讥嫌 - Shows Dao Ying's concern for others' perceptions, indicating his wisdom.
    • 英笑答曰 - Indicates his calm and wise demeanor, showing his wisdom.
    • 体中尚热，勿覆衣也 - Shows the miraculous condition, indicating the warmth of his body.
    • 徐出而归 - Shows the gradual nature of the miracle, emphasizing the miraculous condition.
    • 了无寒色 - Emphasizes the miraculous warmth, showing the absence of cold.
    • 视其身体，如火炙处 - Describes the miraculous condition, showing the appearance of his body.
    • 识者以为入定故也 - Provides a possible explanation, showing the recognition of deep meditation.
    • 贞观中卒 - Means 'he died during the Zhenguan era,' indicating the time of his passing.
    • 法端及道俗皆说云尔 - Means 'Fazhuan and monks and laypeople all said this,' indicating the widespread knowledge of his story.

    The Monk Dao Ying of Hedong

    Dao Ying, a monk of Hedong, practiced Chan meditation from a young age, taking the refinement of the mind as the foundation of his practice. He did not concern himself with external decorum, yet he had an exceptional understanding of the deep meanings of Buddhist scriptures and precepts. Monks and nuns from far and near sought his judgment, and he would reply, "You have not yet formed your doubts; it is better to ponder them. Formulate your questions clearly before coming to ask." Those who returned to ask often found that they had resolved their doubts through reflection and left satisfied. Some, however, still could not understand and returned to ask again. Dao Ying would then explain the key points to them, and they all returned delighted and enlightened.

    Once, Dao Ying traveled on the Yellow River with others. The boat sank in midstream, and everyone else died. Monks and laypeople saw him disappear and cried by the river. At that time, it was late winter, and the river ice was just beginning to melt, with the riverbanks still frozen. Dao Ying walked out of the water onto the shore, walked through the ice, and returned. The people on the riverbank were filled with awe and joy, and they all wanted to cover him with their clothes. Dao Ying said, "My body is still warm; do not cover me with clothes." He slowly emerged and returned, without any signs of cold. His body appeared as if it had been roasted by fire. Those who understood recognized it as the result of deep meditation.

    At times, Dao Ying worked as a cowherd or driver, ate garlic and rice, and sometimes wore secular clothes, with his hair growing several inches long. Once, he visited Ren Shou Temple, and Dao Xuan respectfully arranged for him to stay. Dao Ying said he would seek food later. Dao Xuan remarked, "A person of great virtue may not show signs of hunger, but how can one avoid criticism?" Dao Ying smiled and replied, "Dao Xuan, your mind is racing with worry, not even pausing for a moment's rest, yet you are hungry. Why do you trouble yourself?" Dao Xuan was deeply impressed. Dao Ying died during the Zhenguan era, and Fazhuan, as well as monks and laypeople, all said this. [12]

    幽州沙门释智苑。精练有学识。隋大业中。发心造石经藏之。以备法灭。既而于幽州北山。凿岩为石室。即磨四壁。而以写经。又取方石。别更磨写。藏诸室内。每一室满。即以石塞门。用铁锢之。时随赐帝幸涿郡。内史侍郎萧瑀皇后之同母弟也。性笃信佛法。以其事白后。后施绢千匹。余钱物。以助成之。瑀亦施绢五百匹。朝野闻之。争共舍施。故苑得遂其功。苑尝以役匠既多。道俗奔凑。欲于岩前。造木佛堂。并食堂寝屋。而念木瓦难辨。恐分费经物。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 精练有学识 - Skilled and well-learned; describes Zhi Yuan's expertise and knowledge.
    • 发心造石经 - He resolved to carve stone scriptures; '发心' means 'to resolve or vow,' and '造' means 'to create or carve.'
    • 凿岩为石室 - He carved out rock to create a stone chamber; '凿岩' means 'to carve rock,' and '石室' means 'stone chamber.'
    • 磨四壁 - He polished the four walls; '磨' means 'to polish or grind.'
    • 写经 - To write scriptures; '写' means 'to write,' and '经' refers to Buddhist scriptures.
    • 藏诸室内 - He stored them inside the room; '藏' means 'to store,' and '诸' means 'various or many.'
    • 每一室满 - When each room was full; '满' means 'full.'
    • 以石塞门 - He sealed the door with a stone; '塞' means 'to seal or block.'
    • 用铁锢之 - He secured it with iron; '锢' means 'to secure or fasten.'
    • 帝幸涿郡 - The emperor visited Zhuo Commandery; '幸' means 'to visit or grace,' and '涿郡' was a region in present-day Beijing.
    • 内史侍郎萧瑀 - The Secretary of the Board of Works, Xiao Yu; '内史侍郎' refers to a high-ranking official.
    • 皇后之同母弟也 - He was the younger brother of the empress by the same mother; '同母弟' means 'younger brother by the same mother.'
    • 性笃信佛法 - He was deeply devoted to Buddhism; '笃信' means 'deeply believe or trust.'
    • 以其事白后 - He informed the empress about his matter; '白' means 'to inform or report.'
    • 后施绢千匹 - The empress donated a thousand bolts of silk; '绢' means 'silk,' and '匹' means 'bolt.'
    • 余钱物 - The remaining money and goods; '余' means 'remaining,' and '钱物' means 'money and goods.'
    • 瑀亦施绢五百匹 - Xiao Yu also donated five hundred bolts of silk.
    • 朝野闻之 - The court and the people heard about it; '朝野' means 'court and people.'
    • 争共舍施 - They competed to donate; '争共' means 'compete to do something together.'
    • 苑得遂其功 - Zhi Yuan was able to complete his task; '遂' means 'to achieve or complete.'
    • 役匠既多 - There were many laborers; '役匠' means 'laborers or workers.'
    • 道俗奔凑 - Both monks and laypeople gathered; '道俗' means 'monks and laypeople,' and '奔凑' means 'gathered or converged.'
    • 欲于岩前 - He wanted to build in front of the rock; '欲' means 'to want or intend.'
    • 造木佛堂 - To build a wooden Buddha hall; '佛堂' means 'Buddha hall.'
    • 并食堂寝屋 - And dining halls and sleeping quarters; '并' means 'and,' '食堂' means 'dining hall,' and '寝屋' means 'sleeping quarters.'
    • 念木瓦难辨 - He thought that wood and tiles were hard to distinguish; '念' means 'to think,' and '难辨' means 'hard to distinguish.'
    • 恐分费经物 - He was afraid that it would divide and waste the scriptures and materials; '恐' means 'afraid,' '分费' means 'divide and waste,' and '经物' means 'scriptures and materials.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 幽州 - Youzhou, an ancient region in northern China, roughly corresponding to present-day Beijing.
    • 隋大业中 - The Dàyè period (605–618) of the Sui Dynasty, a time of significant construction and expansion.
    • 石经 - Stone scriptures, which were carved into stone for preservation, often associated with Buddhist texts.
    • 萧瑀 - Xiao Yu, a prominent official during the Sui and Tang dynasties, known for his political and cultural influence.
    • 涿郡 - Zhuo Commandery, an important administrative region during the Sui and Tang dynasties.
    • 皇后 - The empress, referring to the wife of the emperor.
    • 朝野 - The court and the people, a common phrase referring to the entire society, including both the ruling class and the general populace.
    • 舍施 - Donations, often in the context of religious or charitable acts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 幽州 - Youzhou
    • 释智苑 - Zhi Yuan
    • 隋大业中 - Dàyè period of the Sui Dynasty
    • 石经 - Stone scriptures
    • 萧瑀 - Xiao Yu
    • 涿郡 - Zhuo Commandery
    • 皇后 - The empress
    • 朝野 - The court and the people
    • 舍施 - Donations

    A monk named Zhi Yuan from Youzhou, skilled and well-learned, resolved during the Dàyè period of the Sui Dynasty to carve stone scriptures and store them, in preparation for the potential extinction of the Dharma. He then carved out rock in the northern mountains of Youzhou to create a stone chamber, polished the four walls, and wrote the scriptures on them. He also took flat stones, polished and wrote on them separately, and stored them inside the chamber. When each room was full, he sealed the door with a stone and secured it with iron. At that time, the emperor visited Zhuo Commandery, and Xiao Yu, the Secretary of the Board of Works and the younger brother of the empress by the same mother, was deeply devoted to Buddhism. He informed the empress about Zhi Yuan's project, and the empress donated a thousand bolts of silk, along with the remaining money and goods, to assist in its completion. Xiao Yu also donated five hundred bolts of silk. The court and the people heard about this and competed to donate. Thus, Zhi Yuan was able to complete his task. Zhi Yuan once thought that with so many laborers, both monks and laypeople had gathered, and he wanted to build a wooden Buddha hall, dining halls, and sleeping quarters in front of the rock. However, he was concerned that it would be difficult to distinguish between wood and tiles, and that it would divide and waste the scriptures and materials. [13]

    故未能起作。一夜暴雨雷电震山。明旦既晴。乃见山下有大松柏数千株。为水所漂流。积道次。山东少林木。松柏尤稀。道俗惊骇。不知来处。推寻踪迹。远自西山。崩岸倒木。漂送来此。于是远近叹服。谓为神助。苑乃使匠择取其木。余皆分与邑里。邑里喜悦。而共助造堂宇。顷之毕成。皆如其志焉。苑所造石满七室。以贞观十三年卒。弟子犹继其功（殿中丞相里玄奘。大理丞釆宣明等。皆为临说云尔。临以十九年从车驾幽州。问乡人。亦同云尔而以军事不得云）。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 故未能起作 - Conclusion based on previous events; it was not possible to start construction.
    • 一夜暴雨雷电震山 - Vivid description of a natural event; a fierce rainstorm with thunder and lightning struck the mountain.
    • 明旦既晴 - Change in weather; the next morning, the sky cleared.
    • 乃见山下有大松柏数千株 - Discovery; then, it was seen that there were thousands of large cypress and pine trees at the foot of the mountain.
    • 为水所漂流 - Passive construction; carried away by the water.
    • 积道次 - Accumulated along the road.
    • 山东少林木 - Scarcity of forested areas in the eastern part of the mountain.
    • 松柏尤稀 - Rarity of cypress and pine trees.
    • 道俗惊骇 - Reaction of monks and laypeople; startled.
    • 不知来处 - Lack of knowledge about the origin of the trees.
    • 推寻踪迹 - Process of tracing the origin of the trees.
    • 远自西山 - Origin of the trees; from far away in the west.
    • 崩岸倒木 - Natural disaster; collapsed banks and fallen trees.
    • 漂送来此 - Movement of the trees; drifted here.
    • 于是远近叹服 - Reaction of people; people far and near were amazed and admired.
    • 谓为神助 - Interpretation of the event as divine assistance.
    • 苑乃使匠择取其木 - Actions of the official; the official then ordered craftsmen to select the trees.
    • 余皆分与邑里 - Distribution of the remaining trees; the rest were distributed to the townspeople.
    • 邑里喜悦 - Reaction of the townspeople; delighted.
    • 而共助造堂宇 - Helping build the halls and buildings; together helped build the halls and buildings.
    • 顷之毕成 - Completion; soon after, it was completed.
    • 皆如其志焉 - According to their wishes; all according to their wishes.
    • 苑所造石满七室 - Construction of seven chambers filled with stones.
    • 以贞观十三年卒 - Death in the thirteenth year of the Zhenguan era.
    • 弟子犹继其功 - Continuation of his work; his disciples continued his work.
    • 殿中丞相里玄奘 - Title and name of a high-ranking official; the Chancellor Li Xuanzang of the Palace Department.
