    • 大理丞采宣明等 - Title and name of a high-ranking official; the Magistrate Cai Xueming and others.
    • 皆为临说云尔 - All told me this.
    • 临以十九年从车驾幽州 - Following the imperial carriage to Youzhou; in the nineteenth year, I followed the imperial carriage to Youzhou.
    • 问乡人 - Asked the locals.
    • 亦同云尔 - They also said the same.
    • 而以军事不得云 - Due to military affairs, they could not say.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 苑 - Name of an official who oversaw the construction project.
    • 贞观十三年 - Thirteenth year of the Zhenguan era (639 AD), a period of great prosperity and cultural achievement during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 殿中丞 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty's Palace Department.
    • 大理丞 - High-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty's judicial department.
    • 相里玄奘 - Historical figure, possibly a reference to the famous monk Xuanzang, known for his travels to India.
    • 采宣明 - Historical figure, possibly a magistrate or official.
    • 临 - Name of the speaker, who recounts the story.
    • 幽州 - Ancient Chinese city, now part of modern-day Beijing.
    • 贞观 - Era name of Emperor Taizong of Tang, known for its cultural and political achievements.

    Proper Nouns

    • 苑 - Name of an official.
    • 贞观 - Era name of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 殿中丞 - Title of a high-ranking official.
    • 大理丞 - Title of a high-ranking official.
    • 相里玄奘 - Historical figure.
    • 采宣明 - Historical figure.
    • 临 - Name of the speaker.
    • 幽州 - Ancient Chinese city.

    Therefore, construction could not begin. One night, a fierce rainstorm with thunder and lightning struck the mountain. The next morning, the sky cleared, and it was seen that there were thousands of large cypress and pine trees at the foot of the mountain, carried away by the water and accumulated along the road. In the area east of the mountain, there are few forested areas, and cypress and pine trees are especially rare. Monks and laypeople were startled, not knowing where they came from. Tracing the traces, it was found that they came from far away in the west, from the western mountain, where collapsed banks and fallen trees had drifted here. People far and near were amazed and admired, saying it was divine assistance. The official then ordered craftsmen to select the trees, and the rest were distributed to the townspeople, who were delighted and together helped build the halls and buildings. Soon after, it was completed, all according to their wishes. The official built seven chambers filled with stones. He died in the thirteenth year of the Zhenguan era. His disciples continued his work (the Chancellor Li Xuanzang of the Palace Department, the Magistrate Cai Xueming and others, all told me this. In the nineteenth year, I followed the imperial carriage to Youzhou, and asked the locals, who also said the same, but due to military affairs, they could not say). [14]

    东魏末。邺下人。共入西山采银钞。出穴未毕。而穴崩。有一人在后。为石塞门不得出。而无伤损。其穴崩处。有小穴不合。微见曰光。此人自念终无理。乃一心念佛。其父闻子已压。无处求尸。家又贫窭。无以追福。乃持粗饭一钵。住诣僧寺。请一人斋。众僧多逐丰厚。莫肯为食。父持饭大哭。有一僧愍之受请。食讫为咒愿。因别去。是曰中其子在穴中。忽于小穴明处。见一沙门。从穴中入来。持一钵饭。以授此人。食讫。便不复饥。唯端坐正念。经十余年。齐文皇帝即位。于西山造凉殿。匠工除此崩石。乃见穴中人尚活。出之与归。父母惊喜。遂阖练行（雍州司马卢承业。为临说云。是著作郎降所傅之）。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 东魏末 - End of the Eastern Wei dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 邺下人 - People from the capital city of the Eastern Wei, Ye (邺), located in present-day Hebei Province.
    • 采银钞 - Mining for silver ore; '银钞' can also refer to silver currency, but in this context, it likely means silver ore.
    • 出穴未毕 - Miners had not yet fully exited the mine.
    • 穴崩 - Mine collapsed.
    • 为石塞门不得出 - Entrance was blocked by rocks, preventing escape.
    • 无伤损 - No physical harm was done to the trapped person.
    • 穴崩处 - Location where the mine collapsed.
    • 小穴不合 - Small hole that did not close completely.
    • 微见曰光 - Slightly visible light.
    • 终无理 - Ultimately, there seemed to be no logical explanation or way out.
    • 一心念佛 - Focused on reciting Buddhist mantras or praying to Buddha.
    • 无处求尸 - There was no way to find the body.
    • 贫窭 - Poverty-stricken or destitute.
    • 无以追福 - Unable to perform rituals to benefit the deceased.
    • 粗饭一钵 - A simple bowl of food.
    • 僧寺 - Monastery.
    • 受请 - Accepted the invitation to eat.
    • 咒愿 - Chanted prayers or mantras.
    • 凉殿 - A cool hall or palace, possibly a summer palace.
    • 匠工 - Skilled workers or laborers.
    • 阖练行 - Likely a reference to a person or family name, possibly a misspelling or variant of '阖闾' (Helu), a historical figure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东魏 - The Eastern Wei dynasty (534–550), a short-lived dynasty that followed the Northern Wei.
    • 邺 - The capital of the Eastern Wei, located in present-day Hebei Province.
    • 西山 - A general term for a mountain range, possibly referring to a specific mountain in the region.
    • 凉殿 - A cool hall or palace, often associated with royal or imperial architecture.
    • 齐文皇帝 - Likely refers to Emperor Wen of the Northern Qi dynasty, who ruled from 550 to 559.
    • 著作郎 - A title for a scholar or literary official in the imperial court.
    • 雍州司马卢承业 - A historical figure, likely a military or administrative official.
    • 临说云 - A phrase indicating that someone is recounting or narrating a story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东魏 - The Eastern Wei dynasty.
    • 邺 - The capital of the Eastern Wei.
    • 西山 - A mountain range or specific mountain.
    • 齐文皇帝 - Emperor Wen of the Northern Qi dynasty.
    • 著作郎 - A title for a scholar or literary official.
    • 雍州司马卢承业 - A historical figure.
    • 临说云 - A phrase indicating narration.

    At the end of the Eastern Wei dynasty, people from the capital Ye ventured into the Western Mountains to mine for silver ore. They had not yet fully exited the mine when it collapsed. One person was trapped behind, with the entrance blocked by rocks, preventing escape. However, the person suffered no physical harm. At the site of the collapse, a small hole remained open, allowing a faint light to be seen. The trapped person, realizing there was no logical way out, focused on reciting Buddhist mantras. The person’s father, hearing that his son had been buried, was unable to find the body. Being destitute, he could not perform rituals to benefit the deceased. He carried a simple bowl of food to a monastery, asking one monk to eat with him. Most monks preferred generous offerings and were unwilling to accept the meager meal. The father, holding the bowl of food, wept bitterly. One monk, moved by his plight, accepted the invitation. After eating, the monk chanted prayers for the father. Then, he left. Meanwhile, the son, still alive in the mine, suddenly saw a monk through the small hole. The monk entered the mine, carrying a bowl of food, which he offered to the trapped person. After eating, the person no longer felt hungry and sat in meditation, maintaining focus for over ten years. When Emperor Wen of the Northern Qi ascended the throne, he built a cool hall in the Western Mountains. The skilled workers cleared the collapsed rocks and discovered that the person in the mine was still alive. They rescued the person and brought him home. The parents were overjoyed, and the family’s fortunes improved. (According to Lu Chengye, the Military Governor of Yongzhou, who recounted the story, the person was a scholar who had been sent to the Western Mountains.) [15]

    北齐时。有冀州人。徒军伐梁。战败。见擒为奴。其父母在乡不知音问。谓以死。为追福。造塼浮图。塼浮图成。设斋会。道俗数百人。方坐食。闻叩门声。主人父出视。见一僧。形容甚雅。谓主人曰。乞斋食黍糜。可以布手巾裹之。并乞鞋一量。主人请留住食。僧不肯曰。欲早去。不暇坐食也。主人如其言。以新布裹糜。并鞋一量奉之。僧受而去。是曰斋时。主人子在江南泽中。为其主牧牛。忽见一僧手持糜一裹。及新鞋一量。至奴所。问曰。亦思归见父母乎。奴泣曰。无敢望也。僧以与糜令坐食。食毕。又与鞋令着之。而敷袈裟于地。令坐袈裟上。僧取袈裟四角。总把擎举而挥之。可移二丈许着地。奴开视。不见僧及袈裟。而身已在其宅门外。入门见。大众方食。父母惊喜。就问。具说由缘。视其巾内。余糜及鞋。乃向奉僧者也。乡邑惊骇。竞为笃信。是曰。月六曰也。因名所造浮图。为六曰浮图。浮图今尚。邑里犹传之矣。 [16]

    During the Northern Qi period, there was a man from Jizhou who was sent with an army to attack Liang. After the battle was lost, he was captured and enslaved. His parents, living in the countryside, did not know of his fate and believed he had died. To seek blessings for him, they built a brick stupa. Once the stupa was completed, they held a vegetarian feast, attended by hundreds of monks and laypeople. While they were sitting to eat, they heard knocking at the door. The owner’s father went to see and found a monk with a very dignified appearance. The monk said to the owner, "I beg for a vegetarian meal of millet porridge, which you can wrap in a handkerchief. And I also ask for a pair of shoes." The owner invited the monk to stay and eat, but the monk declined, saying, "I wish to leave early; I don’t have time to sit and eat." The owner complied, wrapping the porridge in a new cloth and presenting the shoes to the monk. The monk accepted and left.

    At the time of the feast, the owner’s son was in the southern marshes, herding cattle for his master. Suddenly, he saw a monk holding a wrapped bundle of porridge and a pair of shoes. The monk approached him and asked, "Do you also wish to return home to see your parents?" The slave wept and said, "I dare not hope for it." The monk gave him the porridge and told him to sit and eat. After finishing, the monk gave him the shoes and had him put them on. Then, the monk spread his monk’s robe on the ground and told the slave to sit on it. The monk took the four corners of the robe, grasped them together, and lifted it up, moving it about two zhang and placing it on the ground. When the slave opened his eyes, he did not see the monk or the robe, but found himself outside his house. Upon entering, he saw that the crowd was still eating. His parents were overjoyed and asked him what had happened. He explained the whole story. They looked inside the handkerchief and found the remaining porridge and the shoes, which were the same ones they had given to the monk. The villagers were shocked and rushed to become devout believers. This day was the sixth day of the month, so they named the stupa the Sixth Day Stupa. The stupa still stands today, and the village still tells of it. [16]

    梁武帝微时。识一寒士。及即位。游于苑中。见牵舟。帝问之。尚贫贱如故。敕曰。明曰可上谒。吾当与汝县令。此人奉敕而往。会故不得见。频往遇有事。终不得通。自怪之以问沙门宝志。志方为大众讲经。听者数千人。寒士不得进。宝志谓。众曰。有人欲来见问。请开道内之。众人乃为开。此人进未至。宝志迎谓曰。君为不得县令来问耶。终不得矣。但受虚恩耳。过去。帝为斋主。君其疏许施钱五百。而竟不与。是故今曰但蒙许官。终不得也。此人闻之终去。帝亦更不求之（江东道俗至今传之）。 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 敕曰 - Formal imperial command or edict.
    • 明曰可上谒 - You may come to pay respects tomorrow, a polite invitation.
    • 吾当与汝县令 - I will appoint you as the magistrate of a county, a promise of official position.
    • 奉敕而往 - To go in response to the edict, indicating compliance.
    • 会故不得见 - Due to some reason, you cannot meet, suggesting an obstacle.
    • 频往遇有事 - You went repeatedly but always encountered some affairs, indicating repeated attempts thwarted by circumstances.
    • 自怪之以问沙门宝志 - You wondered and asked the monk Baoyi, showing the character's action of seeking clarification.
    • 君为不得县令来问耶 - Are you here to ask why you didn't get the magistrate's position?, a direct question.
    • 终不得矣 - You will never get it, a definitive statement.
    • 但受虚恩耳 - You will only receive empty favors, indicating the futility of the promise.
    • 过去 - In the past, referring to a previous event.
    • 帝为斋主 - The emperor was the host of a vegetarian feast, indicating a religious or ceremonial context.
    • 君其疏许施钱五百 - You promised to donate five hundred coins, indicating a previous agreement.
    • 而竟不与 - But you never gave it, indicating a failure to fulfill the promise.
    • 是故今曰但蒙许官 - That is why you only received the promise of an official position, linking past actions to present consequences.
    • 终不得也 - And you will never get it, reinforcing the previous statement.
    • 此人闻之终去 - This person heard it and finally left, indicating the character's reaction.
    • 帝亦更不求之 - The emperor also stopped pursuing him, showing the emperor's response.
    • 江东道俗至今传之 - The story is still being told by people in the Jiangdong region, indicating the story's lasting impact.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang, the founder of the Liang dynasty (502–557 CE), known for his religious devotion and patronage of Buddhism.
    • 寒士 - Refers to a poor scholar, often used to describe individuals from humble backgrounds.
    • 沙门宝志 - A famous Buddhist monk and prophet who lived during the Southern and Northern Dynasties period, known for his spiritual wisdom and prophecies.
    • 县令 - A local administrative official responsible for governing a county, a position of significant authority.
    • 斋主 - The host of a vegetarian feast, often held for religious or ceremonial purposes.
    • 江东 - Refers to the region east of the Yangtze River, historically a center of cultural and political activity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 寒士 - A poor scholar.
    • 沙门宝志 - Monk Baoyi.
    • 县令 - Magistrate of a county.
    • 斋主 - Host of a vegetarian feast.
    • 江东 - Region east of the Yangtze River.

    During his humble beginnings, Emperor Wu of Liang met a poor scholar. After ascending to the throne, while strolling in the imperial gardens, he saw someone pulling a boat and asked about him. The scholar, still poor and lowly, was summoned by an imperial edict, which stated that he could come to pay respects the next day and would be appointed as the magistrate of a county. The scholar went in response to the edict, but due to some reason, he could not meet the emperor. He tried repeatedly but always encountered some affairs that prevented him from seeing the emperor. Frustrated, he wondered and asked the monk Baoyi. Monk Baoyi was just preaching to a large audience, with thousands of listeners. The poor scholar could not get in. Monk Baoyi called out to the crowd, "There is someone who wants to come and ask a question. Please open the path for him." The crowd then opened the way. As the scholar approached, Monk Baoyi met him and said, "Are you here to ask why you didn't get the magistrate's position? You will never get it. You will only receive empty favors." Monk Baoyi explained, "In the past, the emperor was the host of a vegetarian feast, and you promised to donate five hundred coins, but you never gave it. That is why you only received the promise of an official position, and you will never get it." The scholar heard this and finally left. The emperor also stopped pursuing him. The story is still being told by people in the Jiangdong region to this day. [17]

    杨州严恭者。本泉州人。家富于财。而无兄弟。父母爱恭。言无所违。陈太建初恭年弱冠。请于父母。愿得钱五万。往杨州市物。父母从之。恭乘船载钱。而下去杨州。数十里。江中逢一船载鼋。将诣市卖之。恭问知其故。念鼋当死。请赎之。鼋主曰。我鼋大头。千钱乃可。恭问有几头。答有五十。恭曰。我正有钱五万。愿以赎之。鼋主喜取钱。付鼋而去。恭尽以鼋放江中。空船诣杨州。其鼋主。别恭行十余里。船没而死。是曰恭父母在家。昏时有乌衣客五十人。诣门寄宿。并送钱五万。付恭父曰。君儿在杨州市。附此钱归。愿依数受也。恭父怪愕疑谓恭死。因审之。客曰。儿无恙。但不须钱。故附归耳。恭父受之。记是本钱。而皆水湿。留客为设食。客止。明旦辞去。后月余曰。恭还。父母大喜。既而问附钱所由。恭答无之。父母说客形状。及附钱月曰。乃赎鼋之曰。于是知五十客。皆所赎鼋也。父子惊叹。 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 楊州嚴恭者 - Refers to a person named Yan Gong from Yangzhou.
    • 本泉州人 - Indicates that Yan Gong was originally from Quanzhou.
    • 家富于財 - Means 'rich in wealth.'
    • 而無兄弟 - Means 'but without brothers.'
    • 父母愛恭 - Means 'parents loved Gong.'
    • 言无所违 - Means 'his words were never disobeyed.'
    • 陈太建初 - Refers to the early years of the Tain Jian era during the Chen dynasty (557-589 CE).
    • 恭年弱冠 - Means 'Gong was in his early twenties.'
    • 请于父母 - Means 'asked his parents.'
    • 愿得钱五万 - Means 'wished to get five thousand coins.'
    • 往杨州市物 - Means 'to go to Yangzhou to buy goods.'
    • 父母从之 - Means 'parents agreed.'
    • 恭乘船载钱 - Means 'Gong sailed on a boat carrying money.'
    • 而下去杨州 - Means 'and went down to Yangzhou.'
    • 数十里 - Means 'several tens of miles.'
    • 江中逢一船载鼋 - Means 'encountered a boat carrying turtles in the river.'
    • 将诣市卖之 - Means 'intended to sell them in the market.'
    • 恭问知其故 - Means 'Gong asked to know the reason.'
    • 念鼋当死 - Means 'thought the turtles would die.'
    • 请赎之 - Means 'asked to redeem them.'
    • 鼋主曰 - Means 'the turtle owner said.'
    • 我鼋大头 - Means 'my turtles have large heads.'
    • 千钱乃可 - Means 'a thousand coins each.'
    • 恭问有几头 - Means 'Gong asked how many there were.'
    • 答有五十 - Means 'answered there were fifty.'
    • 恭曰 - Means 'Gong said.'
    • 我正有钱五万 - Means 'I have exactly five thousand coins.'
    • 愿以赎之 - Means 'willing to redeem them.'
    • 鼋主喜取钱 - Means 'the turtle owner happily took the money.'
    • 付鼋而去 - Means 'gave the turtles and left.'
    • 恭尽以鼋放江中 - Means 'Gong released all the turtles into the river.'
    • 空船诣杨州 - Means 'with an empty boat went to Yangzhou.'
    • 其鼋主 - Means 'the turtle owner.'
    • 别恭行十余里 - Means 'traveled more than ten miles away from Gong.'
    • 船没而死 - Means 'the boat sank and he died.'
    • 是日恭父母在家 - Means 'that day Gong's parents were at home.'
    • 昏时有乌衣客五十人 - Means 'at dusk, there were fifty black-clothed guests.'
    • 诣门寄宿 - Means 'came to the door to stay overnight.'
    • 并送钱五万 - Means 'also brought five thousand coins.'
    • 付恭父曰 - Means 'gave to Gong's father and said.'
    • 君儿在杨州市 - Means 'your son is in Yangzhou.'
    • 附此钱归 - Means 'attached these coins to return.'
    • 愿依数受也 - Means 'wish to receive according to the amount.'
    • 恭父怪愕 - Means 'Gong's father was surprised and startled.'
    • 疑谓恭死 - Means 'doubted that Gong was dead.'
    • 因审之 - Means 'therefore investigated it.'
    • 客曰 - Means 'the guest said.'
    • 儿无恙 - Means 'my son is fine.'
    • 但不须钱 - Means 'but does not need the money.'
    • 故附归耳 - Means 'so it is returned.'
    • 恭父受之 - Means 'Gong's father accepted it.'
    • 记是本钱 - Means 'remembered it as the original capital.'
    • 而皆水湿 - Means 'but all were wet.'
    • 留客为设食 - Means 'kept the guests to prepare food.'
    • 客止 - Means 'the guests stopped.'
    • 明旦辞去 - Means 'the next morning they departed.'
    • 后月余日 - Means 'after more than a month.'
    • 恭还 - Means 'Gong returned.'
    • 父母大喜 - Means 'parents were very happy.'
    • 既而问附钱所由 - Means 'then asked about the source of the attached money.'
    • 恭答无之 - Means 'Gong answered that there was none.'
    • 父母说客形状 - Means 'parents described the appearance of the guests.'
    • 及附钱月日 - Means 'and the date of the attached money.'
    • 乃赎鼋之日 - Means 'it was the day Gong redeemed the turtles.'
    • 于是知五十客 - Means 'thus knowing the fifty guests.'
    • 皆所赎鼋也 - Means 'were all the redeemed turtles.'
    • 父子惊叹 - Means 'father and son were amazed.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 楊州 - Refers to Yangzhou, an important city in southern China.
    • 泉州 - Refers to Quanzhou, a city in Fujian Province.
    • 陳太建初 - Refers to the early years of the Tain Jian era during the Chen dynasty (557-589 CE).
    • 弱冠 - Refers to the age of 20, when a man traditionally came of age.
    • 烏衣客 - Refers to guests dressed in black clothes, possibly a reference to a specific group or custom.
    • 鼋 - Refers to a large freshwater turtle, often associated with longevity and good fortune in Chinese culture.
    • 附錢歸 - A practice of attaching money to a letter or message to ensure it is returned safely.

    Yan Gong of Yangzhou. Originally from Quanzhou, he was rich in wealth but without brothers. His parents loved him, and he never disobeyed their words. In the early years of the Tain Jian era during the Chen dynasty, Gong was in his early twenties. He asked his parents for permission to get five thousand coins to go to Yangzhou to buy goods. His parents agreed. Gong sailed on a boat carrying money and went down to Yangzhou. After traveling several tens of miles, he encountered a boat carrying turtles in the river, which intended to sell them in the market. Gong asked to know the reason and thought the turtles would die, so he asked to redeem them. The turtle owner said, "My turtles have large heads, and a thousand coins each." Gong asked how many there were, and the owner answered there were fifty. Gong said, "I have exactly five thousand coins. I am willing to redeem them." The turtle owner happily took the money and gave the turtles to Gong, then left. Gong released all the turtles into the river and continued his journey with an empty boat to Yangzhou. The turtle owner, traveling more than ten miles away from Gong, had his boat sink and he died. That day, Gong's parents were at home. At dusk, there were fifty black-clothed guests who came to the door to stay overnight. They also brought five thousand coins and gave them to Gong's father, saying, "Your son is in Yangzhou. These coins are attached for safe return. We wish to receive them according to the amount." Gong's father was surprised and startled, doubting that Gong was dead. He investigated it, and the guest said, "My son is fine. He does not need the money, so it is returned." Gong's father accepted it, remembering it as the original capital, but all were wet. He kept the guests to prepare food, and the guests stopped. The next morning they departed. After more than a month, Gong returned, and his parents were very happy. They then asked about the source of the attached money. Gong answered that there was none. His parents described the appearance of the guests and the date of the attached money, which was the day Gong redeemed the turtles. Thus, they knew the fifty guests were all the redeemed turtles. Father and son were amazed. [18]

    因共往杨州起精舍。专写法华经。遂徙家杨州。家转富。大起房廊为写经之室。庄严清净。供给丰厚。书生常数十人。杨州道俗。共相崇敬。号曰严法华。尝有知亲。从从贷经钱一万。恭不获已与之。贷者受钱。以船载归。中路船倾。所贷之钱落水。而船人不溺。是曰恭入钱库。见有万湿钱如斯出水。恭甚怪之。后见前贷钱人。乃知湿钱是所贷者。又有商人。至宫湖。于神所。祭酒食。并上物。其夜梦。神送物还之。谓曰。倩君为我持此。奉严法华。以供经用也。且而所上神物。皆在其前。于是商人叹异。送达恭处。而倍加厚施。其后。恭至市买经纸。适遇少钱。忽见一人。持钱三千。授恭曰。君买纸。言毕不见。而钱在其前。怪异如此。非一。隋开皇末恭死。子孙传其业。隋季盗贼至江都者。皆相与约勿入严法华里。里人赖之获全。其家今。写经不已（州邑共见。京师人士亦多知之。驸马守国公萧锐最所详审也）。 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 因共往楊州起精舍 - Together they went to Yangzhou to build a study hall.
    • 專寫法華經 - Dedicated to writing the *Lotus Sutra*.
    • 遂徙家楊州 - They then moved their family to Yangzhou.
    • 家轉富 - Their family became wealthier.
    • 大起房廊為寫經之室 - They built large rooms for writing scriptures.
    • 莊嚴清淨 - Elegant and pure.
    • 供給豐厚 - Ample supplies.
    • 書生常數十人 - There were often dozens of students.
    • 楊州道俗共相崇敬 - The people of Yangzhou, both laypeople and monks, revered him.
    • 號曰嚴法華 - He was known as Yan Fa-hua.
    • 從從貸經錢一萬 - He borrowed 10,000 coins for scriptures.
    • 恭不獲已與之 - Yan reluctantly gave it to him.
    • 貸者受錢，以船載歸 - The borrower accepted the money and took it back in a boat.
    • 中路船傾 - The boat capsized in midstream.
    • 所貸之錢落水 - The borrowed money fell into the water.
    • 而船人不溺 - But the boatman did not drown.
    • 是曰恭入錢庫 - It was said that Yan entered the treasury.
    • 見有萬濕錢如斯出水 - He saw 10,000 wet coins like those that had come out of the water.
    • 恭甚怪之 - Yan was very surprised by this.
    • 後見前貸錢人 - Later, he saw the person who had borrowed the money.
    • 乃知濕錢是所貸者 - He then realized that the wet coins were the ones he had lent.
    • 又有商人 - There was also a merchant.
    • 至宮湖 - He arrived at Gonghu Lake.
    • 於神所，祭酒食 - At the temple, he offered wine and food.
    • 並上物 - And presented offerings.
    • 其夜夢 - That night, he dreamed.
    • 神送物還之 - The deity returned the offerings to him.
    • 謂曰 - Saying to him.
    • 倩君為我持此，奉嚴法華，以供經用也 - Please take this for me to offer to Yan Fa-hua for the use of writing scriptures.
    • 且而所上神物，皆在其前 - And the items he had offered to the deity were all before him.
    • 於是商人嘆異 - The merchant was amazed.
    • 送达恭处 - He delivered the items to Yan.
    • 而倍加厚施 - And gave even more generously.
    • 其后 - After that.
    • 恭至市买经纸 - Yan went to the market to buy paper for writing scriptures.
    • 适遇少钱 - He just happened to have a little money.
    • 忽见一人 - Suddenly, he saw a person.
    • 持钱三千，授恭曰 - Who held 3,000 coins and gave them to Yan, saying.
    • 君买纸 - Buy paper.
    • 言毕不见 - After he spoke, the person disappeared.
    • 而钱在其前 - And the coins were in front of him.
    • 怪异如此，非一 - Such strange occurrences were not one.
    • 隋开皇末恭死 - At the end of the Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty, Yan died.
    • 子孙传其业 - His descendants continued his work.
    • 隋季盗贼至江都者 - Bandits who came to Jiangdu at the end of the Sui dynasty.
    • 皆相与约勿入严法华里 - All agreed not to enter Yan Fa-hua's neighborhood.
    • 里人赖之获全 - The people of the neighborhood relied on him and were saved.
    • 其家今，写经不已 - His family continues to write scriptures without stopping.
    • 州邑共见 - Both the prefecture and the county saw this.
    • 京师人士亦多知之 - People in the capital also knew about it.
    • 驸马守国公萧锐最所详审也 - Prince Xiao Rui, the Duke of Guo, was the most detailed and thorough in his knowledge of this.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 楊州 - Refers to Yangzhou, an important city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and economic significance.
    • 法華經 - Refers to the *Lotus Sutra*, a significant Mahayana Buddhist text.
    • 精舍 - Refers to a study hall or monastery, a place for scholarly and religious activities.
    • 隋开皇 - Refers to the Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty (581-604 CE), a period of significant political and cultural development.
    • 江都 - Refers to Jiangdu, an ancient name for Yangzhou, known for its historical and cultural importance.
    • 驸马守国公萧锐 - Refers to Prince Xiao Rui, the Duke of Guo, a prominent figure in the Sui dynasty.

    Yan Fa-hua, a devout Buddhist, gathered his fellow believers to Yangzhou to build a study hall dedicated to writing the Lotus Sutra. They then moved their family to Yangzhou, where their wealth gradually increased. They constructed large, elegant rooms for writing scriptures, which were well-supplied and often attended by dozens of students. The people of Yangzhou, both laypeople and monks, revered him, and he was known as Yan Fa-hua.

    One day, a relative borrowed 10,000 coins for scriptures. Yan reluctantly gave the money to him. The borrower accepted the money and returned home by boat, but the boat capsized in midstream, causing the borrowed money to fall into the water. Miraculously, the boatman did not drown. It was said that Yan then entered the treasury and saw 10,000 wet coins like those that had come out of the water. Yan was very surprised by this and later realized that the wet coins were the ones he had lent.

    Another time, a merchant arrived at Gonghu Lake and offered wine and food at a temple, presenting additional offerings. That night, he dreamed that a deity returned the offerings to him, saying, "Please take this for me to offer to Yan Fa-hua for the use of writing scriptures." The items he had offered to the deity were all before him. The merchant was amazed and delivered the items to Yan, who gave even more generously.

    Later, Yan went to the market to buy paper for writing scriptures. He just happened to have a little money when suddenly, he saw a person holding 3,000 coins and gave them to Yan, saying, "Buy paper." After he spoke, the person disappeared, and the coins were left in front of Yan. Such strange occurrences were not one.

    At the end of the Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty, Yan died, but his descendants continued his work. Bandits who came to Jiangdu at the end of the Sui dynasty all agreed not to enter Yan Fa-hua's neighborhood, and the people of the neighborhood relied on him and were saved. His family continues to write scriptures without stopping. Both the prefecture and the county saw this, and people in the capital also knew about it. Prince Xiao Rui, the Duke of Guo, was the most detailed and thorough in his knowledge of this. [19]

    冥报记卷中
    　　隋开皇中。魏州刺史博陵崔彦武。因行部。至一邑。愕然惊喜。谓从者。吾昔尝在此邑中。为人妇。今知家处。回马入修巷。曲至一家叩门。命主人。主人公年老。走出拜谒。彦武入家。先升其堂。视东壁上。去地六七尺有高隆。谓主人曰。吾昔所读法华经。并金钗五只。藏此壁中。高处是也。经第七卷尾后纸。火烧失文字。吾至今。每诵此经。至第七卷尾。恒忘失不能记。因令左右凿壁。果得经函。开第七卷尾。及金钗。并如其言。主人涕泣曰。亡妻存曰。常读此经。钗亦是亡妻之物。妻因产死。遂失所在。不淫使君。乃示其处。彦武亦云。庭前槐树。吾欲产时。自解头发。置此树空中。试令人探。果得发。主人于是主人悲喜。彦武留衣物。厚给主人而去。崔尚书敦礼说云然。往年见卢文励。亦同。但言齐州刺史不得姓名。不崔具。仍依崔录。 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 冥報記卷中 - Title of the text, meaning 'Records of Karmic Retribution, Volume 5.'
    • 開皇中 - Refers to the middle of the Kaihuang era (581-604 CE), the reign of Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 魏州刺史博陵崔彦武 - Refers to Cui Yanwu, a governor of Weizhou from Boling (a place name).
    • 行部 - Term for the inspection tours conducted by officials.
    • 愕然驚喜 - Compound expression meaning 'startled with joy,' indicating a sudden and positive reaction.
    • 吾昔嘗在此邑中 - Personal statement meaning 'I once lived in this town.'
    • 回馬入修巷 - Vivid description of turning back and entering a long alley.
    • 曲至一家叩門 - Phrase describing the action of turning into a house and knocking on the door.
    • 主人公年老 - Description of the homeowner as an elderly person.
    • 走出拜谒 - Respectful action of the elderly homeowner coming out to greet.
    • 先升其堂 - Action of entering the main hall first.
    • 去地六七尺有高隆 - Description of a raised area on the wall about six to seven feet from the ground.
    • 法華經 - Refers to the Lotus Sutra, a significant Buddhist scripture.
    • 金钗五只 - Five gold hairpins, a valuable item.
    • 第七卷尾后纸 - The last part of the seventh scroll of the scripture.
    • 火烧失文字 - Indicates that the text was partially burned, causing some words to be lost.
    • 恒忘失不能记 - Statement about frequently forgetting the part of the scripture due to the missing text.
    • 凿壁 - The action of chiseling through the wall.
    • 果得经函 - The result of finding the scripture case.
    • 主人涕泣 - The elderly homeowner weeps.
    • 亡妻存曰 - Statement about the deceased wife when she was alive.
    • 庭前槐树 - Refers to a槐 tree in front of the courtyard.
    • 自解头发 - The action of the wife cutting her own hair.
    • 置此树空中 - Placing the hair in a high place in the tree.
    • 试令人探 - Asking someone to check if the hair is still there.
    • 果得发 - The result of finding the hair.
    • 崔尚书敦礼说云然 - Statement indicating that Cui Shangshi, a high-ranking official, told this story.
    • 往年见卢文励 - Reference to seeing Lu Wenli in the past.
    • 齐州刺史不得姓名 - The name of the Qi Prefect is not known.
    • 但言齐州刺史 - Only the title of Qi Prefect is mentioned.
    • 仍依崔录 - Still following Cui's record.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋朝 - The Sui Dynasty (581-618 CE), a short-lived but significant dynasty in Chinese history.
    • 魏州 - A state in northern China during the Sui and Tang dynasties.
    • 博陵 - A place name, likely referring to a region or county.
    • 法華經 - The Lotus Sutra, a central text in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 金钗 - Gold hairpins, often used as gifts or dowry items.
    • 冥報記 - A collection of stories about karmic retribution, reflecting Buddhist beliefs in cause and effect.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冥報記 - The title of the text.
    • 開皇 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 魏州 - A state in northern China.
    • 博陵 - A place name.
    • 崔彦武 - The name of the official.
    • 法華經 - The Lotus Sutra.
    • 金钗 - Gold hairpins.
    • 崔尚书敦礼 - The name of the high-ranking official.
    • 卢文励 - The name of another person.
    • 齐州刺史 - The title of the Qi Prefect.

    Records of Karmic Retribution, Volume 5

    During the middle of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, Cui Yanwu, the governor of Weizhou from Boling, was on an inspection tour. He arrived at a town and was startled with joy. He told his attendants, "I once lived in this town and was married. Now that I know where my former home is, I will return there." Turning his horse, he entered a long alley and turned into a house, knocking on the door. The elderly homeowner came out to greet him respectfully. Cui Yanwu entered the house and first ascended to the main hall. He looked up at the eastern wall and saw a raised area about six to seven feet from the ground. He said to the homeowner, "I once hid my copy of the Lotus Sutra and five gold hairpins in this wall. They are hidden in the high spot." He also mentioned that the last part of the seventh scroll of the scripture had been partially burned, causing some words to be lost. He said, "Until now, every time I recite this scripture, I always forget the part at the end of the seventh scroll." He then ordered his attendants to chisel through the wall. They found the scripture case, and when they opened the seventh scroll, they found the gold hairpins, just as he had said. The elderly homeowner wept and said, "My deceased wife, when she was alive, used to read this scripture. The hairpins are also hers. She died during childbirth and vanished. She did not seduce you, but she showed you the place where she hid these items." Cui Yanwu also said, "There is a槐 tree in front of the courtyard. When I was about to give birth, I cut my own hair and placed it in a high place in the tree. I asked someone to check if the hair was still there, and they found it." The homeowner was moved to tears and joy. Cui Yanwu left some clothing and gave the homeowner generous gifts before departing. According to Cui Shangshi, a high-ranking official, this story was told to him. He also mentioned that he had seen Lu Wenli in the past, who had a similar account. However, the name of the Qi Prefect was not known, and the story was still recorded according to Cui's account. [20]

    隋大业中。有客僧。行至大山庙。求寄宿。庙令曰。此无别舍。唯神庙庑下。可宿。然而比来。寄宿者辄死僧曰。无苦也。令不得已从之为设床于庑下。僧至夜端坐。诵经可一更。闻屋中环佩声。须臾神出。为僧礼拜。僧曰。闻比来宿者多死岂檀越害之耶。愿见护。神曰。遇其死时将至。闻弟子声。因自惧死。非杀之也。愿师无虑。僧因延坐。谈说如人。良久。僧问曰。闻世人传说。大山治鬼。宁有之也。神曰。弟子薄福有之。岂欲见先亡已乎。僧曰。有两同学僧。先死。愿见之。神问名曰。一人已生人间。一在人狱。罪重不可见。与师就见可也。僧甚悦。因共起出门。不遂而至一所。多见庙狱。 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 无苦也 - A polite way of saying 'don't worry' or 'there's no problem.'
    • 端坐 - To sit upright, often implying a state of meditation or concentration.
    • 先亡 - Refers to the deceased, often used in a respectful context.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋大业中 - The reign of Emperor Yang of Sui (569–618), a period known for its cultural and religious developments.
    • 客僧 - Traveling monks were common in ancient China, often seeking spiritual guidance and lodging.
    • 大山庙 - A temple dedicated to a mountain deity, which was a common site for worship and lodging.
    • 环佩声 - The sound of jade pendants was a symbol of nobility and elegance, often associated with deities or high-ranking officials.
    • 人狱 - The idea of imprisonment reflects the legal and moral framework of ancient Chinese society.

    Proper Nouns

    • 隋大业中 - The period during the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 大山庙 - A temple dedicated to a mountain deity.
    • 客僧 - A traveling monk.
    • 庙令 - The official in charge of the temple.
    • 大山 - The deity or mountain associated with the temple.
    • 先亡 - The deceased.

    During the Dàyè period of the Sui dynasty, a traveling monk arrived at a temple dedicated to a mountain deity and requested lodging. The temple official said, "There is no other place to stay; only the eaves of the temple can be used." However, recently, those who stayed there always died. The monk said, "Don't worry." The official, having no choice, allowed him to stay and provided a bed under the eaves.

    That night, the monk sat upright and recited sutras for about one watch. He heard the sound of jade pendants in the room. Soon, the deity appeared and bowed to the monk. The monk said, "I have heard that recently, those who stayed here always died. Did you harm them? Please protect me." The deity said, "When they die, I hear their disciples' voices and become afraid of death. I do not kill them. Please do not worry." The monk then invited the deity to sit and talked to him as if he were a human. After a long time, the monk asked, "I have heard that people say the mountain deity can subdue ghosts. Is this true?" The deity said, "I have little merit and can do so. Do you want to see the deceased?" The monk said, "Two of my fellow monks died earlier. I wish to see them." The deity asked for their names and said, "One has already been reborn in the human world, and the other is in prison. Because of heavy sins, he cannot be seen. You can go and see the one in the human world." The monk was very pleased and went out with the deity. They did not walk far before arriving at a place where they saw many temples and prisons. [21]

    火光甚盛。神将僧入一院。遥见一人在火中号呼不能言。形变不可复识。而血肉焦臭。令人伤心。此是也。师不复欲历观也。僧愁愍求出。俄而至庙。又与神同坐。因问欲救同学。有得理耶。神曰。可得耳。能为写法华经者。便免。既而将曙。神辞僧入堂。旦而庙令视僧不死。怪异之。僧因为说。仍即为写法华经一部。经既成。庄严毕。又将经就庙宿。其夜神出如初。欢喜礼拜。慰问来意。僧以事告。神曰。弟子知之。师为写经。始尽题目。彼已脱免。今久出生不在也。然此处不净洁。不可安经。愿师还送经向寺。言说久之将晓。辞而去。入僧送经于寺（杭州别驾张德言。前任兖州。具知其事。自向临说云尔也）。 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 火光甚盛 - The fire was very bright; indicates a vivid and possibly supernatural scene.
    • 形变不可复识 - The form had changed beyond recognition; suggests a dramatic transformation.
    • 血肉焦臭 - The flesh was burned and smelled of charred meat; describes a gruesome and intense scene.
    • 愁愍求出 - Feeling sorrow and compassion, he sought to leave; shows the monk's emotional response and desire to escape.
    • 能为写法华经者，便免 - If one writes the Lotus Sutra, they will be saved; indicates a specific action for salvation.
    • 经既成，庄严毕 - Once the sutra was completed, it was decorated; indicates the completion and decoration of the sutra.
    • 其夜神出如初 - That night, the deity appeared as before; indicates the deity's return.
    • 欢喜礼拜，慰问来意 - The deity happily bowed and inquired about the monk's purpose; shows the deity's friendly and inquisitive nature.
    • 师以事告 - The master then told him the story; indicates the monk's recounting.
    • 弟子知之 - The disciple knows; shows the deity's acknowledgment.
    • 师为写经，始尽题目，彼已脱免，今久出生不在也 - The master wrote the sutra, completing the title, and the other has already been saved and has long since been reborn; indicates the sequence of events and the outcome.
    • 然此处不净洁，不可安经 - However, this place is not clean, and it is not suitable for the sutra; shows the deity's concern for the sutra's sanctity.
    • 愿师还送经向寺 - I hope you will return and take the sutra to the temple; indicates the deity's request.
    • 言说久之将晓，辞而去 - They talked for a long time until dawn; shows the duration of their conversation.
    • 入僧送经于寺 - The monk then took the sutra to the temple; shows the final action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神将 - Refers to a deity or supernatural being, often associated with religious or mystical contexts in Chinese culture.
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a significant Buddhist scripture that emphasizes the universality of Buddhahood and the path to enlightenment.
    • 庙令 - Refers to the temple official or caretaker, a common figure in religious institutions.
    • 杭州别驾张德言 - Zhang Deyan, a historical figure who served as a governor in Hangzhou and was known for his knowledge and recounting of such events.

    Proper Nouns

    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a significant Buddhist scripture.
    • 杭州别驾张德言 - Zhang Deyan, a historical figure who served as a governor in Hangzhou.

    Fire was very bright. The deity led the monk into a courtyard. From a distance, he saw a person in the fire, calling out but unable to speak, with a form so changed it was unrecognizable, and the flesh burned and smelled of charred meat, which was heart-wrenching. This was it. The master no longer wanted to observe further. Feeling sorrow and compassion, he sought to leave. Soon he arrived at the temple. He sat down with the deity again and asked if there was a way to save his fellow student. The deity said, "It can be done. If one writes the Lotus Sutra, they will be saved." Soon it was dawn, and the deity bid the monk farewell and entered the hall. In the morning, the temple official saw that the monk was not dead and found it strange. The monk then explained. He then wrote the Lotus Sutra. Once the sutra was completed, it was decorated, and he took the sutra to the temple to stay overnight. That night, the deity appeared as before, happily bowed, and inquired about the monk's purpose. The master then told him the story. The deity said, "I know. The master wrote the sutra, completing the title, and the other has already been saved and has long since been reborn." However, this place is not clean, and it is not suitable for the sutra. I hope you will return and take the sutra to the temple. They talked for a long time until dawn, and then they bade farewell and left. The monk then took the sutra to the temple. (Zhang Deyan, the former governor of Yanzhou, who now serves in Hangzhou, knows all about this. He recounted it himself.) [22]

    国子祭酒萧璟。兰陵人。梁武帝之孙。梁王岿之第五子也。梁灭入隋。姊为炀帝皇后。生长贵盛。而家崇佛法。大业中。自以诵法华经。乃依经文作多宝塔。以檀香为之。塔高三尺许。其上方厚等为木多宝像。经数年。其兄子诠在宅。朝起。忽于前院草中。见一檀木浮图。盖下有一□石佛像。制作异于中国。面形似胡。其眼睛以银为之。中黑精光净如自然者。诠走告璟。璟视惊喜。取盖还。试置塔上。宛然相称。如故作者。虽木色小异。而塔形更妙。以佛像安塔中。亦相称如故造。璟喜欢。自以精诚所感。其佛像函内有舍利百余枚。璟女尼年少。窃疑。胡僧每云。舍利锤打不破。乃试取三十枚。于石上斧打之。 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 國子祭酒 - High-ranking official in charge of the Imperial Academy during the Sui and Tang dynasties.
    • 蘭陵人 - Refers to someone from Lanling, a place name in Shandong Province.
    • 梁武帝之孫 - Indicates that the person is a grandson of Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 梁王岿之第五子也 - Specifies that the person is the fifth son of Liang Wang Kui.
    • 梁滅入隋 - Means that after the fall of the Liang dynasty, the person entered the Sui dynasty.
    • 姊為炀帝皇后 - Indicates that the person's sister became the empress of Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 長大貴盛 - Means 'grew up in great wealth and status.'
    • 家崇佛法 - Indicates that the family revered Buddhism.
    • 大業中 - Refers to the Dali era during the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 依經文作多寶塔 - Means 'constructed a Multitude of Treasures Pagoda according to the text of the Sutra.'
    • 檀香為之 - Means 'made of sandalwood.'
    • 塔高三尺許 - Indicates that the pagoda was about three feet tall.
    • 上方厚等為木多寶像 - Means 'the top was made of wood to resemble the Multitude of Treasures Buddha.'
    • 經數年 - Means 'after several years.'
    • 忽於前院草中 - Means 'suddenly in the grass of the front courtyard.'
    • 見一檀木浮圖 - Means 'saw a sandalwood stupa.'
    • 蓋下有一□石佛像 - Means 'under the canopy was a stone Buddha statue.'
    • 製作異於中國 - Means 'made differently from those in China.'
    • 面形似胡 - Means 'the facial features resembled those of a foreigner.'
    • 眼睛以銀為之 - Means 'the eyes were made of silver.'
    • 中黑精光淨如自然者 - Means 'with a black, lustrous appearance as if naturally formed.'
    • 試置塔上 - Means 'tried placing it on the pagoda.'
    • 宛然相稱 - Means 'fit perfectly.'
    • 雖木色小異 - Means 'although the wood color was slightly different.'
    • 塔形更妙 - Means 'the pagoda shape was even more exquisite.'
    • 以佛像安塔中 - Means 'placed the Buddha statue inside the pagoda.'
    • 亦相稱如故造 - Means 'also fit perfectly as if originally made.'
    • 自以精誠所感 - Means 'he believed it was due to his sincere devotion.'
    • 其佛像函内有舍利百余枚 - Means 'the Buddha statue contained over a hundred relics.'
    • 女尼年少 - Means 'a young nun.'
    • 胡僧每云 - Means 'the foreign monk often said.'
    • 舍利锤打不破 - Means 'the relics cannot be broken by hammering.'
    • 乃试取三十枚 - Means 'he then tried taking thirty of them.'
    • 于石上斧打之 - Means 'and struck them with an axe on a stone.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 國子祭酒 is a high-ranking official in charge of the Imperial Academy, indicating the person's high status and education.
    • 蘭陵 is a place name in Shandong Province, indicating the person's origin.
    • 梁武帝 is Emperor Wu of Liang, a significant figure in the Southern Dynasties.
    • 梁王岿 is Liang Wang Kui, a prince of the Liang dynasty.
    • 隋 is the Sui dynasty, which succeeded the Liang dynasty.
    • 炀帝 is Emperor Yang of Sui, known for his ambitious projects and eventual downfall.
    • 多寶塔 is a type of pagoda, often associated with Buddhist temples.
    • 檀香 is sandalwood, a valuable and fragrant wood used in religious contexts.
    • 舍利 are relics of the Buddha or other enlightened beings, considered sacred in Buddhism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蕭璟 - Xiao Jing, the person being described.
    • 蘭陵 - Lanling, a place name in Shandong Province.
    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 梁王岿 - Liang Wang Kui.
    • 隋 - The Sui dynasty.
    • 炀帝 - Emperor Yang of Sui.
    • 多寶塔 - Multitude of Treasures Pagoda.
    • 檀香 - Sandalwood.
    • 舍利 - Relics of the Buddha.

    Xiao Jing, the Grand Master of the Imperial Academy, was from Lanling. He was the grandson of Emperor Wu of Liang and the fifth son of Liang Wang Kui. After the fall of the Liang dynasty, he entered the Sui dynasty. His sister became the empress of Emperor Yang of Sui. He grew up in great wealth and status, and his family revered Buddhism. During the Dali era, he believed that by reciting the Lotus Sutra, he constructed a Multitude of Treasures Pagoda according to the text of the Sutra, made of sandalwood. The pagoda was about three feet tall, and the top was made of wood to resemble the Multitude of Treasures Buddha. After several years, his nephew Quan, who lived in the house, suddenly saw a sandalwood stupa in the grass of the front courtyard upon waking up one morning. Under the canopy was a stone Buddha statue, made differently from those in China. The facial features resembled those of a foreigner, and the eyes were made of silver, with a black, lustrous appearance as if naturally formed. Quan ran to tell Xiao Jing, who was surprised and delighted upon seeing it. He took the canopy back and tried placing it on the pagoda, and it fit perfectly, as if originally made. Although the wood color was slightly different, the pagoda shape was even more exquisite. Placing the Buddha statue inside the pagoda also fit perfectly as if originally made. Xiao Jing was very happy and believed it was due to his sincere devotion. The Buddha statue contained over a hundred relics. Xiao Jing's young nun sister suspected and thought that the foreign monk often said the relics cannot be broken by hammering. She then tried taking thirty of them and struck them with an axe on a stone. [23]

    舍利了无著者。女就地拾觅。唯得三四枚。余并失。不见所在。既而惧以告璟。璟往塔中视之。则舍利皆在如旧。璟从此曰诵法华经一遍。以至干身终。贞观十一年病。萧后及弟侄视之。璟与相见。各令燃香因即共别。唯留弟宗公瑀及女为尼者。令燃香诵经。顷之谓其尼曰。我欲去。普贤菩萨来迎。我在东院。师可迎之。尼如言往迎之未还。璟曰。此院不净。不肯来。吾当往就。汝等好住。因与瑀等别。仍起长跪合掌。正向西方。顷之。倒卧遂绝。遗令。独载一车。敛以在身之服。妇人不得送葬。勿以肉祭。制坎才令没棺。朝野叹其通寤。家人奉而行之（仲圭弟孝谐。为大理主簿。为临说。更闻州人亦同云尔）。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 舍利了无著者 - Indicates that the relics (舍利) were nowhere to be found, emphasizing their absence.
    • 女就地拾觅 - Literal expression meaning 'the woman searched on the ground,' describing a specific action.
    • 唯得三四枚 - Means 'only obtained three or four pieces,' indicating a small quantity.
    • 余并失，不见所在 - Indicates that the rest were lost and could not be found, emphasizing the loss.
    • 璟从此曰诵法华经一遍 - Indicates that Jing started reciting the Lotus Sutra once, marking the beginning of a new practice.
    • 以至干身终 - Means 'until the end of his life,' indicating a continuous action.
    • 贞观十一年病 - Indicates the year of illness during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 萧后及弟侄视之 - Means 'the Empress Xiao and his brothers and nephews visited him,' indicating a visit by family members.
    • 璟与相见 - Means 'Jing met with them,' indicating a meeting.
    • 各令燃香因即共别 - Means 'each was instructed to light incense and then they parted,' indicating a ritual farewell.
    • 唯留弟宗公瑀及女为尼者 - Means 'only left his brother Zonggong Yu and his daughter who became a nun,' indicating those left behind.
    • 令燃香诵经 - Means 'instructed to light incense and recite sutras,' indicating a religious practice.
    • 顷之谓其尼曰 - Means 'soon after, he said to his nun,' indicating a specific moment of communication.
    • 我欲去，普贤菩萨来迎 - Means 'I want to go, the Bodhisattva Universal Worthy is coming to welcome me,' indicating a spiritual transition.
    • 我在东院 - Means 'I am in the eastern courtyard,' indicating a location.
    • 师可迎之 - Means 'you can welcome him,' indicating a request.
    • 此院不净，不肯来 - Means 'this courtyard is not clean, he will not come,' indicating a condition.
    • 吾当往就 - Means 'I will go to him,' indicating a decision.
    • 汝等好住 - Means 'you all live well,' indicating a farewell message.
    • 因与瑀等别 - Means 'then he parted from Yu and others,' indicating a separation.
    • 仍起长跪合掌 - Means 'then he rose and knelt long, with palms together,' indicating a respectful gesture.
    • 正向西方 - Means 'facing west,' indicating a direction.
    • 顷之，倒卧遂绝 - Means 'soon after, he lay down and passed away,' indicating the end of life.
    • 遗令 - Means 'left a will,' indicating a final instruction.
    • 独载一车 - Means 'to be carried alone in a cart,' indicating a specific arrangement.
    • 敛以在身之服 - Means 'to be dressed in the clothes he wore,' indicating the attire for burial.
    • 妇人不得送葬 - Means 'women are not to attend the funeral,' indicating a restriction.
    • 勿以肉祭 - Means 'do not offer meat as an offering,' indicating a prohibition.
    • 制坎才令没棺 - Means 'to dig a grave just deep enough to cover the coffin,' indicating a specific burial practice.
    • 朝野叹其通寤 - Means 'the court and the people admired his enlightenment,' indicating widespread recognition.
    • 家人奉而行之 - Means 'his family followed his instructions,' indicating compliance.
    • 仲圭弟孝谐 - Refers to Jing's brother Xiaoxie, indicating a familial relationship.
    • 为大理主簿 - Means 'serving as a clerk in the Dali Temple,' indicating a position.
    • 为临说 - Means 'spoke to Lin,' indicating a communication.
    • 更闻州人亦同云尔 - Means 'it was also said by people in the state,' indicating additional information.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 舍利 - Relics of the Buddha, often venerated in Buddhist practice.
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a significant Mahayana Buddhist scripture.
    • 贞观十一年 - The eleventh year of the Zhenguan era (637 AD) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 萧后 - Empress Xiao, the wife of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 普贤菩萨 - The Bodhisattva Universal Worthy, a prominent figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 东院 - The eastern courtyard, likely a specific location within a temple or residence.
    • 仲圭 - Jing's brother, indicating a familial relationship.
    • 孝谐 - Jing's brother Xiaoxie, indicating a familial relationship.
    • 大理主簿 - A clerical position in the Dali Temple, a judicial institution.
    • 临 - Likely a person or place, but the exact identity is unclear without additional context.
    • 州人 - People of the state, indicating a broader audience.

    Proper Nouns

    • 舍利 - Relics of the Buddha.
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra.
    • 贞观十一年 - The eleventh year of the Zhenguan era (637 AD).
    • 萧后 - Empress Xiao.
    • 普贤菩萨 - The Bodhisattva Universal Worthy.
    • 东院 - The eastern courtyard.
    • 仲圭 - Jing's brother.
    • 孝谐 - Jing's brother Xiaoxie.
    • 大理主簿 - A clerical position in the Dali Temple.
    • 临 - Likely a person or place.
    • 州人 - People of the state.

    The relics were nowhere to be found. The woman searched on the ground and only obtained three or four pieces, while the rest were lost and could not be found. Fearing this, she informed Jing. Jing went to the pagoda to look and found that the relics were all there as before. From then on, Jing began to recite the Lotus Sutra once, continuing until the end of his life. In the eleventh year of the Zhenguan era, he fell ill. Empress Xiao and his brothers and nephews visited him. Jing met with them, and each was instructed to light incense, then they parted. Only his brother Zonggong Yu and his daughter who became a nun were left behind, and they were instructed to light incense and recite sutras. Soon after, Jing said to his nun, "I want to go; the Bodhisattva Universal Worthy is coming to welcome me. I am in the eastern courtyard. You can welcome him." The nun went as instructed to welcome him, but he did not return. Jing said, "This courtyard is not clean, he will not come. I will go to him. You all live well." Then he parted from Yu and others, rose, knelt long with palms together, facing west. Soon after, he lay down and passed away. He left a will: to be carried alone in a cart, dressed in the clothes he wore, with women not attending the funeral, no meat offerings, and the grave dug just deep enough to cover the coffin. The court and the people admired his enlightenment, and his family followed his instructions. (Jing's brother Xiaoxie served as a clerk in the Dali Temple and spoke to Lin. It was also said by people in the state.) [24]

    临印韦仲圭者。天性孝悌。为州里所敬。年十七。郡表左异为蒙阳长。父廉目资阳郡丞。以老归。武德中。遘病。仲圭不释冠带而养。久之父卒。谢遣妻妾。守干墓左。信佛教。诵法华经。昼则负土成境。夜专诵经典。精此氏不倦。终三年不归。尝有虎夜至墙前。蹲踞听经。久而不去。仲圭正念曰。不愿猛兽之相通也。即起去。明旦见绕墓。生芝草七十二茎。当坟前者。行次第如人种植。皆朱茎紫盖。盖径五寸。光色异常。邻里以告州县。时辛君为刺史。沈裕为别驾。共至墓所察之。忽有一鸟如鸭。衔双鲤。置于地而去。君昌等。尤深嗟叹。采芝封奏。诏表门闾（临以贞观七年奉使江东。杨州针医甄陀。为临说此）。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天性孝悌 - Describes a person's innate filial piety and respect for elders.
    • 为州里所敬 - Indicates that he was respected by the local community.
    • 不释冠带而养 - Means to care for the father without removing his official attire, indicating dedication.
    • 守干墓左 - Means to guard the tomb on the left side, likely a symbolic act of filial piety.
    • 信佛教 - Indicates belief in Buddhism.
    • 诵法华经 - Means to recite the Lotus Sutra, a central text in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 昼则负土成境 - Means to carry soil during the day to create a boundary or enclosure.
    • 夜专诵经典 - Means to focus on reciting scriptures at night.
    • 精此氏不倦 - Means to be diligent and tireless in this practice.
    • 终三年不归 - Means to remain for three years without returning home.
    • 不愿猛兽之相通也 - Means to not wish for communication with wild animals.
    • 采芝封奏 - Means to collect and report on the discovery of lingzhi mushrooms.
    • 诏表门闾 - Means to receive an imperial decree to honor the family with a tablet at the gate.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 臨印韋仲圭 - A historical figure, possibly a local official known for his filial piety.
    • 武德中 - Refers to the early Tang Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Gaozu.
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a significant Buddhist text.
    • 采芝封奏 - Refers to the practice of reporting unusual natural phenomena to the imperial court, often leading to honors or rewards.
    • 诏表门闾 - An imperial decree to honor a family, typically involving the installation of a tablet at the entrance of their home.

    Proper Nouns

    • 臨印韋仲圭 - A historical figure, possibly a local official.
    • 左异 - A person recommended to be the magistrate of Mengyang.
    • 蒙阳长 - The position of magistrate of Mengyang.
    • 廉目 - The father's name.
    • 资阳郡丞 - The position of deputy magistrate of Ziyang.
    • 武德中 - The early Tang Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Gaozu.
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra.
    • 辛君 - A person named Xin, likely a local official.
    • 沈裕 - A person named Shen Yu, likely a local official.

    Wei Zhonggui, an official in the county, was known for his innate filial piety and respect for elders, and he was greatly respected by the local community. At the age of seventeen, the county recommended Zuo Yi to be the magistrate of Mengyang. Wei Zhonggui's father, named Lianmu, served as the deputy magistrate of Ziyang and retired due to old age. In the early Tang Dynasty, Lianmu fell ill, and Wei Zhonggui cared for him without removing his official attire, showing his dedication. After a long time, Lianmu passed away, and Wei Zhonggui dismissed his wives and concubines, guarding the tomb on the left side. He believed in Buddhism and recited the Lotus Sutra. During the day, he carried soil to create a boundary, and at night, he focused on reciting scriptures. He was diligent and tireless in this practice, remaining for three years without returning home. Once, a tiger came to the wall at night, crouching and listening to the recitation, staying for a long time without leaving. Wei Zhonggui, not wishing for communication with wild animals, got up and left. The next morning, he saw a bird like a duck, carrying two carp, which it placed on the ground and then flew away. The officials, deeply moved, reported the discovery of the lingzhi mushrooms to the state and county. At that time, Xin was the governor, and Shen Yu was the deputy governor, and they all went to the tomb to investigate. Suddenly, a bird like a duck appeared, carrying two carp, which it placed on the ground and then flew away. The officials, deeply moved, reported the discovery of the lingzhi mushrooms to the imperial court, and an imperial decree was issued to honor the family with a tablet at the gate. [25]

    江都孙宝。本是北人。隋末徙居焉。少时死而身暖。经四十余曰乃苏。自说。初被收。诣官曹内。忽见其母在中受禁。宝见悲喜。母因自言。从死以来。久禁无进止。无由自诉。明旦。主司引宝见官。官谓。宝无罪放出。宝因请问曰。未审。生时罪福。定有报不。官曰定报。又问。兼作罪福。得相析除不。官曰得。宝曰。宝邻里人。某甲等。生平罪多福少。今见在外。宝母福多罪少。乃被久留。若有定报。何为如此。官召问主吏。吏曰无案。乃呼宝母勘问。知其福多罪少。责主吏。吏失案故。不知本案状轻重。官吏勘别簿。 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 江都 - Historical region in present-day Jiangsu Province, China.
    • 少時 - Refers to a young age or early years.
    • 身暖 - Literally means 'body warm,' used here to describe a state of being after death.
    • 經四十余曰 - 'For over forty days' (where '曰' is an archaic unit of time).
    • 自说 - Literally 'self-said,' meaning the person recounted their experience.
    • 初被收 - 'Initially was taken' or 'was first captured.'
    • 官曹内 - 'Within the office' or 'inside the government quarters.'
    • 主司 - Refers to the official in charge or the head of the bureau.
    • 宝无罪放出 - 'Stranger is released without blame.'
    • 未审 - 'Not yet clear' or 'not yet understood.'
    • 定有报不 - 'Is there a definite retribution?'
    • 兼作罪福 - 'Can both sins and merits be offset?'
    • 析除 - 'To separate and remove' or 'to cancel out.'
    • 勘问 - 'To investigate and question.'
    • 失案 - 'Lost case record' or 'missing case file.'
    • 簿 - Refers to a record or ledger.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 江都孙宝 - A historical figure from the Jiangdu region, known for his unusual experience.
    • 隋末 - Refers to the end of the Sui Dynasty (581-618 CE), a period of political instability and social upheaval.
    • 身暖 - A phenomenon where a corpse remains warm for an extended period, often associated with spiritual or supernatural events.
    • 主司 - Refers to officials in charge of legal or administrative matters.
    • 官 - Refers to a government official or magistrate.
    • 福 - Refers to good fortune or merit.
    • 罪 - Refers to sin or wrongdoing.
    • 报 - Refers to retribution or karmic consequence.
    • 析除 - The concept of balancing sins and merits, often found in Buddhist and Taoist thought.

    Proper Nouns

    • 江都 - A historical region in present-day Jiangsu Province, China.
    • 孙宝 - A historical figure from the Jiangdu region.
    • 隋末 - Refers to the end of the Sui Dynasty (581-618 CE).

    A Man from Jiangdu Named Sun Bao

    Sun Bao was originally from the north. At the end of the Sui Dynasty, he moved to Jiangdu. When he was young, he died, but his body remained warm. After over forty days, he revived. He recounted his experience: "Initially, I was captured and brought to the government quarters. Suddenly, I saw my mother inside, under arrest. I was filled with both sorrow and joy. My mother then spoke, saying that since her death, she had been detained for a long time without any resolution or way to appeal. The next morning, the official in charge brought me before the magistrate. The magistrate said, 'You are released without blame.' I then asked, 'Unclear, is there a definite retribution for sins and merits in life?' The magistrate replied, 'There is.' I asked again, 'Can sins and merits be offset against each other?' The magistrate said, 'They can.' I said, 'My neighbor, Mr. A, has more sins than merits in life. He is now outside. My mother has more merits than sins, yet she has been detained for so long. If there is a definite retribution, why is this so?' The magistrate summoned the head clerk to ask. The clerk said there was no record. The magistrate then called my mother for questioning and found that she indeed had more merits than sins. He blamed the clerk for losing the case file and not knowing the details of the case. The official then reviewed the records." [26]

    如所言。因命释放。配生乐堂母子。但出宝送生处。其乐堂者。如好宫殿。有大堂阁。众人男女。受乐其中。宝无复还意。但历观诸堂游戏而已。可月余曰。过见其伯父于路。责之曰。汝未合死。何不早还。宝曰。不愿还也。伯怒曰。人死各从本业受报。汝业恶。不得生乐堂。但以未合死。故得客游其中耳。若死官当收录。汝岂得见母耶。因以瓶水灌之。从顶至足。遍淋其体。唯臂间少有不遍。而水尽。指一空舍。令宝入中。既入而苏。其灌水不遍之处。肉遂糜烂堕落。至今见骨（临以贞观七年。奉使江东。甄陀为说此云尔。宝见在也）。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 如所言 - As he said or According to what he said, indicating a response to prior speech.
    • 因命释放 - Therefore, he ordered to be released, a formal expression of an official action.
    • 配生乐堂母子 - Matched with the mother and son of the Music Hall, a phrase that requires contextual explanation, as '乐堂' (Music Hall) likely refers to a specific place or realm in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 宝无复还意 - The treasure had no intention of returning, a figurative expression suggesting a person's unwillingness to return to their original state or life.
    • 但历观诸堂游戏而已 - But only to tour and play in the various halls, indicating a leisurely activity without deeper purpose.
    • 可月余曰 - About a month later, a vague time reference that can be translated flexibly.
    • 责之曰 - He scolded him, saying, a formal expression of rebuke.
    • 汝未合死 - You were not yet destined to die, a phrase that combines religious or karmic reasoning with the idea of a fixed fate.
    • 何不早还 - Why didn't you return early?, a rhetorical question expressing frustration or disappointment.
    • 伯怒曰 - The elder brother angrily said, indicating a strong emotional reaction.
    • 人死各从本业受报 - When people die, they receive their retribution based on their karma, a Buddhist concept of cause and effect in the afterlife.
    • 汝业恶 - Your karma is evil, a judgment based on Buddhist teachings.
    • 不得生乐堂 - You cannot be born into the Music Hall, a statement about the consequences of one's karma.
    • 但以未合死 - But because you were not yet destined to die, a justification for temporary existence in a certain state.
    • 故得客游其中耳 - Therefore, you could only be a guest there, indicating a temporary or inferior status.
    • 若死官当收录 - If you die, the officials will take you in, a reference to the afterlife and the role of officials in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 汝岂得见母耶 - How could you see your mother?, a rhetorical question expressing impossibility.
    • 因以瓶水灌之 - He then poured water from a bottle over him, a ritual or symbolic act.
    • 从顶至足 - From head to toe, a literal description of the action.
    • 遍淋其体 - Completely drenched his body, indicating thoroughness.
    • 唯臂间少有不遍 - Only a little bit on his arm was not fully drenched, a specific detail about the incomplete action.
    • 而水尽 - And the water ran out, indicating the end of the ritual.
    • 指一空舍 - He pointed to an empty room, a gesture that leads to a specific action.
    • 令宝入中 - And ordered the treasure to enter it, a formal command.
    • 既入而苏 - After entering, he revived, indicating a change in state.
    • 其灌水不遍之处 - The place where the water did not fully drench him, a reference to the specific area affected by the incomplete ritual.
    • 肉遂糜烂堕落 - The flesh then rotted and fell off, a vivid description of the physical consequence.
    • 至今见骨 - Until today, only bones can be seen, indicating a permanent change in state.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宝 - Treasure, a metaphorical term that likely refers to a person, possibly a monk or a spiritual figure.
    • 乐堂 - Music Hall, a term that likely refers to a specific realm in Buddhist cosmology, possibly a heaven or a place of reward.
    • 伯父 - Elder brother, a familial term that may also carry religious or spiritual connotations in the context of the story.
    • 灌水 - Pouring water, a ritual act that may have symbolic or purificatory significance in Buddhist or Taoist practices.
    • 空舍 - Empty room, a term that may refer to a specific place or state in the narrative, possibly a liminal or transitional space.
    • 贞观七年 - The seventh year of the Zhen Guan era, a specific historical period during the Tang Dynasty, indicating the time frame of the story.
    • 甄陀 - Zhen Tuo, a proper name that likely refers to a historical or literary figure involved in the narrative.

    Proper Nouns

    • 贞观七年 - The seventh year of the Zhen Guan era, a specific historical period during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 甄陀 - A proper name that likely refers to a historical or literary figure involved in the narrative.
    • 宝 - A metaphorical term that likely refers to a person, possibly a monk or a spiritual figure.
    • 乐堂 - A term that likely refers to a specific realm in Buddhist cosmology, possibly a heaven or a place of reward.
    • 伯父 - A familial term that may also carry religious or spiritual connotations in the context of the story.
    • 空舍 - A term that may refer to a specific place or state in the narrative, possibly a liminal or transitional space.

    According to what he said, he was ordered to be released and matched with the mother and son of the Music Hall. But the treasure was sent to a place of birth. The Music Hall was like a splendid palace, with grand halls and pavilions. Men and women enjoyed themselves there. The treasure had no intention of returning, but only toured and played in the various halls. About a month later, he encountered his elder brother on the road. The elder brother scolded him, saying, "You were not yet destined to die. Why didn't you return early?" The treasure replied, "I do not wish to return." The elder brother angrily said, "When people die, they receive their retribution based on their karma. Your karma is evil, and you cannot be born into the Music Hall. But because you were not yet destined to die, you could only be a guest there. If you die, the officials will take you in. How could you see your mother?" He then poured water from a bottle over the treasure, from head to toe, completely drenching his body. Only a little bit on his arm was not fully drenched, and the water ran out. He pointed to an empty room and ordered the treasure to enter it. After entering, he revived. The place where the water did not fully drench him, the flesh then rotted and fell off. Until today, only bones can be seen. (During the seventh year of the Zhen Guan era, I was sent as an envoy to the east, and Zhen Tuo told me this. The treasure is still alive.) [27]

    张亮为幽州都督府长吏。崇信佛。尝入寺。见佛像高与身翟贿。亮因别供养之。后在堂坐。两婢立侍。忽闻雷电。亮性畏雷。因心念佛等像。俄而霹雳。震其堂柱。侍婢一人走出及阶而死。有柱迸中亮额。而不甚痛。视之。才有赤痕。而柱木半裂堕地。如人折者。既而亮诣寺。像额后有大痕。如物击者。正亮痕处相当。亮及众惊嗟叹息（亮自为高昱说云尔。幽州人亦知）。
    　　监察御史范阳卢文厉。初为云阳尉。奉使荆州道覆囚。至江南。遇病甚笃。腹胀如石。饮食不下。医药不瘳。文厉自谓。必死。无生望。乃专心念观世音菩萨。经数曰。恍惚如睡。忽见一沙门来。自言是观世音菩萨。语文厉曰。汝能专念。故来救。今当为汝去腹中病。因手执一木把。用将其腹。腹中出秽物三升余。极臭恶。曰差矣。既而惊寤。身腹坦然。即食能起。而痼疾皆愈。至今甚强。宝与监同为御史。自说云尔。 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 崇信佛 - Devoutly believes in Buddhism; indicates a strong faith in Buddhism.
    • 专心念观世音菩萨 - Focus one's mind on the Bodhisattva Guanyin; indicates a devotional practice.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张亮 - Historical figure, Zhang Liang, who served as a high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 幽州 - Region in northern China, known for its strategic importance.
    • 都督府 - Military and administrative office during the Tang dynasty.
    • 佛 - Buddha, the central figure in Buddhism.
    • 观世音菩萨 - Bodhisattva Guanyin, a bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism known for compassion and healing.
    • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han, a ruler of the late Eastern Han period.
    • 荆州 - Region in central China, known for its strategic and economic importance.
    • 宝与监 - Historical figures, Bao and Jian, who served as censors during the Tang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张亮 - Zhang Liang, a historical figure.
    • 幽州 - Youzhou, a region in northern China.
    • 都督府 - Duzhufu, a military and administrative office.
    • 佛 - Buddha, the central figure in Buddhism.
    • 观世音菩萨 - Guanyin, a bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 荆州 - Jingzhou, a region in central China.
    • 宝与监 - Bao and Jian, historical figures.
    • 范阳 - Fanyang, a region in northern China.
    • 云阳 - Yunyang, a place in central China.
    • 江南 - Jiangnan, a region in southern China.

    Zhang Liang was the highest-ranking official in the Duzhufu (都督府) of Youzhou. He was devoutly devoted to Buddhism. Once, he entered a temple and saw a statue of the Buddha that was as tall as himself. Zhang Liang treated the statue with special reverence. Later, while sitting in the hall, two maids stood beside him. Suddenly, he heard thunder and lightning. Zhang Liang had a natural fear of thunder, so he focused his mind on the images of the Buddha. Soon after, a loud thunderclap shook the hall's pillars. One of the maids ran out and died at the steps. A piece of wood struck Zhang Liang's forehead, but it did not hurt much. When he looked, there was only a red mark. The wooden pillar split in half and fell to the ground, as if it had been broken by a person. Later, Zhang Liang went to the temple and found a large mark on the back of the Buddha's head, as if it had been struck by something. The mark matched the place where Zhang Liang's mark was. Zhang Liang and the others were shocked and sighed in amazement (Zhang Liang himself told this story to Gao Yu, and it was also known in Youzhou).

    The censor from Fanyang, Lu Wenli, was initially the magistrate of Yunyang. He served as an envoy to the road to Jingzhou, covering prisoners, and traveled to Jiangnan. He encountered a very serious illness, with his abdomen swelling like a stone. He could not eat or drink, and medicines did not cure him. Lu Wenli thought that death was inevitable and had no hope of survival. He then focused his mind on the Bodhisattva Guanyin. After several days, he felt drowsy and dreamy. Suddenly, he saw a monk come to him and said that he was the Bodhisattva Guanyin. The monk said to Lu Wenli, "Since you can focus your mind, I have come to save you. Now, I will remove the illness from your abdomen." The monk held a piece of wood and used it to treat his abdomen. Three liters or more of foul matter came out of his abdomen, which was extremely foul-smelling. He felt better. He woke up in shock and found that his body and abdomen felt normal. He could eat and get up, and all his chronic illnesses were cured. He is still very strong. Bao and Jian both served as censors. They said this themselves. [28]

    睦仁茜者。赵郡邯郸人也。少有经学。不信鬼神。常其欲试有无。就思人学之。十余年。不能得见。后徙家向县。于路见一人。如大官。衣冠甚伟。乘好马。从五十余人骑。视仁茜而不言。后数见之。帝如此。经十年。凡数十相见。后忽驻马。呼茜曰。比频见君。情相眷慕。题与君交游。茜即拜之。问公何人也。答吾是鬼耳。姓成。名景。本弘农。西晋时。为别驾。今任临胡国长吏。仁茜问其国何在。王何姓名。答曰。黄河已北。总为临胡国。国都在楼烦西北。州碛是也。其王。是故赵武灵王。今统此国。 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 欲试有无 - Means 'wants to test the existence or non-existence.'
    • 情相眷慕 - Means 'feel affectionate and admire each other.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 赵郡邯郸 - Refers to Zhaojun and Handan, both important places in ancient China.
    • 弘农 - Refers to Hongnong, an ancient region in China.
    • 西晋 - Refers to the Western Jin dynasty (265-316 AD).
    • 临胡国 - Refers to the country of Linhu, a fictional or historical region.
    • 黄河 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the most important rivers in China.
    • 楼烦 - Refers to Loufan, an ancient place in China.
    • 州碛 - Refers to Zhouqi, another ancient place in China.
    • 赵武灵王 - Refers to King Wuling of Zhao, a historical figure known for his military reforms.

    Proper Nouns

    • 睦仁茜 - The name of the main character.
    • 成景 - The name of the ghost.
    • 弘农 - The place of origin of the ghost.
    • 西晋 - The historical period.
    • 临胡国 - The name of the country ruled by the ghost.
    • 黄河 - The name of the river.
    • 楼烦 - The place of the capital.
    • 州碛 - The name of the state.
    • 赵武灵王 - The name of the king.

    Muxi Ren, a person from Zhaojun and Handan, was young and knowledgeable in the classics but did not believe in ghosts and spirits. He wanted to test the existence or non-existence of ghosts and went to seek knowledge from someone. After more than ten years, he could not see them. Later, he moved his family to a county and saw a person on the road who looked like a high-ranking official, dressed in very grand attire, riding a fine horse, accompanied by more than fifty riders. The person looked at Ren without speaking. After several times, the person still looked at Ren without speaking. The person was like this for ten years, meeting many times. Later, the person suddenly stopped the horse and called Ren, saying, "I have often seen you. I feel affectionate and admire each other. I want to be friends with you." Ren asked who the person was. The person replied, "I am a ghost. My surname is Cheng, and my name is Jing. Originally from Hongnong, during the Western Jin period, I served as an assistant governor. Now I serve as a high official in the country of Linhu." Ren asked where the country was and what the king's name was. The person replied, "North of the Yellow River, all is the country of Linhu. The capital is in the northwest of Loufan, it is the state of Zhouqi. The king is the former King Wuling of Zhao, now rules this country." [29]

    总受太山摄。每月各使上相朝于太山。是以数来过此。与君相遇也吾乃能有相益。令君预知祸难。而先避之。可免横害。唯死生之命。与大祸福之报。不能移动耳。茜从之。景因命其从骑。常掌事。以是赠之。遣随茜行。有事令先报之。即尔所不知。当来告我。于是便别。掌事恒随逐如从者。顷有所问。无不先知。时大业初。江陵岑之象为邯郸令。子文本。年未弱冠。之象请仁茜于家。教文本书。茜以此事告文本。仍谓曰。成长史谓曰。我有一事。羞君不得道。既与君交。亦不能不告君。鬼神道中亦有食。 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 总受太山摄 - Phrase indicating that the person is under the jurisdiction or authority of Mount Tai, a significant mountain in Chinese culture associated with divination and the afterlife.
    • 每月各使上相朝于太山 - Monthly ritual or meeting where officials or representatives pay homage to Mount Tai, a practice rooted in ancient Chinese religious and political traditions.
    • 与君相遇也 - Means 'and I met you,' indicating a personal encounter.
    • 吾乃能有相益 - Means 'I can indeed be beneficial to you,' suggesting that the speaker has something valuable to offer.
    • 令君预知祸难，而先避之 - Means 'to allow you to foresee disasters and avoid them beforehand,' indicating a warning or advice.
    • 可免横害 - Means 'you can avoid unexpected harm,' reinforcing the idea of foresight and prevention.
    • 唯死生之命，与大祸福之报，不能移动耳 - Means 'only matters of life and death, and great calamities and blessings, cannot be altered,' indicating that certain fates are beyond control.
    • 景因命其从骑，常掌事 - Means 'Jing then ordered his attendant to always manage affairs,' suggesting a practical arrangement.
    • 遣随茜行，有事令先报之 - Means 'sent to follow Xian, to report matters first,' indicating a role of communication and support.
    • 即尔所不知，当来告我 - Means 'if there is anything you don't know, come tell me,' suggesting a relationship of trust and guidance.
    • 掌事恒随逐如从者 - Means 'the attendant always followed like a retainer,' indicating a consistent and supportive presence.
    • 顷有所问，无不先知 - Means 'recently asked questions, nothing was unknown beforehand,' suggesting that the attendant was well-informed.
    • 大业初 - Refers to the early years of the Sui Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Yang (Yang Jian).
    • 江陵岑之象为邯郸令 - Means 'Cen Zhi Xiang of Jiangling was the magistrate of Handan,' providing a specific historical context.
    • 子文本，年未弱冠 - Means 'his son Wenben, who had not yet reached adulthood,' indicating the age of the son.
    • 之象请仁茜于家，教文本书 - Means 'Zhi Xiang invited Ren Xian to his home to teach Wenben,' describing a specific event.
    • 成长史谓曰 - Means 'the grown-up history says,' indicating a reference to historical records.
    • 我有一事，羞君不得道 - Means 'I have something to say, but it is embarrassing to you,' suggesting a sensitive topic.
    • 既与君交，亦不能不告君 - Means 'since we are friends, I cannot keep it from you,' indicating a sense of duty in friendship.
    • 鬼神道中亦有食 - Means 'even in the realm of spirits and deities, there is food,' suggesting a belief in the sustenance of spiritual beings.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Shandong Province, often associated with the emperor's ritual visits and the afterlife.
    • 大业初 - The early years of the Sui Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Yang (Yang Jian), a period of significant political and cultural development.
    • 江陵 - Jiangling, a city in Hubei Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 邯郸 - Handan, a city in Hebei Province, also historically significant.
    • 弱冠 - A term referring to a man who has reached the age of 20, traditionally the age of adulthood in ancient China.
    • 鬼神道 - The realm of spirits and deities, a concept deeply rooted in Chinese religious and philosophical traditions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太山 - Mount Tai
    • 大业初 - Early years of the Sui Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Yang (Yang Jian)
    • 江陵 - Jiangling
    • 邯郸 - Handan
    • 弱冠 - Age of 20, traditionally the age of adulthood
    • 鬼神道 - Realm of spirits and deities

    Under the jurisdiction of Mount Tai, each month the officials and representatives would ascend to pay homage at Mount Tai. Thus, I often pass through this place, and here I met you. I can indeed be beneficial to you. To allow you to foresee disasters and avoid them beforehand, you can avoid unexpected harm. Only matters of life and death, and great calamities and blessings, cannot be altered. Xian followed my advice. Jing then ordered his attendant to always manage affairs and to be a constant companion. He gave this attendant to follow Xian, to report matters first. If there is anything you don't know, come tell me. After this, they parted ways. The attendant always followed like a retainer, and recently asked questions, nothing was unknown beforehand. At the beginning of the Sui Dynasty, Cen Zhi Xiang of Jiangling was the magistrate of Handan. His son Wenben, who had not yet reached adulthood, Zhi Xiang invited Ren Xian to his home to teach Wenben. Xian recounted this matter to Wenben, saying, "The grown-up history says, 'I have something to say, but it is embarrassing to you.' Since we are friends, I cannot keep it from you." Even in the realm of spirits and deities, there is food. [30]

    然不能饱。苦饥。若得人食。便得一年饱。众鬼多偷窃人食。我既贵重。不能偷之。从君请一餐。茜既告文本。文本即为具馔。备设珍羞。茜曰。鬼不欲入人屋。可于外边。张幕设席。陈酒食于上。文本如其言。至时。仁茜见景两客来坐。从百余骑。既坐文本向席再拜。谢以食之不精。亦传意。辞谢。初文本将设食。仁茜请有金帛以赎之。文本问是何等物。茜云。鬼所用物。皆与人异。唯黄金及绢。为得通用。然亦不如假者。以黄色涂大锡作金。以纸为绢帛。最为贵上。文本如言作之。及景食毕。 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 然不能饱，苦饥 - Common expression meaning 'yet cannot be satisfied, suffering from hunger' or 'still cannot be full, tormented by hunger.'
    • 若得人食，便得一年饱 - Conditional statement meaning 'if one could get human food, then one could be full for a year.'
    • 众鬼多偷窃人食 - Means 'many ghosts often steal human food.'
    • 我既贵重，不能偷之 - Ambiguous phrase meaning 'I, being valuable, cannot steal it' or 'I, being precious, cannot steal it.'
    • 从君请一餐 - Means 'I ask you for a meal.'
    • 茜既告文本，文本即为具馔 - Ambiguous phrase meaning 'Xian informed Wenben, and Wenben then prepared the meal' or 'Xian informed Wenben, and Wenben immediately prepared the meal.'
    • 备设珍羞 - Means 'prepared exquisite delicacies.'
