    • 后行，渐瘦恶，或着枷锁，或但去巾带 - '后行' means 'those in the back,' '渐瘦恶' means 'gradually becoming thinner and uglier,' '或着枷锁' means 'some wearing shackles,' and '或但去巾带' means 'others just without headbands and belts.' This describes the prisoners in the back row as thinner, uglier, and either shackled or only without headbands and belts.
    • 皆行连袂 - '皆行' means 'all walk,' and '连袂' means 'with linked sleeves.' This describes the prisoners walking with their sleeves linked together.
    • 严兵守之 - '严兵' means 'strict soldiers,' and '守之' means 'guarding them.' This describes the prisoners being guarded by strict soldiers.
    • 师辨至，配入第三行东头第三立 - '师辨至' means 'when Vang Shiban arrived,' '配入' means 'assigned to,' '第三行' means 'the third row,' and '东头第三立' means 'the third position from the east.' This describes Vang Shiban being assigned to the third position in the third row from the east.
    • 亦去巾带，连袂 - '亦' means 'also,' '去巾带' means 'without headbands and belts,' and '连袂' means 'with linked sleeves.' This describes Vang Shiban also walking with linked sleeves and without headbands and belts.
    • 忧惧，专心念佛 - '忧惧' means 'fearful,' and '专心念佛' means 'focusing on reciting Buddha's name.' This describes Vang Shiban's state of mind and his religious practice.
    • 忽见生平相识僧来 - '忽见' means 'suddenly saw,' '生平相识' means 'acquaintance from before,' and '僧' means 'monk.' This describes Vang Shiban suddenly seeing a monk he knew from before.
    • 入兵围行内 - '入' means 'enter,' '兵围行内' means 'into the line surrounded by soldiers.' This describes the monk entering the line of prisoners surrounded by soldiers.
    • 兵莫之止 - '兵' means 'soldiers,' '莫之止' means 'did not stop him.' This describes the soldiers not stopping the monk.
    • 因至辨所，谓曰 - '因至' means 'then went to,' '辨所' means 'Vang Shiban's place,' and '谓曰' means 'said to him.' This describes the monk going to Vang Shiban and speaking to him.
    • 平生不修福，今忽至此如何 - '平生' means 'in life,' '不修福' means 'did not cultivate merit,' '今忽至此' means 'now suddenly here,' and '如何' means 'what will you do?' This describes the monk asking Vang Shiban about his lack of merit and what he will do now.
    • 求哀请救 - '求哀' means 'begging for mercy,' and '请救' means 'asking for help.' This describes Vang Shiban begging for mercy and asking for help.
    • 吾今救汝得出，可持戒也 - '吾今' means 'I will now,' '救汝得出' means 'save you out,' '可持戒也' means 'you can keep precepts.' This describes the monk offering to save Vang Shiban if he keeps the precepts.
    • 许诺 - '许诺' means 'agreed.' This describes Vang Shiban agreeing to the monk's offer.
    • 须臾，吏引入诸囚，至官前以次许问至辨 - '须臾' means 'in a moment,' '吏' means 'official,' '引入' means 'led in,' '诸囚' means 'all prisoners,' '至官前' means 'to the official's presence,' '以次许问' means 'in order to ask,' and '至辨' means 'up to Vang Shiban.' This describes an official leading in all the prisoners and asking each one in order until Vang Shiban.
    • 因见，向者僧，为官说其福业 - '因见' means 'then saw,' '向者僧' means 'the monk from before,' '为官' means 'for the official,' '说其福业' means 'spoke of his merits.' This describes the monk seeing Vang Shiban and speaking to the official about his merits.
    • 官曰放之 - '官曰' means 'the official said,' and '放之' means 'let him go.' This describes the official ordering Vang Shiban to be released.
    • 僧因引辨出至门外，为授五戒，用瓶水灌其额，谓曰，曰西当活 - '僧因引' means 'the monk then led,' '辨出至门外' means 'Vang Shiban out to the door,' '为授五戒' means 'gave him the Five Precepts,' '用瓶水灌其额' means 'poured water from a bottle over his forehead,' '谓曰' means 'said to him,' and '曰西当活' means 'you will live in the west.' This describes the monk leading Vang Shiban out to the door, giving him the Five Precepts, pouring water over his forehead, and telling him he will live in the west.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东宫 - The Eastern Palace, also known as the Imperial Palace, was the residence of the heir apparent to the throne in ancient China.
    • 右监门兵曹参军 - A specific military position within the imperial court, indicating the hierarchical structure of the government.
    • 弱冠 - The age of 20, traditionally the age of adulthood in ancient China.
    • 异病死 - Refers to a death caused by an unusual or supernatural illness, which could be interpreted as a spiritual or divine intervention.
    • 三日而苏 - Reviving after three days, which is a common motif in stories of near-death experiences and supernatural events.
    • 官府大门 - The main gate of an official government building, symbolizing the authority and power of the state.
    • 囚 - Prisoners, often used to describe those who are under legal or moral judgment.
    • 北面立 - Facing north, which was a common practice in ancient China to show respect to authority figures.
    • 好衣服 - Good clothes, indicating social status and wealth.
    • 枷锁 - Shackles, used to restrain prisoners.
    • 巾带 - Headbands and belts, which were common accessories in ancient China.
    • 连袂 - Linked sleeves, a form of restraint used to prevent prisoners from escaping.
    • 严兵 - Strict soldiers, emphasizing the severity of the situation.
    • 念佛 - Reciting Buddha's name, a common Buddhist practice for seeking spiritual protection and merit.
    • 僧 - Monk, a religious figure in Buddhism.
    • 修福 - Cultivating merit, a key concept in Buddhism and other religions, referring to good deeds and virtuous actions.
    • 持戒 - Keeping precepts, which are moral guidelines in Buddhism.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts, which are fundamental ethical guidelines in Buddhism.
    • 瓶水灌其额 - Pouring water from a bottle over his forehead, a ritual act in Buddhism.

    A Military Officer of the Right Gate of the Eastern Palace named Zheng Shiban. When he was still young, not yet having reached the age of twenty, he died from a strange illness. After three days, he revived. He recounted that several people were taken and were about to enter the main gate of an official government building. There were over a hundred prisoners there, all standing in rows facing north. There were a total of six rows. Those in the front row were fat and white in appearance, dressed well, and resembled nobles. Those in the back rows gradually became thinner and uglier, some wearing shackles, others just without headbands and belts. All were walking with linked sleeves, guarded by strict soldiers. When Zheng Shiban arrived, he was assigned to the third position from the east in the third row. He too was without headbands and belts, walking with linked sleeves. Feeling fearful, he focused on reciting Buddha's name. Suddenly, he saw a monk he knew from before come in. The monk entered the line of prisoners surrounded by soldiers, and the soldiers did not stop him. The monk then went to Zheng Shiban and said to him, "In your life, you did not cultivate merit. Now, how will you handle being here?" Zheng Shiban begged for mercy and asked for help. The monk said, "I will now save you if you keep the precepts." Zheng Shiban agreed. In a moment, an official led in all the prisoners and asked each one in order until reaching Zheng Shiban. The monk then saw Zheng Shiban and spoke to the official about his merits. The official said to release him. The monk then led Zheng Shiban out to the door, gave him the Five Precepts, poured water from a bottle over his forehead, and said to him, "You will live in the west." [46]

    又以黄帔一枚。与辨曰。披此至家。置净处也。仍示归路。辨披之而归。至家叠帔置床角上。既而目开身动。家人惊散。谓欲起尸。唯母不去。问曰。汝治耶。辨曰。曰西当活辨意。时疑曰午问母。母曰。夜半。方知死生返昼夜也。既至曰西。能食而愈。犹见帔在床头。及辨能起。帔形渐灭而尚有光。七曰乃尽。辨遂持五戒。后数年。有人劝食猪肉。辨不得已。食一脔。是夜梦。已化为罗刹。爪齿各长数尺。捉生猪食之。既晓觉口腥唾出。而使人视之。满口中。尽是凝血。辨惊惧不敢食肉。又数年。娶妻。妻逼之食食乃无验。然而辨自六年来。鼻常有大疮洪烂然自不能愈。或恐以破戒之故也（临昔与辨同直东宫。见其自说云尔）。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 黄帔一枚 - A yellow shawl, which may have symbolic or ritual significance.
    • 披此至家，置净处也 - Wear this to get home and place it in a clean place, indicating a specific ritual or command.
    • 辨披之而归 - 辨 wore it and returned home, with '辨' likely referring to a person or spirit.
    • 辨意 - 辨's intention or meaning, which may refer to a spiritual or ritual purpose.
    • 曰西当活辨意 - On the west side, 辨's intention will be fulfilled or 辨 will be revived.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts in Buddhism: not to kill, steal, engage in sexual misconduct, lie, or consume intoxicants.
    • 罗刹 - A type of demon or evil spirit in Buddhist and Hindu mythology.
    • 凝血 - Clotted blood, which may be a metaphor for the consequences of breaking a religious vow.
    • 鼻常有大疮洪烂然自不能愈 - A large sore on the nose that is swollen and does not heal, which may be interpreted as a physical manifestation of guilt or divine punishment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄帔 - A yellow shawl, which may have been used in rituals or as a protective charm.
    • 辨 - The name or title of a person or spirit, possibly a deity or a medium.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts, which are fundamental ethical guidelines in Buddhism.
    • 罗刹 - A demon or evil spirit, often associated with causing harm or suffering.
    • 临昔 - A person or figure who is mentioned as a witness to the events described.

    Proper Nouns

    • 黄帔 - A yellow shawl, which may have ritual or symbolic significance.
    • 辨 - The name or title of a person or spirit.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts in Buddhism.
    • 罗刹 - A demon or evil spirit.
    • 临昔 - A person or figure who is mentioned as a witness.

    A yellow shawl was given to Bian, and he was told, "Wear this to get home and place it in a clean place. I will also show you the way back." Bian wore the shawl and returned home. Upon arriving, he folded the shawl and placed it at the corner of the bed. Soon after, his eyes opened and his body moved. The family members were startled and scattered, thinking he was coming back to life. Only his mother stayed, asking, "Are you well?" Bian replied, "On the west side, my intention will be fulfilled." At that time, there was doubt about the timing, and his mother asked again. She said, "It was midnight." It was then known that the reversal of life and death occurred during the night. When Bian reached the west side, he could eat and recovered. He still saw the shawl at the head of the bed. When Bian could stand up, the shape of the shawl gradually faded but still had a glow. After seven days, it completely disappeared. Bian then kept the Five Precepts. Several years later, someone persuaded him to eat pork. Bian, unable to refuse, ate a small piece. That night, he had a dream in which he had transformed into a Rakshasa, with claws and teeth each several feet long, catching and eating a live pig. In the morning, he woke up feeling a fishy taste in his mouth and spitting out blood. When others looked inside, his mouth was full of clotted blood. Bian was terrified and dared not eat meat anymore. Another few years passed, and he married. His wife forced him to eat meat, but it had no effect. However, for the past six years, Bian has had a large, swollen sore on his nose that has not healed. It is feared that this might be due to breaking his precepts. (Linxie and Bian served together in the Eastern Palace and Bian told him himself.) [47]

    陈公太夫人豆卢氏。芮公宽之姊也。夫人信福。诵金刚般若经。未尽卷。一纸许。久而不彻。后一曰昏时。苦头痛。四体不安。夜卧愈甚。夫人自念。傥死遂不得终经。欲起诵之。而堂烛已灭。夫人因起。命婢燃烛。须臾婢还。厨中无火。夫人命开门。于人家访取之。又无火。夫人深益叹恨。忽见庭中有燃火烛。上阶来入堂内。直至床前。去地三尺许。而无人执。光明若昼。夫人惊喜。头痛亦愈。即取经诵之。有顷。家人钻燧得火。燃烛入堂中。烛光即灭。便以此夜。诵竟之。自此曰诵五遍以为常。后芮公将死。夫人往视。公谓夫人曰。吾姊以诵经之福。寿百岁。好处生。夫人至今尚康。年八十年矣。夫人自向临嫂说之云尔。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 诵经之福 - Blessings from reciting the sutra.
    • 光明若昼 - As bright as day.
    • 四体不安 - All four limbs are uneasy; feeling unwell.
    • 夜卧愈甚 - Becomes more severe at night.
    • 傥死遂不得终经 - If I die, I will not be able to finish the sutra.
    • 命婢燃烛 - Ordered the maid to light a candle.
    • 厨中无火 - There is no fire in the kitchen.
    • 命开门 - Ordered to open the door.
    • 于人家访取之 - Went to someone's house to ask for fire.
    • 深益叹恨 - Deeply sighed with regret.
    • 忽见庭中有燃火烛 - Suddenly saw a burning candle in the courtyard.
    • 上阶来入堂内 - Came up the steps and entered the hall.
    • 直至床前 - Up to the bed.
    • 去地三尺许 - About three feet off the ground.
    • 而无人执 - But no one was holding it.
    • 夫人惊喜 - The lady was surprised and delighted.
    • 头痛亦愈 - The headache also subsided.
    • 即取经诵之 - Immediately took the sutra and recited it.
    • 有顷 - After a short while.
    • 家人钻燧得火 - The family obtained fire by drilling wood.
    • 诵竟之 - Finished reciting it.
    • 自此曰诵五遍以为常 - From this day on, she recited it five times daily as a routine.
    • 后芮公将死 - After Rui Gong was about to die.
    • 公谓夫人曰 - He said to the lady.
    • 吾姊以诵经之福 - My sister, due to the blessings from reciting the sutra.
    • 寿百岁 - Lived to be a hundred years old.
    • 好处生 - Was born in a good place; was born into a favorable situation.
    • 夫人至今尚康 - The lady is still healthy.
    • 年八十年矣 - Is already eighty years old.
    • 夫人自向临嫂说之云尔 - The lady herself told her younger sister-in-law about this.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • The Diamond Sutra, a key text in Mahayana Buddhism, emphasizing the concept of emptiness and wisdom.
    • Reciting Buddhist sutras, a common practice in Chinese Buddhism for spiritual merit.
    • The ancient method of obtaining fire by drilling wood, a common practice in pre-modern China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陈公 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 芮公宽 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or noble.
    • 金刚般若经 - The Diamond Sutra, a key text in Mahayana Buddhism.

    The Lady Doulu, the mother of Chen Gong, was the sister of Rui Gong Kuan. She was devoted to good fortune and recited the Diamond Sutra. She had not yet finished reading the entire scroll, only about one sheet, and had been unable to complete it for a long time. Later, one evening, she suffered from a headache and felt uneasy all over. Her condition worsened at night when she lay down to sleep. The lady thought to herself, "If I die, I will not be able to finish the sutra." She wanted to get up and recite it, but the candle in the hall had already gone out. The lady then ordered the maid to light a candle. After a short while, the maid returned and reported that there was no fire in the kitchen. The lady ordered to open the door and went to someone's house to ask for fire, but there was still no fire. The lady deeply sighed with regret. Suddenly, she saw a burning candle in the courtyard. It came up the steps and entered the hall, reaching the bed, about three feet off the ground, but no one was holding it. It was as bright as day. The lady was surprised and delighted, and her headache also subsided. She immediately took the sutra and recited it. After a short while, the family obtained fire by drilling wood and lit a candle in the hall. The candlelight then went out. From this day on, she recited it five times daily as a routine. After Rui Gong was about to die, the lady went to see him. He said to her, "My sister, due to the blessings from reciting the sutra, lived to be a hundred years old and was born in a good place." The lady is still healthy and is already eighty years old. The lady herself told her younger sister-in-law about this. [48]

    左监门校尉。凭翊李山龙。以武德中。暴病亡。而心上不冷如掌许。家人未忍殡敛。至七曰而苏。自说云。当死时。被锁宫收录。至一官曹。厅事甚宏然。其庭亦广大。庭内有囚数千人。或枷锁。或杻械。皆北面立。满庭中。吏将山龙至厅事。一大官坐高床座。侍卫如王者。山龙问吏。此何官。吏曰。是王也。山龙前至阶下。王问曰。汝生平作何福业。山龙对曰。乡人每设斋讲。恒施物同之。王曰。汝身作何善业。山龙曰。诵法华经两卷。王曰。大善。可升阶。既升。厅上东北间有一高座。如讲座者。王指坐。 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 左监门校尉 - A military rank in the Tang Dynasty, translating to 'Left Gatekeeper General'
    • 暴病亡 - A sudden illness leading to death
    • 心上不冷如掌许 - The heart area remaining warm despite the body being cold, indicating a unique post-death phenomenon
    • 殡敛 - The process of preparing the body for burial
    • 苏 - Reviving or coming back to life
    • 当死时 - At the moment of death
    • 被锁宫收录 - Being captured by palace guards
    • 一大官坐高床座 - A high-ranking official sitting on an elevated seat
    • 侍卫如王者 - Attendants resembling those of a king
    • 生平作何福业 - What good deeds have been done in life
    • 乡人每设斋讲 - The locals often held gatherings for religious teachings
    • 恒施物同之 - Always contributed material support to these gatherings
    • 诵法华经两卷 - Recited two volumes of the Lotus Sutra
    • 大善 - Great virtue
    • 升阶 - Ascend the steps
    • 高座 - An elevated seat, typically used for lecturing or teaching
    • 王指坐 - The king pointed to the seat, inviting the person to sit

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武德中 - Refers to the period of Emperor Taizong of Tang's reign, known as the Wu De era
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a significant Buddhist scripture
    • 暴病亡 - Sudden death, often associated with divine intervention or supernatural phenomena
    • 心上不冷如掌许 - A miraculous or supernatural event, as it defies the natural process of death

    Proper Nouns

    • 武德 - The era name of Emperor Taizong of Tang
    • 左监门校尉 - A specific military rank in the Tang Dynasty
    • 凭翊 - The name of the individual's hometown
    • 李山龙 - The name of the individual
    • 法华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a significant Buddhist scripture
    • 王 - The high-ranking official encountered in the supernatural experience

    Left Gatekeeper General Li Shanlong from Pingyi. In the Wu De era, he suddenly fell ill and died. However, his heart area remained warm as if the size of a palm. His family hesitated to prepare the body for burial. On the seventh day, he revived and recounted his experience: "When I was dying, I was captured by palace guards and taken to an official office. The hall was very grand, and the courtyard was also spacious. Inside the courtyard, there were thousands of prisoners, some in shackles and others in chains, all facing north standing in the courtyard. The officials led me to the hall. A high-ranking official sat on an elevated seat, surrounded by attendants like a king. I asked the official, 'What kind of official is this?' The official replied, 'He is a king.' I approached the steps and the king asked, 'What good deeds have you done in your life?' I answered, 'The locals often held gatherings for religious teachings, and I always contributed material support to these gatherings.' The king then asked, 'What good deeds have you personally done?' I said, 'I recited two volumes of the Lotus Sutra.' The king said, 'Great virtue. You may ascend the steps.' After ascending, there was an elevated seat in the northeast corner of the hall, like a lectern. The king pointed to the seat." [49]

    谓山龙曰。可升此座诵经。山龙奉命。至座侧。王即起立曰。请法师升座。山龙升座讫。王乃向之而坐。山龙诵曰。妙法莲华经序品第一。王曰。请法师止。山龙下座。复立阶下。顾庭内。向囚已尽。无一人在者。谓山龙曰。君诵经之福。非唯自利。乃令庭内众囚。皆如闻经获免。岂不善哉。今放君还去。山龙拜辞。行数十步。王复呼还。谓吏曰。可将此。历观诸狱。吏即将山龙。东行百余步。见一铁城。甚广大。上有屋覆。其城傍。多有小窗。或大如小盆。或如盂碗。见诸人男女从地飞入窗中。即不复出。山龙怪问吏。吏曰。 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 謂山龍曰 - A direct speech marker meaning 'said to Shanlong.'
    • 可升此座诵经 - Means 'you may ascend this seat to recite the sutra.'
    • 请法师升座 - Means 'please ascend the seat, Venerable Master.'
    • 山龙升座讫 - Means 'Shanlong ascended the seat and finished.'
    • 请法师止 - Means 'please stop, Venerable Master.'
    • 顾庭内 - Means 'look around the courtyard.'
    • 无一人在者 - Means 'there is no one left.'
    • 君诵经之福 - Means 'the merit of your reciting the sutra.'
    • 非唯自利 - Means 'not only for your own benefit.'
    • 乃令庭内众囚，皆如闻经获免 - Means 'but also caused all the prisoners in the courtyard to gain release as if they had heard the sutra.'
    • 岂不善哉 - Means 'how good it is!'
    • 今放君还去 - Means 'now I will let you return.'
    • 拜辞 - Means 'to bow and take leave.'
    • 行数十步 - Means 'walked several steps.'
    • 复呼还 - Means 'called back again.'
    • 可将此历观诸狱 - Means 'take this person to view all the prisons.'
    • 东行百余步 - Means 'walked more than a hundred steps east.'
    • 见一铁城 - Means 'saw an iron city.'
    • 甚广大 - Means 'very large and spacious.'
    • 上有屋覆 - Means 'with a roof above it.'
    • 其城傍多有小窗 - Means 'there were many small windows on the side of the city.'
    • 或大如小盆，或如盂碗 - Means 'some as large as small bowls, others as large as cups or bowls.'
    • 见诸人男女从地飞入窗中 - Means 'saw men and women flying into the windows from the ground.'
    • 即不复出 - Means 'and did not come out again.'
    • 山龙怪问吏 - Means 'Shanlong asked the official in surprise.'
    • 吏曰 - Means 'the official said.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 山龙 - A character in the story, likely a monk or a religious figure.
    • 妙法莲华经 - The Lotus Sutra, a major Buddhist scripture.
    • 序品第一 - The first chapter of the Lotus Sutra.
    • 囚 - Prisoners, often used in Buddhist stories to illustrate the suffering of beings in samsara.
    • 庭内 - The courtyard, often a place of gathering or performance in traditional settings.
    • 铁城 - An iron city, a metaphorical or symbolic place in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 小盆，盂碗 - Small bowls and cups, common household items used in various contexts.
    • 从地飞入窗中 - A metaphorical description of beings entering a different realm or state of existence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 山龙 - Shanlong, a character in the story.
    • 妙法莲华经 - The Lotus Sutra.
    • 序品第一 - The first chapter of the Lotus Sutra.
    • 王 - The king or ruler.
    • 吏 - The official.

    The king said to Shanlong, "You may ascend this seat to recite the sutra." Shanlong, following the command, approached the side of the seat. The king immediately stood up and said, "Please ascend the seat, Venerable Master." Shanlong ascended the seat and finished. The king then sat facing him. Shanlong began to recite, "The First Chapter of the Lotus Sutra." The king said, "Please stop, Venerable Master."

    Shanlong descended from the seat and stood at the foot of the steps. He looked around the courtyard and saw that all the prisoners had disappeared, leaving no one behind. The king said to Shanlong, "The merit of your reciting the sutra is not only for your own benefit but also caused all the prisoners in the courtyard to gain release as if they had heard the sutra. How good it is! Now I will let you return." Shanlong bowed and took leave, walking several steps. The king called him back and said to the official, "Take this person to view all the prisons."

    The official led Shanlong more than a hundred steps east, where they saw an iron city, very large and spacious, with a roof above it. There were many small windows on the side of the city, some as large as small bowls, others as large as cups or bowls. Shanlong saw men and women flying into the windows from the ground and did not come out again. Shanlong asked the official in surprise. The official said, "These are the realms of the hungry ghosts and the hell beings. They are born there due to their past misdeeds and remain trapped there, unable to escape. The recitation of the sutra by a virtuous person like you has the power to liberate them, even though they are not present here."

    Shanlong was deeply moved by this revelation and thanked the king for the opportunity to perform such a meritorious act. The king smiled and said, "The power of the Dharma is indeed wondrous. Continue to recite the sutra and spread the teachings, and you will bring great benefit to all beings."

    With these words, the king released Shanlong, who returned to his monastery, his heart filled with joy and a renewed sense of purpose. [50]

    此是大狱。狱中多有分隔。罪罚各异。此诸人者。各随本业。赴狱受罪耳。山龙闻之悲惧。称南无佛。请吏求出。至院门。见一大镬大猛汤。傍有二人坐睡。山龙问之。二人曰。我等罪报。入此镬汤。蒙贤者称南无佛。故狱中罪人。皆得一曰休息。疲睡耳。山龙又称南无佛。吏谓山龙曰。这府数移改。今王放君去。君可白王请抄。若不尔恐他官不知。复追录君。山龙即谒王请抄。王命纸书一行字。付吏曰。为取五道等署。吏受命。将山龙更历两曹。各厅事侍卫。亦如此。王之遣吏。皆取其官署。各书一行讫。付山龙。龙持出至门。 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 此是大狱 - Literal phrase meaning 'this is a great prison,' used to introduce the setting.
    • 狱中多有分隔 - Means 'the prison has many divisions,' indicating the structure of the prison.
    • 罪罚各异 - Means 'punishments vary,' highlighting the diversity of punishments.
    • 各随本业 - Means 'each follows their own profession,' suggesting that the prisoners are engaged in activities related to their past lives.
    • 赴狱受罪耳 - Means 'to go to prison to suffer punishment,' emphasizing the fate of the prisoners.
    • 山龙闻之悲惧 - Means 'Mountain Dragon heard it and was filled with fear and sorrow,' expressing the emotional reaction of the protagonist.
    • 称南无佛 - Means 'to chant 'Namo Buddha,' indicating a religious act.
    • 请吏求出 - Means 'to ask the official to let him out,' showing the request made by the protagonist.
    • 至院门 - Means 'to the courtyard gate,' indicating the location.
    • 见一大镬大猛汤 - Means 'to see a large cauldron of boiling hot soup,' describing a scene in the prison.
    • 傍有二人坐睡 - Means 'there were two people sitting and sleeping nearby,' describing the scene further.
    • 我等罪报 - Means 'our punishment,' indicating the reason for their suffering.
    • 蒙贤者称南无佛 - Means 'we are blessed by the virtuous one chanting 'Namo Buddha,' expressing gratitude.
    • 故狱中罪人 - Means 'therefore, the prisoners in the prison,' indicating the effect of the chant.
    • 皆得一日休息 - Means 'all get one day of rest,' describing the result of the chant.
    • 疲睡耳 - Means 'tired and sleeping,' indicating the state of the prisoners.
    • 这府数移改 - Means 'this office frequently changes,' indicating the instability of the prison.
    • 今王放君去 - Means 'now the king lets you go,' indicating the decision of the king.
    • 君可白王请抄 - Means 'you can report to the king and request a record,' indicating the advice given to the protagonist.
    • 若不尔恐他官不知 - Means 'if not, other officials might not know,' indicating the potential consequence.
    • 复追录君 - Means 'they will pursue and record you again,' indicating the risk.
    • 山龙即谒王请抄 - Means 'Mountain Dragon immediately went to the king and requested a record,' indicating the action taken by the protagonist.
    • 王命纸书一行字 - Means 'the king ordered paper and wrote one line of text,' indicating the king's action.
    • 付吏曰 - Means 'and gave it to the official, saying,' indicating the communication.
    • 为取五道等署 - Means 'to obtain signatures from the Five Departments,' indicating the purpose.
    • 吏受命 - Means 'the official received the order,' indicating the official's action.
    • 将山龙更历两曹 - Means 'the official took Mountain Dragon through two departments,' indicating the process.
    • 各厅事侍卫 - Means 'various hall attendants,' indicating the people involved.
    • 亦如此 - Means 'it was the same,' indicating consistency.
    • 王之遣吏 - Means 'the king sent the official,' indicating the king's action.
    • 皆取其官署 - Means 'to obtain their official seals,' indicating the purpose.
    • 各书一行讫 - Means 'each wrote one line and finished,' indicating the completion.
    • 付山龙 - Means 'and gave it to Mountain Dragon,' indicating the final step.
    • 龙持出至门 - Means 'Dragon held it and went out to the gate,' indicating the final action.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 大狱 - Refers to a significant prison, possibly a metaphor for a place of suffering or punishment.
    • 南无佛 - A Buddhist chant meaning 'Homage to the Buddha,' indicating a religious practice.
    • 五道等署 - Refers to the Five Departments, which were administrative divisions in ancient China.
    • 王 - Refers to the king, indicating the ruler of the kingdom.
    • 吏 - Refers to officials, indicating the administrative personnel.
    • 山龙 - The name of the protagonist, indicating a character in the story.
    • 镬 - A large cooking pot, often used in ancient times for boiling.
    • 猛汤 - Boiling hot soup, indicating a severe form of punishment.
    • 贤者 - A virtuous person, indicating someone of high moral character.
    • 抄 - A record or document, indicating an official document.
    • 五道 - The Five Departments, indicating the administrative divisions.
    • 官署 - Official seals, indicating the authority of the officials.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This opening sequence introduces the setting and the nature of the prison.
    • This clause describes the prisoners and their fate.

    This is a great prison. The prison has many divisions, and punishments vary. These people each follow their own profession, going to prison to suffer their punishment. Mountain Dragon heard of this and was filled with fear and sorrow. He chanted "Namo Buddha" and asked the official to let him out. Upon reaching the courtyard gate, he saw a large cauldron of boiling hot soup. Nearby, two people were sitting and sleeping. Mountain Dragon asked them, and they replied, "Our punishment is to enter this cauldron of boiling soup. We are blessed by the virtuous one chanting 'Namo Buddha,' so all the prisoners in the prison get one day of rest, tired and sleeping." Mountain Dragon chanted "Namo Buddha" again. The official told Mountain Dragon, "This office frequently changes. Now the king lets you go. You can report to the king and request a record. If not, other officials might not know and will pursue and record you again." Mountain Dragon immediately went to the king and requested a record. The king ordered paper and wrote one line of text, giving it to the official and saying, "To obtain signatures from the Five Departments." The official received the order and took Mountain Dragon through two departments, with various hall attendants present, and it was the same as before. The king sent the official to obtain their official seals, and each wrote one line and finished, giving it to Mountain Dragon. Dragon held it and went out to the gate. [51]

    有三人。语山龙曰。王放君去。可不少多乞遣我等。山龙未言。吏谓山龙曰。王放君。不由彼。三人者。是前收录君。使人。一是绳主。当以赤绳缚君者。一是棒主。击君头者。一是袋主。吸君气者。见君得还。故乞物耳。山龙惶惧。谢三人曰。愚不识公。请至家备物。但不知于何处送之。三人曰。于水边若树下。山龙许诺。辞吏。归家。见正哭经营殡具。山龙入至尸傍。即苏。后曰剪纸作钱帛。并酒食。自送于水边烧之。忽见三人来谢曰。蒙君不失信。重相赠遗。愧荷。言毕不见山龙自向总持寺主僧说之。转向临说之。云尔。 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有三人 - A straightforward phrase indicating the presence of three people.
    • 王放君去 - The king is letting you go, where '放君去' is a polite way of saying someone is being released or dismissed.
    • 可不少多乞遣我等 - Can you not ask for a little more to send us? This is a humble request for something in return.
    • 吏谓山龙曰 - The official said to Shan Long, where '吏' refers to an official or bureaucrat.
    • 不由彼 - Not through him/her, indicating that the king's decision is independent of the person being addressed.
    • 三人者，是前收录君，使人 - These three are those who previously captured you and were your handlers, where '收录' means to capture or detain, and '使人' refers to servants or attendants.
    • 一是绳主，当以赤绳缚君者 - One is the rope master, who should have tied you with a red rope, where '绳主' refers to someone responsible for binding or restraining.
    • 一是棒主，击君头者 - One is the stick master, who hit your head, where '棒主' refers to someone responsible for striking or beating.
    • 一是袋主，吸君气者 - One is the bag master, who sucked your breath, where '袋主' refers to someone responsible for restraining or capturing breath.
    • 见君得还，故乞物耳 - Seeing that you have returned, they are asking for something in return, where '乞物' means to ask for an item or favor.
    • 山龙惶惧 - Shan Long was terrified, where '惶惧' conveys a sense of fear and anxiety.
    • 谢三人曰 - He thanked the three, where '谢' means to thank or express gratitude.
    • 愚不识公 - I am foolish and do not recognize you, where '愚' means foolish, and '不识公' means not recognizing the person.
    • 请至家备物 - Please come to my home to prepare things, where '备物' means to prepare items or offerings.
    • 但不知于何处送之 - But I don't know where to send them, where '送之' means to send them away.
    • 于水边若树下 - By the water or under a tree, where '若' means or, indicating two possible locations.
    • 山龙许诺 - Shan Long agreed, where '许诺' means to agree or promise.
    • 辞吏 - He bid farewell to the official, where '辞' means to bid farewell.
    • 归家 - He returned home, where '归家' means to return home.
    • 正哭经营殡具 - He was crying and preparing funeral items, where '经营' means to prepare or arrange, and '殡具' refers to funeral items.
    • 山龙入至尸傍，即苏 - Shan Long entered and approached the body, and immediately revived, where '苏' means to revive or come back to life.
    • 后曰剪纸作钱帛，并酒食 - Later, he said to cut paper into money and silk, and prepare wine and food, where '剪纸作钱帛' means to cut paper into paper money and silk, and '并酒食' means to prepare wine and food.
    • 自送于水边烧之 - He personally took them to the water's edge and burned them, where '烧之' means to burn them.
    • 忽见三人来谢曰 - Suddenly, he saw the three come and thank him, where '忽见' means suddenly saw, and '来谢' means to come and thank.
    • 蒙君不失信，重相赠遗 - Thank you for not breaking your promise, and for generously giving us more, where '蒙君' means to be favored by you, '不失信' means not breaking your promise, and '重相赠遗' means to generously give more.
    • 愧荷 - We are grateful and ashamed, where '愧荷' conveys a sense of gratitude and humility.
    • 言毕不见 - After they finished speaking, they disappeared, where '言毕' means after they finished speaking, and '不见' means they disappeared.
    • 山龙自向总持寺主僧说之 - Shan Long told the chief monk of Zongchi Temple, where '总持寺主僧' refers to the chief monk of Zongchi Temple.
    • 转向临说之 - He then turned to Lin and told him, where '转向' means to turn to, and '临' is a proper name.
    • 云尔 - He said so, where '云尔' is a phrase used to indicate that something was said.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 山龙 - A proper name, likely referring to a person named Shan Long.
    • 王 - Refers to the king or ruler, indicating a historical or fictional context.
    • 吏 - Refers to an official or bureaucrat, indicating a governmental or administrative setting.
    • 绳主、棒主、袋主 - These terms refer to specific roles in ancient Chinese society, possibly in the context of punishment or detention.
    • 剪纸作钱帛 - Refers to the practice of cutting paper into shapes resembling money and silk, a common folk custom in Chinese culture.
    • 总持寺 - Refers to Zongchi Temple, a specific temple in Chinese history or folklore.
    • 临 - A proper name, likely referring to a person named Lin.

    Proper Nouns

    • 山龙 - A proper name, likely referring to a person named Shan Long.
    • 王 - Refers to the king or ruler.
    • 吏 - Refers to an official or bureaucrat.
    • 总持寺 - Refers to Zongchi Temple.
    • 临 - A proper name, likely referring to a person named Lin.

    Three people approached Shan Long and said, "The king is letting you go. Can you not ask for a little more to send us?" Shan Long remained silent. The official then told Shan Long, "The king is letting you go, not through you." The three people said, "These are those who previously captured you and were your handlers. One is the rope master, who should have tied you with a red rope; one is the stick master, who hit your head; one is the bag master, who sucked your breath. Seeing that you have returned, they are asking for something in return." Shan Long was terrified and thanked the three people, saying, "I am foolish and do not recognize you. Please come to my home to prepare things, but I don't know where to send them." The three people replied, "By the water or under a tree." Shan Long agreed, bid farewell to the official, and returned home. Upon arriving, he found himself crying and preparing funeral items. Shan Long entered and approached the body, and immediately revived. Later, he said, "Cut paper into money and silk, and prepare wine and food. I will personally take them to the water's edge and burn them." Suddenly, he saw the three people return and thank him, saying, "Thank you for not breaking your promise and for generously giving us more. We are grateful and ashamed." After they finished speaking, they disappeared. Shan Long then told the chief monk of Zongchi Temple and turned to Lin, recounting the story. [52]

    冥报记卷下
    　　隋开皇末。代州人。姓王。任为骑骠将军。在荆州镇守。性好田猎。所杀数。有五男无女。后生一女。端美如画。见者皆爱奇之。父母钟念。既还乡里。里人亲族。争为作好衣服。而共爱之。女年七岁。一旦失不知所在。初疑邻里戏藏。访问终无见者。诸兄乘马远觅。乃去家三十余里。棘中见之。欲就挽取。即惊走远去。马追不及。兄等以十骑。围而得之。口中作声。似兔鸣。抱归家。不能言。而身体为棘刺所伤。母为挑之。得刺盈掬月余曰。不食而死。父母悲痛。合家斋戒练行大理丞釆宣明尝为代府法曹为临说云尔。 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 冥報記卷下 - Title of the text, '冥報記' means 'Records of Rebirth' or 'Records of Karma,' and '卷下' indicates it is the second part or volume.
    • 隋開皇末 - Refers to the late Sui Dynasty during the Kaihuang era, a specific historical period.
    • 任為騎驍將軍 - Means 'appointed as a cavalry general,' with '騎驍' referring to a type of cavalry officer.
    • 性好田獵 - Means 'had a fondness for hunting,' where '田獵' is a classical term for hunting.
    • 所殺數 - Means 'killed many,' where '數' implies a large number.
    • 五男無女 - Means 'five sons and no daughters,' a common phrase in classical Chinese to describe family structure.
    • 端美如畫 - Means 'beautiful and graceful like a painting,' a poetic way to describe physical beauty.
    • 見者皆爱奇之 - Means 'those who saw her all found her to be extraordinary,' where '奇' means 'extraordinary' or 'unique.'
    • 父母鐘念 - Means 'parents cherished and loved her deeply,' where '鐘念' is a term for deep affection.
    • 既還鄉里 - Means 'after returning to his hometown,' where '鄉里' refers to one's hometown or native place.
    • 里人親族 - Means 'villagers and relatives,' where '里人' refers to villagers or people from the same neighborhood.
    • 爭為作好衣服 - Means 'competed to make her good clothes,' where '爭' means 'competed' or 'strived.'
    • 女年七歲 - Means 'the girl was seven years old.'
    • 一旦失不知所在 - Means 'one day she disappeared and no one knew where she was,' where '一旦' means 'one day' or 'suddenly.'
    • 初疑邻里戲藏 - Means 'at first they suspected the neighbors were hiding her for fun,' where '戲藏' means 'hiding for fun.'
    • 訪問終無見者 - Means 'after searching, no one saw her,' where '訪問' means 'searching' or 'inquiring.'
    • 諸兄乘馬遠觅 - Means 'her brothers rode horses to search far away,' where '遠觅' means 'searched far away.'
    • 乃去家三十余里 - Means 'they went more than thirty miles away from home,' where '三十余里' is a specific distance.
    • 棘中見之 - Means 'they saw her in the thorns,' where '棘' refers to thorny bushes.
    • 欲就挽取 - Means 'they tried to approach and take her,' where '就挽' means 'approach and take.'
    • 即驚走遠去 - Means 'she immediately ran away in fear,' where '驚走' means 'ran away in fear.'
    • 馬追不及 - Means 'the horse could not catch up,' where '追不及' means 'could not catch up.'
    • 兄等以十騎 - Means 'her brothers with ten riders,' where '十騎' means 'ten riders' or 'ten horsemen.'
    • 圍而得之 - Means 'they surrounded and captured her,' where '圍' means 'surrounded.'
    • 口中作聲，似兔鳴 - Means 'she made a sound like a rabbit,' where '兔鳴' means 'rabbit's cry.'
    • 抱歸家 - Means 'they carried her back home,' where '抱' means 'carry' or 'embrace.'
    • 不能言 - Means 'she could not speak,' where '不能言' means 'could not speak.'
    • 而身體為棘刺所傷 - Means 'but her body was injured by thorn pricks,' where '棘刺' means 'thorn pricks.'
    • 母為挑之 - Means 'her mother picked out the thorns,' where '挑' means 'pick out' or 'remove.'
    • 得刺盈掬 - Means 'she removed many thorns,' where '盈掬' means 'many' or 'a full handful.'
    • 月余曰 - Means 'after a month or so,' where '月余' means 'a month or so.'
    • 不食而死 - Means 'she died without eating,' where '不食' means 'without eating.'
    • 父母悲痛 - Means 'her parents were heartbroken,' where '悲痛' means 'heartbroken.'
    • 合家齋戒 - Means 'the whole family fasted and prayed,' where '齋戒' means 'fasting and praying.'
    • 大理丞釆宣明 - Refers to a specific person, '大理丞' is a title for a high-ranking official, and '釆宣明' is his name.
    • 嘗為代府法曹 - Means 'he once served as a legal official in the Dai State,' where '代府' refers to the Dai State.
    • 為臨說云爾 - Means 'he told me this,' where '為臨說' means 'told me.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋開皇末 - The late Sui Dynasty during the Kaihuang era, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 騎驍將軍 - A type of cavalry general, reflecting the military structure of the time.
    • 田獵 - Hunting, a common pastime and activity for nobility and officials in ancient China.
    • 五男無女 - A common family structure in classical Chinese literature, often used to describe family dynamics.
    • 端美如畫 - A poetic way to describe physical beauty, reflecting the aesthetic values of the time.
    • 父母鐘念 - Deep parental affection, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 里人親族 - Villagers and relatives, reflecting the social structure of the time.
    • 爭為作好衣服 - Competing to make good clothes, reflecting the social status and customs of the time.
    • 棘中見之 - Seeing someone in thorns, a metaphor for a difficult or dangerous situation.
    • 口中作聲，似兔鳴 - Making a sound like a rabbit, a metaphor for fear or distress.
    • 不食而死 - Dying without eating, a tragic event that reflects the fragility of life.
    • 合家齋戒 - Fasting and praying, a common practice in times of grief or to seek divine intervention.
    • 大理丞釆宣明 - A specific historical figure, '大理丞' is a title for a high-ranking official, and '釆宣明' is his name.
    • 嘗為代府法曹 - Serving as a legal official in the Dai State, reflecting the administrative structure of the time.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The opening sentence introduces the main character and his position, with '隋開皇末' setting the historical context.
    • This clause describes the character's personality and actions, with '性好田獵' indicating a fondness for hunting.
    • This clause describes the family structure, with '五男無女' indicating five sons and no daughters.
    • This clause introduces the daughter, with '端美如畫' describing her beauty.
    • This clause describes the reactions to the daughter, with '見者皆爱奇之' indicating that everyone found her extraordinary and '父母鐘念' indicating the parents' deep affection.
    • This clause describes the daughter's return to her hometown and the reactions of the villagers and relatives, with '爭為作好衣服' indicating that they competed to make her good clothes.
    • This clause describes the daughter's disappearance, with '一旦失不知所在' indicating that she disappeared and no one knew where she was.
    • This clause describes the initial suspicion and subsequent search, with '初疑邻里戲藏' indicating that they initially suspected the neighbors were hiding her for fun and '訪問終無見者' indicating that no one saw her after searching.
    • This clause describes the brothers' search, with '諸兄乘馬遠觅' indicating that they rode horses to search far away and '乃去家三十余里' indicating that they went more than thirty miles away from home.
    • This clause describes the encounter with the daughter, with '棘中見之' indicating that they saw her in the thorns, '欲就挽取' indicating that they tried to approach and take her, and '即驚走遠去' indicating that she immediately ran away in fear.
    • This clause describes the capture of the daughter, with '馬追不及' indicating that the horse could not catch up, '兄等以十騎' indicating that her brothers with ten riders, and '圍而得之' indicating that they surrounded and captured her.

    Records of Rebirth, Volume 2

    During the late Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, there was a man from Dai State, surnamed Wang, who was appointed as a cavalry general and stationed in Jingzhou to guard the region. He had a fondness for hunting and killed many animals. He had five sons and no daughters. Later, he had a daughter who was as beautiful and graceful as a painting. Those who saw her all found her to be extraordinary, and her parents cherished and loved her deeply. After returning to his hometown, the villagers and relatives competed to make her good clothes and shared in her beauty. The girl was seven years old when one day she disappeared and no one knew where she was. At first, they suspected the neighbors were hiding her for fun, but after searching, no one saw her. Her brothers rode horses to search far away, going more than thirty miles from home. They saw her in the thorns and tried to approach and take her, but she immediately ran away in fear. The horse could not catch up. Her brothers, with ten riders, surrounded and captured her. She made a sound like a rabbit and was carried back home. She could not speak, but her body was injured by thorn pricks. Her mother picked out the thorns, removing many thorns. After a month or so, she died without eating. Her parents were heartbroken and the whole family fasted and prayed. The high-ranking official Cai Xuanming, who once served as a legal official in the Dai State, told me this. [53]

    后魏司徒崔浩。博学有才略。事太武。言行计从。国人以为模楷。浩师事道士冠谦之。尤不信佛。常虚诞为百姓所费。见其妻读经。夺而投于井中。从太武。至长安。入寺。见有弓矢刀稻。帝怒诛寺僧。浩因进说。尽杀沙门。焚经像。敕留台下。四方依长安行事。冠诵之与浩争。浩不从。谦之谓浩曰。卿从今受戮。灭门户矣。后四年。浩果无罪而族诛。将刑载于露车。官使十人。在车上。更尿其口。行数里。不堪困苦。号叫求哀。竟备五刑。自古戮辱未之前有。帝亦枉诛太子。又寻为阉人宗爱所杀。时人以为毁佛法之报验（见后魏书。及十云因春秋）。 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 司徒 - High-ranking official position in the imperial government; translated as 'Grand Tutor' or 'Minister of Works.'
    • 博學有才略 - Means 'broadly learned and possessing strategic insight.'
    • 言行計從 - Means 'his words and actions were followed without question.'
    • 國人以為模楷 - Means 'the people regarded him as a model.'
    • 師事 - Means 'to serve as a student of' or 'to learn from.'
    • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or scholar.
    • 冠謙之 - The name of a Taoist priest.
    • 不信佛 - Means 'did not believe in Buddhism.'
    • 虛誕 - Means 'falsehoods' or 'deception.'
    • 百姓所費 - Means 'at the expense of the common people.'
    • 奪而投於井中 - Means 'took it away and threw it into the well.'
    • 入寺 - Means 'entered the temple.'
    • 弓矢刀稻 - Refers to weapons (bows, arrows, swords, and spears) found in the temple.
    • 帝怒 - Means 'the emperor was angry.'
    • 盡殺沙門 - Means 'killed all the monks.'
    • 焚經像 - Means 'burned the scriptures and images.'
    • 敕留台下 - Means 'ordered the command center to issue an edict.'
    • 四方依長安行事 - Means 'all regions followed the example of Chang'an.'
    • 與浩爭 - Means 'argued with Cui Hao.'
    • 浩不從 - Means 'Cui Hao did not follow his advice.'
    • 浩果無罪而族诛 - Means 'Cui Hao was indeed executed by the entire family despite being innocent.'
    • 載於露車 - Means 'put on a cart.'
    • 官使十人 - Means 'ten officials.'
    • 更尿其口 - Means 'urinated in his mouth.'
    • 號叫求哀 - Means 'cried out in distress.'
    • 竟備五刑 - Means 'finally endured all five punishments.'
    • 自古戮辱未之前有 - Means 'no such humiliation and punishment had been seen before in ancient times.'
    • 枉诛太子 - Means 'wrongly executed the crown prince.'
    • 尋為阉人宗愛所杀 - Means 'was soon killed by the eunuch Zong Ai.'
    • 毁佛法之报验 - Means 'the retribution for destroying Buddhism.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后魏 - Refers to the Northern Wei dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 司徒崔浩 - Cui Hao, a prominent official during the Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 太武 - Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei, a powerful ruler known for his military campaigns and religious policies.
    • 长安 - The capital city of the Northern Wei dynasty, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 经像 - Refers to Buddhist scriptures and statues.
    • 留台 - Refers to the central government or command center.
    • 宗爱 - A eunuch who played a significant role in the Northern Wei court.
    • 毁佛法之报验 - Refers to the belief that destroying Buddhism brought about negative consequences.

    Proper Nouns

    • 后魏 - Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 司徒崔浩 - Cui Hao.
    • 太武 - Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei.
    • 长安 - Chang'an.
    • 冠谦之 - Taoist priest Guan Qianzhi.
    • 宗爱 - Eunuch Zong Ai.

    Cui Hao, the Grand Tutor of the Northern Wei, was broadly learned and possessed strategic insight. He served Emperor Taiwu, and his words and actions were followed without question. The people regarded him as a model. Cui Hao served under the Taoist priest Guan Qianzhi, who particularly did not believe in Buddhism. He often spread falsehoods at the expense of the common people. Once, he saw his wife reading scriptures and took it away, throwing it into a well. Accompanying Emperor Taiwu to Chang'an, he entered a temple and found weapons (bows, arrows, swords, and spears) inside. The emperor was furious and ordered the execution of the monks. Cui Hao then advised, leading to the killing of all the monks, the burning of the scriptures and images, and the issuance of an edict from the command center. All regions followed the example of Chang'an. Guan Qianzhi argued with Cui Hao, but Cui Hao did not follow his advice. Guan Qianzhi told Cui Hao, "You will be punished from now on, and your family will be destroyed." Four years later, Cui Hao was indeed executed by the entire family despite being innocent. Before his execution, he was put on a cart, with ten officials present. They urinated in his mouth, and after traveling several miles, he could no longer bear the suffering and cried out in distress. He finally endured all five punishments. No such humiliation and punishment had been seen before in ancient times. The emperor also wrongly executed the crown prince. Soon after, Cui Hao was killed by the eunuch Zong Ai. At the time, people believed this was the retribution for destroying Buddhism. (See the History of the Northern Wei and Ten Clouds Because of Spring and Autumn Annals.) [54]

    梁元帝。年六岁。见母庄匣中有大珠。取一珠口含。遂误吞之。其珠意左右盗。讯问莫服。乃灰生鱼自以诅之。明曰帝大使。珠随使而出。帝一目遂眇（见梁后略说之）。
    　　周武帝。好食鸡卵。一食数枚。有监膳仪同名拔彪。常进御食有宠。隋文帝即位。犹监膳进食。开皇中。暴死。而心尚暖。家人不忍殡之。三曰乃苏。能语。先云。举我见王。为周武帝传语。既而请见。文帝引问。言曰。始忽见人唤。随至一处。有大地穴。所行之道。径入穴中。才到穴口。遥见西方有百余骑来。仪卫如王者。俄至穴口。乃周武帝也。仪同拜。帝曰。王唤汝。证我事了。汝身无所罪。言讫即入穴中。使者亦引仪同入。使宫门。引入庭。见武帝共一人同坐。而有加敬之客。使者令仪同拜王。王问。汝为帝作食。前后进白团几枚。仪同不识白团。 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 年六岁 - Literal meaning 'six years old,' used to specify the age of the subject.
    • 见母庄匣中有大珠 - Literal description meaning 'saw his mother's box containing a large pearl.'
    • 取一珠口含，遂误吞之 - A sequence of actions: 'took one pearl and put it in his mouth, then accidentally swallowed it.'
    • 其珠意左右盗 - A vague statement meaning 'the pearl was suspected to have been stolen by someone nearby.'
    • 讯问莫服 - Means 'no one admitted to it after questioning.'
    • 乃灰生鱼自以诅之 - A ritual act: 'he then used ashes and live fish to curse the pearl.'
    • 明曰帝大使 - A phrase that is ambiguous but likely means 'the next day, the emperor sent an envoy.'
    • 珠随使而出 - Means 'the pearl followed the envoy out.'
    • 帝一目遂眇 - Means 'the emperor's one eye became blind.'
    • 好食鸡卵 - A cultural reference to the dietary preferences of the time.
    • 一食数枚 - Indicates the quantity of eggs consumed at one sitting.
    • 暴死 - Means 'died suddenly.'
    • 心尚暖 - Means 'the heart was still warm,' indicating recent death.
    • 家人不忍殡之 - Means 'the family was unwilling to bury him.'
    • 三曰乃苏 - Means 'he revived after three days.'
    • 举我见王 - Means 'raise me to see the king.'
    • 证我事了 - Means 'to prove my case.'
    • 使者引仪同入 - Means 'the messenger led Babi into the presence of the king.'
    • 使宫门 - Means 'through the palace gates.'
    • 引入庭 - Means 'into the courtyard.'
    • 加敬之客 - Refers to a guest who is treated with extra respect.
    • 白团 - Refers to a type of food, possibly a white cake or dumpling.
    • 不识白团 - Means 'did not recognize what white团 was.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁元帝 - Refers to Emperor Yuan of Liang, a ruler of the Southern Dynasties period.
    • 周武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Zhou, a ruler of the Northern Zhou dynasty.
    • 隋文帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Sui.
    • 拔彪 - Babi, a person who served as a supervisor of the imperial kitchen.

    Emperor Yuan of Liang was six years old when he saw a large pearl in his mother's box. He took one pearl and put it in his mouth, then accidentally swallowed it. The pearl was suspected to have been stolen by someone nearby. After questioning, no one admitted to it. He then used ashes and live fish to curse the pearl. The next day, the emperor sent an envoy, and the pearl followed the envoy out. The emperor's one eye became blind (see the later account of Liang).

    Emperor Wu of Zhou enjoyed eating chicken eggs and would eat several at one sitting. There was a supervisor of the imperial kitchen named Babi, who often served the emperor and was favored. When Emperor Wen of Sui ascended the throne, Babi continued to serve the imperial kitchen. During the Kaihuang era, Babi died suddenly, but his heart was still warm. His family was unwilling to bury him. After three days, he revived and could speak. He first said, "Raise me to see the king." He was to convey a message for Emperor Wu of Zhou. Then he requested to see the king. Emperor Wen of Sui led him to ask, and Babi said, "Suddenly, I saw someone calling me. I followed them to a place. There was a large underground cave. We walked along the path and entered the cave. As soon as we reached the entrance of the cave, I saw over a hundred riders coming from the west. They were guarded like a king. Soon they arrived at the cave entrance. It was Emperor Wu of Zhou. Babi bowed. The king said, 'The king called you. To prove my case. You have no guilt.' After saying this, he entered the cave. The messenger also led Babi into the palace through the palace gates and into the courtyard. He saw Emperor Wu of Zhou sitting with another person, and there was a guest treated with extra respect. The messenger ordered Babi to bow to the king. The king asked, 'How many white团 did you serve the emperor?' Babi did not recognize what white团 was." [55]

    顾左右。左右教曰。名鸡卵为白团也。仪同即答。帝常食白团。实不说数。王谓帝曰。此人不记。当须出之。帝惨然不悦而起。急见庭前。有一铁床。并狱卒数十人。皆牛头人身。帝已卧床上。狱卒用铁梁押之。帝胁割裂。裂处。鸡子全出。俄与床齐。可十余斛。乃命数之讫。床及狱急皆不见。帝又已在王坐。王谓仪同还去。有人引出至穴口中。又见武帝出来。语仪同云。为闻大隋天子。昔曾与我共事。仓库玉帛。亦我储之。我今身为自帝为灭佛法。极受大苦。可为吾作功德也。于是。文帝敕天下人。出一钱。为追福焉（临外祖齐公亲见时归家。具说云尔）。 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 顧左右 - A phrase meaning 'to look around' or 'to glance at one's surroundings,' often used to indicate hesitation or uncertainty.
    • 教曰 - Means 'to teach' or 'to instruct,' here used in the sense of someone explaining or clarifying.
    • 名雞卵為白團也 - Literally 'to name chicken eggs as white balls,' which is likely a euphemism or a play on words, possibly indicating a misunderstanding or a symbolic representation.
    • 實不说数 - Literally 'in fact, not saying numbers,' which could mean that the emperor did not want to discuss the matter in detail or that he was uncomfortable with the number.
    • 此人不記 - Means 'this person does not remember,' but the context suggests that the emperor is being told that the person (likely the translator) does not recall the event.
    • 當須出之 - Means 'must be expelled' or 'must be removed,' indicating that the emperor is being advised to dismiss the person.
    • 慘然不悅而起 - Means 'sadly and unwillingly got up,' indicating the emperor's emotional state.
    • 鐵床 - Refers to an iron bed, which is likely a metaphorical or symbolic object.
    • 牛頭人身 - Refers to beings with the head of a cow and the body of a human, which is a common motif in Chinese mythology, often associated with hell or punishment.
    • 脅割裂 - Means 'cutting the ribs open,' which is a vivid and graphic description of the emperor's suffering.
    • 雞子全出 - Means 'chicken eggs all came out,' which is a metaphorical or symbolic description of the emperor's suffering manifesting in a physical form.
    • 可十余斛 - Means 'about ten dou' (a unit of measurement), indicating the quantity of the eggs.
    • 乃命数之讫 - Means 'then counted them all,' indicating the completion of the counting process.
    • 床及獄急皆不見 - Means 'the bed and the prison disappeared,' indicating a sudden and miraculous change in the scene.
    • 王謂儀同還去 - Means 'the king said the translator should return,' indicating the end of the encounter.
    • 有人引出至穴口中 - Means 'someone led out to the mouth of the cave,' indicating the translator's departure.
    • 武帝出來 - Means 'Emperor Wu came out,' indicating the appearance of a significant figure.
    • 昔曾與我共事 - Means 'once worked together with me,' indicating a past relationship or shared history.
    • 倉庫玉帛 - Refers to treasures and valuables stored in the warehouse, indicating wealth and resources.
    • 身為自帝為滅佛法 - Means 'I myself as an emperor destroyed Buddhism,' indicating a significant historical event or personal action.
    • 極受大苦 - Means 'suffered great suffering,' indicating the consequences of the emperor's actions.
    • 可為吾作功德也 - Means 'you can do good deeds for me,' indicating a request for religious or moral actions.
    • 文帝敕天下人 - Means 'Emperor Wen issued an edict to the people of the world,' indicating a formal decree.
    • 出一錢 - Means 'pay one coin,' indicating a small but significant contribution.
    • 為追福焉 - Means 'to seek blessings,' indicating the purpose of the contribution.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 顧左右 - A common phrase in classical Chinese literature, often used to indicate hesitation or uncertainty.
    • 名雞卵為白團也 - This phrase is likely a play on words or a euphemism, possibly indicating a symbolic or metaphorical meaning.
    • 實不说数 - This phrase suggests that the emperor was uncomfortable with discussing the matter in detail, possibly due to its sensitive nature.
    • 此人不記 - This phrase indicates that the translator (likely the person being addressed) does not recall the event, which is a common narrative device in classical Chinese literature.
    • 當須出之 - This phrase suggests that the emperor is being advised to dismiss the person, possibly due to their inability to remember the event.
    • 鐵床 - This object is likely a metaphorical or symbolic representation of suffering or punishment.
    • 牛頭人身 - This motif is common in Chinese mythology, often associated with hell or punishment.
    • 脅割裂 - This vivid and graphic description is likely intended to convey the severity of the emperor's suffering.
    • 雞子全出 - This metaphorical or symbolic description is likely intended to convey the manifestation of the emperor's suffering in a physical form.
    • 可十余斛 - This unit of measurement is used to indicate the quantity of the eggs, which is likely a symbolic or metaphorical representation.
    • 床及獄急皆不見 - This sudden and miraculous change in the scene is likely intended to convey a supernatural or divine intervention.
    • 王謂儀同還去 - This phrase indicates the end of the encounter, possibly suggesting that the translator has been dismissed or that the encounter has concluded.
    • 有人引出至穴口中 - This phrase suggests that the translator is being led out of a dangerous or supernatural place.
    • 武帝出來 - This phrase indicates the appearance of a significant figure, likely a historical or mythological figure.
    • 昔曾與我共事 - This phrase suggests a past relationship or shared history, possibly indicating a connection between the emperor and the person being addressed.
    • 倉庫玉帛 - This phrase refers to treasures and valuables, indicating wealth and resources.
    • 身為自帝為滅佛法 - This phrase suggests that the emperor destroyed Buddhism, possibly indicating a significant historical event or personal action.
    • 極受大苦 - This phrase indicates the consequences of the emperor's actions, possibly suggesting that he is being punished for his deeds.
    • 可為吾作功德也 - This phrase suggests a request for religious or moral actions, possibly indicating a plea for redemption or forgiveness.
    • 文帝敕天下人 - This phrase indicates a formal decree issued by Emperor Wen, possibly suggesting that he is taking action to address the consequences of the emperor's actions.
    • 出一錢 - This phrase suggests a small but significant contribution, possibly indicating a symbolic or ritualistic act.
    • 為追福焉 - This phrase indicates the purpose of the contribution, possibly suggesting that it is intended to seek blessings or good fortune.

    Proper Nouns

    • 顧左右 - A common phrase in classical Chinese literature, often used to indicate hesitation or uncertainty.
    • 名雞卵為白團也 - This phrase is likely a play on words or a euphemism, possibly indicating a symbolic or metaphorical meaning.
    • 實不说数 - This phrase suggests that the emperor was uncomfortable with discussing the matter in detail, possibly due to its sensitive nature.
    • 此人不記 - This phrase indicates that the translator (likely the person being addressed) does not recall the event, which is a common narrative device in classical Chinese literature.
    • 當須出之 - This phrase suggests that the emperor is being advised to dismiss the person, possibly due to their inability to remember the event.
    • 鐵床 - This object is likely a metaphorical or symbolic representation of suffering or punishment.
    • 牛頭人身 - This motif is common in Chinese mythology, often associated with hell or punishment.
    • 脅割裂 - This vivid and graphic description is likely intended to convey the severity of the emperor's suffering.
    • 雞子全出 - This metaphorical or symbolic description is likely intended to convey the manifestation of the emperor's suffering in a physical form.
    • 可十余斛 - This unit of measurement is used to indicate the quantity of the eggs, which is likely a symbolic or metaphorical representation.
    • 床及獄急皆不見 - This sudden and miraculous change in the scene is likely intended to convey a supernatural or divine intervention.
    • 王謂儀同還去 - This phrase indicates the end of the encounter, possibly suggesting that the translator has been dismissed or that the encounter has concluded.
    • 有人引出至穴口中 - This phrase suggests that the translator is being led out of a dangerous or supernatural place.
    • 武帝出來 - This phrase indicates the appearance of a significant figure, likely a historical or mythological figure.
    • 昔曾與我共事 - This phrase suggests a past relationship or shared history, possibly indicating a connection between the emperor and the person being addressed.
    • 倉庫玉帛 - This phrase refers to treasures and valuables, indicating wealth and resources.
    • 身為自帝為滅佛法 - This phrase suggests that the emperor destroyed Buddhism, possibly indicating a significant historical event or personal action.
    • 極受大苦 - This phrase indicates the consequences of the emperor's actions, possibly suggesting that he is being punished for his deeds.
    • 可為吾作功德也 - This phrase suggests a request for religious or moral actions, possibly indicating a plea for redemption or forgiveness.
    • 文帝敕天下人 - This phrase indicates a formal decree issued by Emperor Wen, possibly suggesting that he is taking action to address the consequences of the emperor's actions.
    • 出一錢 - This phrase suggests a small but significant contribution, possibly indicating a symbolic or ritualistic act.
    • 為追福焉 - This phrase indicates the purpose of the contribution, possibly suggesting that it is intended to seek blessings or good fortune.

    The emperor looked around. Those nearby explained, "Chicken eggs are called 'white balls.'" The translator immediately replied, "The emperor often eats white balls, but he really doesn't like to count them." The king said to the emperor, "This person doesn't remember. They must be dismissed." The emperor looked sad and unwillingly got up. He quickly saw a courtyard before him, with an iron bed and dozens of prison guards, all with the heads of cows and the bodies of humans. The emperor was already lying on the bed, and the prison guards used iron beams to bind him. The emperor's ribs were cut open, and from the cuts, chicken eggs all came out. Soon, they filled up the bed, about ten dou. Then, they counted them all, and the bed and the prison disappeared. The emperor was again sitting with the king. The king told the translator to return. Someone led him out to the mouth of a cave. They also saw Emperor Wu coming out. He told the translator, "I heard that the Great Sui Emperor once worked with me. The treasures and valuables in the warehouse were also stored by me. Now, as an emperor, I destroyed Buddhism and suffered great suffering. You can do good deeds for me." Therefore, Emperor Wen issued an edict to the people of the world, asking them to contribute one coin each to seek blessings (my maternal grandfather, Prince Qi, personally witnessed this and told me upon returning home). [56]

    北齐时。有仕人姓梁。甚豪富。将死。谓其妻子曰。吾平生爱奴及马。皆使乘曰久。称人意。吾死。可以为殉。不然。无所使乘也。及其死。家人以囊盛土。压奴杀之。马则未杀。奴死四曰而苏。说云。当不觉去。急至官府门。门人因留止。在门所经一宿。明旦。见其主被锁。严兵守卫。将入官所。见奴谓曰。我死。人得奴婢。遗言唤汝。今各自受苦。名全不相关。今当自官放汝。言毕而入。奴从屏外窥之。见官问守卫人曰。昨曰押脂多少乎。对曰。得八升。官曰。更将去。 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 吾平生爱奴及马 - I have loved my servants and horses all my life.
    • 吾死，可以为殉 - After I die, they can be sacrificed to accompany me.
    • 吾死，人得奴婢，遗言唤汝 - After I died, the people obtained the slaves, and my last words were to call you.
    • 今各自受苦，名全不相关 - Now we are each suffering, but our names are completely unrelated.
    • 今当自官放汝 - Now the official will release you.
    • 当不觉去 - I didn't feel myself being taken away.
    • 急至官府门，门人因留止 - I hurried to the gate of the official residence, and the gatekeeper detained me.
    • 见其主被锁，严兵守卫 - I saw my master being bound and guarded by armed soldiers.
    • 见官问守卫人曰，昨曰押脂多少乎 - He saw the official ask the guard, 'How much oil was pressed yesterday?'
    • 对曰，得八升 - The guard replied, 'Eight sheng were obtained.'
    • 官曰，更将去 - The official said, 'Take it away again.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北齐 - The Northern Qi Dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history known for its political and cultural developments.
    • 仕人 - A scholar-official, a person who has passed the imperial examinations and holds a government position.
    • 殉葬 - The practice of burying servants or objects with the deceased, a common practice in ancient China.
    • 官府 - The official residence or government office.
    • 官 - An official or magistrate.
    • 守卫人 - A guard or watchman.
    • 屏 - A screen or partition, often used to separate spaces in traditional Chinese architecture.
    • 押脂 - A term referring to the collection of oil or other substances, possibly as a form of tribute or tax.
    • 八升 - A unit of volume in ancient China, equivalent to about 1.2 liters.

    Proper Nouns

    • 北齐 - The Northern Qi Dynasty.
    • 官府 - The official residence or government office.
    • 官 - An official or magistrate.
    • 守卫人 - A guard or watchman.
    • 屏 - A screen or partition.
    • 押脂 - A term referring to the collection of oil or other substances, possibly as a form of tribute or tax.
    • 八升 - A unit of volume in ancient China.

    During the Northern Qi Dynasty, there was a scholar-official surnamed Liang, who was very wealthy and prosperous. As he lay on his deathbed, he told his wife and children, "Throughout my life, I have loved my servants and horses. They have all been used for a long time, to my satisfaction. After I die, they can be sacrificed to accompany me. Otherwise, there will be no one to ride them." When he died, his family stuffed the servant with earth and crushed him to death. The horse was not killed. The servant died and then revived after four days. He said, "I didn't feel myself being taken away. I hurried to the gate of the official residence. The gatekeeper detained me. I spent a night at the gate. The next morning, I saw my master being bound and guarded by armed soldiers. As he was being taken into the official residence, he saw the servant and said to him, 'After I died, the people obtained the slaves, and my last words were to call you. Now we are each suffering, but our names are completely unrelated. Now the official will release you.' After he finished speaking, he went in. The servant peered from behind the screen and saw the official ask the guard, 'How much oil was collected yesterday?' The guard replied, 'Eight sheng were obtained.' The official said, 'Take it away again.' [57]

    押取一斛六斗。主即被牵出。竟不得言。明曰又来。有喜色。谓奴曰。今为汝白也。及入。官问得脂乎。对曰不得。官问所以。主司曰。此人死三曰。家人为请僧设斋。每闻经呗声。铁梁辄折。故不得也。官曰。且将去主。因白官。请放奴。官即唤放。俱出门。主遣传语其妻子曰。赖汝等追福。得免大苦。然犹未脱。能更写法华经。造像。以相救济。冀固得免。自今无设祭。既而益吾罪。言毕而别。奴遂生而具言之。家中果以其曰设会。于是倾家追福。令门练行（临舅高经州说云见齐人说之灾）。
    　　隋上柱国蒲山惠公李宽。性好田猎。常养鹰数十。后生一男。口为鹰嘴。遂不举之（公即李蜜之父临家与亲。并悉见之）。 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 押取一斛六斗 - A specific quantity of grain or goods, likely referring to a tax or payment.
    • 竟不得言 - Literally 'finally could not speak,' implying the person was unable to express themselves.
    • 有喜色 - Means 'with a joyful expression,' indicating a positive change in mood.
    • 此人死三曰 - 'This person died three days ago,' where '曰' is an archaic form of '日' (day).
    • 家人为请僧设斋 - 'The family invited monks to set up a vegetarian meal,' a common Buddhist practice.
    • 每闻经呗声 - 'Whenever they heard the recitation of scriptures,' where '经呗' refers to Buddhist chanting.
    • 铁梁辄折 - 'The iron beam would break,' possibly a metaphor for the power of Buddhist rituals.
    • 请放奴 - 'Request to release the slave,' indicating a plea for freedom.
    • 倾家追福 - 'Spend all their wealth to seek blessings,' a common practice in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 门练行 - 'Door practice,' possibly referring to a specific ritual or practice.
    • 临舅高经州说云见齐人说之灾 - A reference to a specific story or event, possibly from a historical or literary source.
    • 上柱国蒲山惠公李宽 - 'The Duke of Pushe Mountain, Li Kuan, who held the title of Shangzhuguo,' a high-ranking official.
    • 性好田猎 - 'Loves hunting,' indicating a personal interest.
    • 常养鹰数十 - 'Often kept dozens of eagles,' suggesting a passion for falconry.
    • 口为鹰嘴 - 'Had an eagle's beak,' a physical deformity.
    • 遂不举之 - 'So he did not raise it,' indicating a decision not to care for the child.
    • 公即李蜜之父 - 'He was the father of Li Mi,' providing a familial connection.
    • 临家与亲 - 'Neighbors and relatives,' indicating a social network.
    • 并悉见之 - 'All saw it,' emphasizing the widespread knowledge of the event.
    • 无设祭 - 'Do not set up sacrifices,' a religious or cultural practice.
    • 既而益吾罪 - 'Afterwards, I increased my own guilt,' indicating a sense of moral responsibility.
    • 言毕而别 - 'After finishing speaking, they parted,' indicating the end of a conversation.
    • 奴遂生而具言之 - 'The slave was born and told it all,' indicating a detailed account.
    • 家中果以其曰设会 - 'The family indeed held a gathering on that day,' indicating a specific event.
    • 于是倾家追福 - 'So they spent all their wealth to seek blessings,' emphasizing the family's actions.
    • 令门练行 - 'Ordered the door practice,' indicating a specific ritual.
    • 临舅高经州说云见齐人说之灾 - A reference to a specific story or event, possibly from a historical or literary source.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 押取一斛六斗 - Likely refers to a tax or payment system in ancient China.
    • 家人为请僧设斋 - A common Buddhist practice of inviting monks to perform rituals and offer vegetarian meals.
    • 每闻经呗声 - Buddhist chanting, a central practice in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 铁梁辄折 - A metaphor for the power of Buddhist rituals, possibly referring to a specific legend or belief.
    • 请放奴 - The practice of releasing slaves, often as part of religious or moral obligations.
    • 倾家追福 - The practice of spending all wealth to seek blessings, a common in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 门练行 - A specific ritual or practice, possibly related to Buddhist or Daoist traditions.
    • 临舅高经州说云见齐人说之灾 - A reference to a specific story or event, possibly from a historical or literary source.
    • 上柱国蒲山惠公李宽 - A high-ranking official in the Sui dynasty, known for his military and political achievements.
    • 性好田猎 - A personal interest in hunting, common among the elite in ancient China.
    • 常养鹰数十 - Falconry, a popular pastime among the nobility.
    • 口为鹰嘴 - A physical deformity, possibly a birth defect.
    • 遂不举之 - The decision not to raise the child, possibly due to the deformity.
    • 公即李蜜之父 - A familial connection, linking the story to a historical figure.
    • 临家与亲 - Social network, indicating the involvement of neighbors and relatives.
    • 并悉见之 - Widespread knowledge, emphasizing the public nature of the event.
    • 无设祭 - The practice of not setting up sacrifices, possibly a religious or cultural decision.
    • 既而益吾罪 - A sense of moral responsibility, indicating the speaker's guilt.
    • 言毕而别 - The end of a conversation, indicating the conclusion of a specific event.
    • 奴遂生而具言之 - A detailed account, emphasizing the slave's testimony.
    • 家中果以其曰设会 - A specific event, indicating the family's actions.
    • 于是倾家追福 - The family's actions, emphasizing their commitment to seeking blessings.
    • 令门练行 - A specific ritual, possibly related to Buddhist or Daoist traditions.
    • 临舅高经州说云见齐人说之灾 - A reference to a specific story or event, possibly from a historical or literary source.

    Proper Nouns

    • 一斛六斗 - A specific quantity of grain or goods.
    • 上柱国蒲山惠公李宽 - A high-ranking official in the Sui dynasty.
    • 李蜜 - A historical figure, possibly a relative of Li Kuan.
    • 临舅高经州 - A specific person, possibly a historian or storyteller.
    • 齐人 - A person from the state of Qi, possibly a historical figure.
    • 灾 - A disaster or calamity, possibly a specific event.

    A certain amount of one shi and six dou was collected. The owner was immediately led out and could not speak. The next day, when he came again, he had a joyful expression. He said to the slave, "I will speak on your behalf today." When they entered, the official asked if they had obtained the fat. They replied that they had not. The official asked why. The owner said, "This person died three days ago. The family invited monks to set up a vegetarian meal. Whenever they heard the recitation of scriptures, the iron beam would break. Therefore, they could not obtain it." The official said, "For now, let us release the owner. I will also inform the official to release the slave." The official then called for the release, and they all went out. The owner sent a message to his wife and children, saying, "Thanks to you all for seeking blessings, I have been spared great suffering. However, I am still not free. If you can write the Lotus Sutra again, create statues, and offer them to the Buddhas, we may be able to be saved. From now on, do not set up sacrifices." Afterward, he felt that he had increased his own guilt. They parted after finishing speaking. The slave was born and told the story in detail. The family indeed held a gathering on that day and spent all their wealth to seek blessings, ordering the door practice (as told by my uncle Gao Jingzhou, who saw a story about a disaster in the state of Qi).

    During the Sui dynasty, the Duke of Pushe Mountain, Li Kuan, who held the title of Shangzhuguo, loved hunting and often kept dozens of eagles. Later, he had a son who was born with an eagle's beak, so he did not raise him (he was the father of Li Mi, and his neighbors and relatives all saw it). [58]

    隋鹰杨郎将天水女姜略。少田猎。善放鹰。后遇病。见群鸟千数。皆无头。围绕略床。鸣叫曰。急还我头来。略辄头痛气绝。久乃苏田请为诸鸟追福。许之。皆去。既而得愈。遂终身绝酒肉。不杀生命（临在陇右夏见姜也。年六十许自临说云尔）。
    　　隋开皇初。冀州外邑中。有小儿。年十三。常盗邻家鸡卵。烧而食之。后早朝村人未起。其父闻。外有人叩门。呼此儿声。父令儿出应之。见一人云。官唤汝侵。儿曰。唤我役者。入取衣粮。使者曰。不须也。因引儿。出村门。村南旧是桑田。耕讫未下种。是且。此儿忽见。道有一小城。四面门楼。丹素甚丽。儿怪曰。何时有此。使者呵之。使勿言。因引至城北门。令儿前入。儿入度间城门忽闭。不见一人。唯是空城。地皆杀灰碎火。深才没踝。儿忽呼叫。走赴南门。垂至而闭。又走东门北门。亦皆如是。 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少田猎 - Literally 'young and skilled in hunting,' used to describe someone who is young and proficient in hunting.
    • 善放鹰 - Literally 'good at releasing hawks,' used to describe someone who is skilled in falconry.
    • 急还我头来 - Literally 'quickly return my head,' an idiomatic expression used by the birds to express their need for their heads.
    • 略辄头痛气绝 - Literally 'Jiao immediately had a headache and lost consciousness,' describing a sudden and severe reaction.
    • 请为诸鸟追福 - Literally 'asked to perform good deeds for the birds,' referring to the act of doing good deeds to benefit the birds.
    • 遂终身绝酒肉 - Literally 'thereafter never ate meat or drank alcohol,' indicating a lifelong commitment to vegetarianism and abstinence from alcohol.
    • 常盗邻家鸡卵 - Literally 'often stole neighbor's chicken eggs,' describing a habitual act of theft.
    • 烧而食之 - Literally 'roasted and ate them,' describing the method of cooking and consumption.
    • 官唤汝侵 - Literally 'the official calls you in,' a phrase used by the messenger to summon the boy.
    • 入取衣粮 - Literally 'go in and get your clothes and food,' a command given to the boy.
    • 使者曰 - Literally 'the messenger said,' introducing a statement made by the messenger.
    • 因引儿 - Literally 'then led the boy,' describing the action of leading the boy.
    • 出村门 - Literally 'out of the village gate,' describing the location.
    • 旧是桑田 - Literally 'was formerly mulberry fields,' describing the previous state of the land.
    • 耕讫未下种 - Literally 'plowed but not yet sown,' describing the state of the land after plowing.
    • 垂至而闭 - Literally 'almost reached but closed,' describing the situation where the boy almost reached the door but it closed before he could enter.
    • 亦皆如是 - Literally 'also all like this,' indicating that the same thing happened at other doors.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋鹰杨郎将 - Refers to a military rank during the Sui Dynasty, indicating the social status of the person mentioned.
    • 天水女姜略 - Refers to a woman named Jiao Lüo from the region of Tianshui, providing a specific cultural and geographical context.
    • 冀州外邑中 - Refers to a place outside the city of Jizhou, indicating the setting of the story.
    • 隋开皇初 - Refers to the early years of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, providing a historical context.
    • 使者 - Refers to a messenger or official, indicating a figure of authority.
    • 城门 - Refers to the gates of a city, symbolizing a boundary or threshold.
    • 空城 - Refers to an empty city, often used in literature to create a sense of mystery or desolation.
    • 杀灰碎火 - Refers to a landscape covered in ashes and broken coals, indicating a scene of destruction or desolation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 隋鹰杨郎将 - A military rank during the Sui Dynasty.
    • 天水女姜略 - A woman named Jiao Lüo from the region of Tianshui.
    • 冀州 - A region in ancient China.
    • 隋开皇初 - The early years of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty.
    • 使者 - A messenger or official.
    • 城门 - The gates of a city.
    • 空城 - An empty city.
    • 杀灰碎火 - A landscape covered in ashes and broken coals.

    During the Sui Dynasty, a woman named Jiao Lüo, who was a military officer, was skilled in hunting and falconry. Later, she fell ill and saw a group of thousands of birds, all without heads, circling around her bed. They cried out, "Quickly return our heads!" Jiao Lüo immediately suffered from a severe headache and lost consciousness. After a long time, she regained consciousness and prayed to perform good deeds for the birds. She was granted permission, and the birds all left. Soon after, she recovered and decided to abstain from meat and alcohol for the rest of her life, and she also refrained from killing any living beings. (This story was told to me by someone named Lin, who saw Jiao Lüo in Longyou during the summer when he was about sixty years old.)

    In the early years of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, in a village outside Jizhou, there was a thirteen-year-old boy who often stole his neighbor's chicken eggs and roasted them to eat. One morning, before the villagers had risen, his father heard someone knocking on the door and calling out for the boy. His father sent the boy to answer the door and saw a man who said, "The official calls you in." The boy replied, "If it's to summon me for service, let me go inside and get my clothes and food." The messenger said, "No need." He then led the boy out of the village gate. The land south of the village was formerly mulberry fields, and it had been plowed but not yet sown.
